text:
A court order restrains the defendant from transferring certain assets. The defendant deliberately transfers the assets in violation of the order. The plaintiff seeks a finding of contempt. The defendant argues that civil contempt requires proof on a balance of probabilities and that no fine or imprisonment is available.

In the enforcement context, what standard of proof applies to a civil contempt finding, and what sanctions are available?
opts: Civil contempt requires proof beyond a reasonable doubt and the only available sanction is a fine up to $1,000.

Civil contempt arising from disobedience of a court order in civil proceedings must be proved beyond a reasonable doubt, consistent with the quasi-criminal nature of contempt; sanctions available include fines, imprisonment, and sequestration of assets.
Civil contempt must be proved on a balance of probabilities and the only sanction is costs awarded against the contemnor.
Civil contempt cannot result in imprisonment; imprisonment is only available for criminal contempt.
expl: In Ontario, civil contempt arising from the disobedience of a court order in civil proceedings is a quasi-criminal matter because it involves the liberty of the subject. Despite being categorized as 'civil contempt,' the standard of proof is beyond a reasonable doubt given the potential for imprisonment as a sanction. The court may impose a range of sanctions for civil contempt including: fines (which may be substantial); imprisonment (typically until the contemptuous conduct is purged or the order is complied with); sequestration of assets; and costs on a solicitor-client basis. The defendant's assertion that civil contempt cannot result in imprisonment is incorrect; imprisonment is available as a sanction for deliberate disobedience of a court order.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.11; Ontario civil contempt practice
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Civil vs Criminal Contempt in the Enforcement Context

text:
At trial, the plaintiff's witness testifies that a third party told her that the defendant had admitted the accident was his fault. The defendant's counsel objects to this testimony as hearsay. Plaintiff's counsel argues the statement is admissible because it is an admission against interest.

Is the witness's testimony about what the third party said the defendant admitted admissible as a party admission exception to the hearsay rule?
opts: Yes — any out-of-court statement by a party to an action is admissible regardless of the route by which it is introduced.

No — the evidence is inadmissible double hearsay because the witness is reporting what a third party said the defendant said; a party admission must be tendered through a witness who directly heard the party make the admission, not through an intermediary.
Yes — an out-of-court statement by the defendant is an admission against interest and is therefore admissible no matter how many levels of hearsay are involved.
No — party admissions are never admissible at trial in Ontario; they are excluded as self-serving.
expl: The party admission exception to the hearsay rule permits a party's own out-of-court statements to be tendered by the opposing party as evidence of the truth of their contents. However, the statement in this scenario involves two levels of hearsay: (1) the defendant said to someone (the third party) that the accident was his fault; and (2) the third party told the witness what the defendant said. The witness on the stand is reporting the third party's statement, not the defendant's direct statement. This is double hearsay — both the defendant's original statement and the third party's repetition of it must each satisfy a hearsay exception independently. The third party's statement to the witness does not qualify under any obvious exception. The proper way to introduce the defendant's admission would be through a witness who directly heard the defendant make the statement.
caseRef: Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23; Ontario civil trial practice
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Hearsay at Trial — General Rule and Principal Exceptions

text:
A party failed to file a notice of appeal within 30 days of a final order. Forty-five days after the order, the party brings a motion under r.3.02 to extend the time to appeal. The party has a compelling legal argument on the merits of the appeal. The respondent argues that the delay of 15 days beyond the appeal period is too long to justify an extension.

On a motion to extend the time to appeal under r.3.02, is the length of the delay a decisive factor, or does the court consider it alongside other factors?
opts: The length of the delay is the only relevant factor; any delay beyond 30 days from the order will result in refusal of the extension.

On a motion under r.3.02, the court considers the totality of circumstances including: the length of the delay, the reason for the delay, whether the proposed appeal raises a genuine issue of merit, and whether the respondent would suffer prejudice from an extension; no single factor is determinative and the court exercises a broad discretion.
The existence of a meritorious appeal argument is the only relevant factor; if the appeal has merit, an extension will always be granted regardless of the delay.
A motion under r.3.02 to extend time to appeal is only available where the delay is less than 14 days; longer delays are jurisdictionally barred.
expl: Under r.3.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may extend time on such terms as are just. On a motion to extend time to appeal, courts apply a multi-factor discretionary test. The relevant considerations include: (1) the length of the delay — a short delay is less problematic, but even a long delay is not automatically fatal; (2) the reason for the delay — an administrative error or inadvertence is treated more sympathetically than deliberate delay; (3) the merits of the proposed appeal — a frivolous appeal may not justify an extension while a genuinely arguable case supports one; (4) prejudice to the respondent from the extension — if the respondent has relied on the judgment in some material way; and (5) the overall interests of justice. No single factor is determinative; the court exercises a broad discretion to achieve a just result.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.3.02; r.61.04(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Extending Time to Appeal — r.3.02 Factors

text:
On appeal from a trial judgment awarding $300,000 to the plaintiff, the Court of Appeal finds that the trial judge applied the correct legal test but made an error of fact that is palpable and overriding. The Court of Appeal believes that on the correct facts, the plaintiff should have been awarded only $180,000. Counsel for the respondent argues that the Court of Appeal must order a new trial and cannot simply substitute a different damages award.

Under s.134 of the Courts of Justice Act, what powers does the Court of Appeal have on appeal from a judgment, and may it substitute its own damages assessment without ordering a new trial?
opts: The Court of Appeal may only affirm or reverse a trial judgment; it may not vary the judgment or substitute its own assessment of damages.

Under s.134 of the Courts of Justice Act, the Court of Appeal may affirm, reverse, or vary the order appealed from; it may substitute its own findings of fact and award different damages without ordering a new trial where the record is sufficient to enable it to do so.
The Court of Appeal may only order a new trial if it finds any error by the trial judge; it may not vary the quantum of a damages award.
The Court of Appeal must order a new trial whenever it finds a palpable and overriding error of fact; it has no power to substitute its own damages assessment.
expl: Under s.134(1) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, the Court of Appeal may, on appeal from a final order: (a) affirm, vary, or set aside the order appealed from; (b) substitute its own decision for that of the court appealed from; (c) direct a new trial; or (d) make any other order that is just. Where the record is complete and sufficient to enable the Court of Appeal to assess damages without hearing fresh evidence, the court may substitute its own damages award rather than ordering a new trial. A new trial is ordered where a re-assessment of damages would require credibility determinations or other findings best left to a trial court. The fact that the record supports a different damages assessment of $180,000 may permit the Court of Appeal to vary the award directly.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.134(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Scope of Appellate Powers — Affirm, Reverse, Vary, and New Trial

text:
After an interlocutory motion on a procedural matter, the judge makes a costs order that the 'costs of the motion be costs in the cause.' Neither party understands what this means in practice. Later, the plaintiff succeeds at trial and is awarded costs of the action.

What is the meaning and effect of a 'costs in the cause' order made on an interlocutory motion?
opts: A 'costs in the cause' order means that each party must immediately pay its own costs of the motion; neither party recovers anything from the other.

A 'costs in the cause' order means that the costs of the interlocutory motion will follow the outcome of the trial or proceeding — the party who ultimately succeeds in the action will also recover the costs of the motion.
A 'costs in the cause' order means the costs of the motion are reserved for determination by the trial judge at the conclusion of trial, who may make any order.
A 'costs in the cause' order means the motion costs are awarded outright to the moving party regardless of the outcome of the action.
expl: A 'costs in the cause' order is a standard form of interlocutory costs disposition under Ontario civil practice. Its effect is that the costs of the interlocutory motion are not determined immediately; instead, they follow the outcome of the overall proceeding. The party who ultimately succeeds at trial or in the proceeding is entitled to recover not only the costs of the action but also the costs of any interlocutory motions where 'costs in the cause' was ordered. In the scenario, since the plaintiff succeeded at trial and was awarded costs of the action, the plaintiff also recovers the costs of the interlocutory motion. 'Costs in the cause' is distinguished from: 'costs to the moving party' (immediate award to winner of motion); 'no costs' (each party bears own costs of motion); and 'costs reserved' (judge decides later).
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Interlocutory Motion Costs — Costs in the Cause and Thrown Away

text:
In a proposed class action against a bank for mis-selling investment products, the plaintiff argues that whether the bank owed a duty of care to all class members is a common issue. The defendant argues that because individual class members had different levels of financial sophistication and different conversations with bank advisors, there can be no common issues and certification must be refused.

Under s.5(1)(c) of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992 and the guidance from Hollick v. Toronto (City), what qualifies as a 'common issue' for purposes of certification?
opts: A common issue exists only where all class members have identical claims and the resolution of the issue would be identical for every class member.

A common issue is an issue of fact or law that, if decided, would advance the claims of all class members in a material way; common issues need not be identical across all class members and need not predominate over individual issues.
A common issue must be the predominant issue in the class action; if individual issues predominate, the certification must be refused regardless of whether there are some common issues.
A common issue exists only where the defendant's conduct was uniform and identical with respect to every class member.
expl: Under the test confirmed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Hollick v. Toronto (City), [2001] 3 SCR 158, a common issue is an issue of fact or law that, if decided, would advance the claims of class members in a material way. The test does not require that all class members have identical claims or that the resolution be identical for each member. Section 5(1)(c) of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992 expressly states that common issues may be certified 'whether or not those common issues predominate over issues affecting only individual class members.' Whether the bank owed a duty of care to customers is a question whose resolution would advance the claims of all class members, even if the breach analysis differs by individual circumstances.
caseRef: Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, s.5(1)(c); Hollick v. Toronto (City), [2001] 3 SCR 158
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 16 — Class Proceedings | Common Issues — What Qualifies as a Common Issue

text:
A trial judge exercises discretion in granting an interlocutory injunction. On appeal, the appellant argues that the trial judge weighed the balance of convenience differently than the appellant would have preferred. The respondent argues that the Court of Appeal should defer to the trial judge's exercise of discretion.

What is the standard of appellate review for a trial judge's exercise of discretion, such as the decision to grant or refuse an interlocutory injunction?
opts: Appellate courts review discretionary decisions on a correctness standard; the Court of Appeal may freely substitute its own view of how the discretion should have been exercised.

Discretionary decisions are reviewed deferentially; an appellate court will only intervene where the trial judge misdirected himself or herself in law, proceeded on a wrong principle, or reached a result so clearly wrong that it can be characterized as an injustice.
Discretionary decisions are never subject to appellate review; an exercise of discretion is final and binding regardless of the reasoning.
Discretionary decisions are reviewed on a balance of probabilities; the appellate court determines whether the trial judge more probably than not exercised the discretion correctly.
expl: In Ontario and across Canada, appellate courts apply a highly deferential standard when reviewing a trial judge's exercise of discretion. An appellate court will not interfere with a discretionary decision simply because it would have weighed the factors differently. Intervention is justified only where: (1) the trial judge misdirected himself or herself in law (applied the wrong legal principles); (2) the trial judge clearly proceeded on a wrong principle; or (3) the decision is so clearly wrong as to amount to an injustice. This high threshold reflects that discretionary decisions depend on the judge's weighing of contextual factors, which the trial judge is best positioned to assess. A mere difference of opinion about how the balance of convenience should have been assessed is not a sufficient ground for appellate intervention.
caseRef: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.134
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Appellate Review of Discretionary Decisions — Deference

text:
The plaintiff accepts the defendant's offer to settle for $80,000 by serving written acceptance. The plaintiff then files a notice of acceptance with the court and asks the registrar to enter a judgment for $80,000 against the defendant. The defendant objects, arguing the plaintiff cannot enter judgment unilaterally after acceptance.

Under r.49.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the consequences of an accepted offer to settle for the underlying action, and how is the settlement enforced?
opts: Acceptance of an offer to settle automatically converts the accepted offer into a judgment of the court, which may be enforced immediately without further steps.

Under r.49.09, where an offer to settle is accepted, the court may stay the action or make such other orders as are just; if the defendant fails to comply with the terms of the accepted offer, the plaintiff may move to enforce the settlement agreement, obtain a consent judgment, or have the action dismissed.
Acceptance of an offer to settle operates as a final dismissal of the action; the plaintiff cannot take any further steps in the action and must commence a new action if the defendant fails to pay.
After acceptance, the plaintiff may immediately enter a judgment by registrar for the amount of the offer without any further motion or consent from the defendant.
expl: Under r.49.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), upon acceptance of an offer to settle, the court may stay the proceeding in whole or in part on such terms as are just, or make such other order as is just. The accepted offer creates a binding contractual settlement, but it does not automatically convert into a judgment. If the defendant fails to comply with the terms of the settlement (e.g., fails to pay the agreed amount), the plaintiff may move to enforce the settlement by seeking a consent judgment, bringing a motion on the settlement agreement, or having the action dismissed on the agreed terms. The plaintiff cannot unilaterally enter a judgment without a court order or consent.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.09
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Effect of Acceptance — Staying Proceedings and Consent Judgment

text:
Default judgment was obtained against the defendant for $90,000. The defendant brings a motion under r.19.08 to set aside the default judgment. The defendant explains that the default occurred because the defendant changed addresses and did not receive the claim. The defendant also provides an affidavit from a lawyer outlining a potential defence to the action on the merits.

Under r.19.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the test for setting aside a default judgment?
opts: A default judgment will be set aside if the defendant can show any reason for the default, however weak; the courts favour allowing cases to be decided on their merits.

Under r.19.08, a court may set aside a default judgment on such terms as are just; the court generally considers whether the defendant has provided a reasonable explanation for the default and whether there is a prima facie defence on the merits — a meritorious defence.
A default judgment can only be set aside if the plaintiff's counsel consents to setting it aside; the defendant has no right to bring a motion without the plaintiff's consent.
A default judgment will be set aside automatically upon the defendant filing an affidavit explaining the reason for the default; no court order is required.
expl: Under r.19.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a court may set aside or vary a default judgment on such terms as are just. The court exercises a broad discretion. The established two-part test requires the defendant to demonstrate: (1) a reasonable explanation for the failure to file a defence (i.e., a satisfactory reason for the default); and (2) a prima facie defence on the merits — meaning the defendant has at least an arguable case or a defence that is not bound to fail. Courts also consider the promptness with which the defendant moved to set aside the judgment, and any prejudice to the plaintiff from setting it aside. Failure to establish either element may result in the motion being dismissed.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.08
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 15 — Default Proceedings | Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment — r.19.08 Test

text:
A self-represented plaintiff succeeds in a contract action at trial. The plaintiff spent significant time preparing for and conducting the litigation, and claims compensation for that time at a rate equivalent to what a lawyer would charge. The defendant argues that a self-represented litigant is not entitled to any costs because they did not pay legal fees.

Under Fong v. Chan (1999), 46 OR (3d) 330 (CA), are self-represented litigants entitled to costs, and how are those costs calculated?
opts: Self-represented litigants are not entitled to any costs; only parties who have retained legal counsel and paid legal fees may recover costs.

Under Fong v. Chan, self-represented litigants may be awarded costs for time spent on the litigation, but the costs are assessed at a reduced rate reflecting that the litigant is not a lawyer; the court awards compensation for the litigant's actual time and effort, not at full lawyer rates.
Self-represented litigants are entitled to costs at the full rate charged by a senior lawyer in Ontario, because the court treats all successful parties equally.
Self-represented litigants may only recover disbursements (out-of-pocket expenses) but not any amount for their time; the concept of 'fees' does not apply to non-lawyers.
expl: In Fong v. Chan (1999), 46 OR (3d) 330 (CA), the Ontario Court of Appeal held that a self-represented litigant may be awarded costs for the time and effort invested in the litigation, but the quantum of those costs is assessed differently from costs for a represented party. A self-represented litigant may recover compensation for their time at a rate that reflects the work done and the complexity of the matter, but not at the full rate that would be charged by a licensed lawyer. The court considers the reasonableness of the time spent and the value of the litigant's contribution to the proceedings. The award is intended to provide fair compensation for the burden of self-representation, not to create a windfall or to equate non-lawyers with counsel.
caseRef: Fong v. Chan (1999), 46 OR (3d) 330 (CA); Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Self-Represented Litigants and Costs — Fong v. Chan

text:
A judgment creditor has an unsatisfied judgment for $750,000. The judgment debtor carries on a business generating income. The judgment creditor believes that writ of seizure and sale and garnishment are insufficient to satisfy the judgment because the debtor is disposing of assets as quickly as they are generated. The creditor seeks appointment of a receiver over the debtor's business.

Under r.41.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure and s.101 of the Courts of Justice Act, in what circumstances will a court appoint a receiver to assist with enforcement of a judgment?
opts: A receiver may be appointed to enforce a judgment only if the judgment creditor has already exhausted all other enforcement mechanisms including writs, garnishment, and examination in aid.

Under r.41.02 and s.101 of the Courts of Justice Act, a court may appoint a receiver by interlocutory order where it appears just and convenient to do so, having regard to: the nature of the debtor's assets, the risk of dissipation or disposal of assets, the inadequacy of other enforcement methods, and the balance of convenience; appointment does not require prior exhaustion of other enforcement methods.
A receiver may only be appointed if the judgment debtor consents; a court cannot impose a receiver over a debtor's active business without consent.
A receiver order is only available in insolvency proceedings under the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act; it is not available as an enforcement tool in ordinary civil litigation.
expl: Under r.41.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and s.101 of the Courts of Justice Act, a court may appoint a receiver by interlocutory order where it appears just and convenient to do so. In the enforcement context, courts consider: the nature and accessibility of the judgment debtor's assets; the risk that assets will be dissipated or disposed of before they can be seized; the inadequacy or impracticality of other enforcement methods such as writs and garnishment; and the balance of convenience between the parties. A receiver is appointed to take control of and manage the debtor's property or business to preserve assets and apply income toward satisfaction of the judgment. Prior exhaustion of other enforcement methods is not a strict prerequisite, although the court will consider whether less intrusive means are adequate.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.41.02; Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.101
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Receiver Orders — When Appointed and Duties

text:
A lawyer is preparing a statement of claim for a commercial dispute. The client asks whether the statement of claim must include an endorsement and, if so, what the endorsement must contain.

Under r.14.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what must a statement of claim include as part of its form?
opts: A statement of claim need not include an endorsement; the relief sought is set out only in the body of the claim.
A statement of claim must include a backsheet only, setting out the names of the parties and the court; no endorsement is required.
A statement of claim must be endorsed with a statement of the nature of the claim and the relief sought, which serves as a short-form summary of the action.
A statement of claim must include the complete evidence the plaintiff intends to rely upon at trial, endorsed on the backsheet.
expl: Under r.14.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an originating process — including a statement of claim — shall be endorsed with a concise statement of the nature of the claim and the relief sought. This endorsement appears on the face of the claim and provides the defendant with a brief summary of what the action is about. The endorsement is distinct from the body of the claim, which sets out the full material facts and relief claimed in detail.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.03
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Statement of Claim — Form and Endorsement

text:
The plaintiff seeks to have the defendant's directing mind committed to jail for contempt after the defendant company failed to comply with an injunction. Counsel for the plaintiff is unsure what procedural steps are required before a contempt order leading to committal can be made.

Under r.60.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what procedural requirements must be met before a court can make an order committing a person to jail for contempt?
opts: A committal order may be made ex parte on notice from the plaintiff; no formal hearing is required.

Before making an order of committal, the court must: ensure the underlying order that was disobeyed was personally served on the person to be committed; the contempt motion must be served on the alleged contemnor and heard with an opportunity for them to respond; and the contempt must be proved beyond a reasonable doubt at the hearing.
A committal order may be made on the strength of an affidavit from the plaintiff's counsel alone; cross-examination of the alleged contemnor is not required.
Committal for contempt is not available in civil proceedings arising from commercial disputes; it is only available in family law proceedings.
expl: Under r.60.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a committal order for contempt arising from disobedience of a court order requires compliance with strict procedural requirements because it involves potential imprisonment. The requirements include: (1) the underlying court order that was allegedly disobeyed must have been personally served on the person to be committed (or the person must have had actual notice of it); (2) the contempt motion must be served on the alleged contemnor, giving them a fair opportunity to respond; (3) a hearing must take place at which the alleged contemnor has the opportunity to be heard; and (4) the contempt must be established beyond a reasonable doubt at the hearing. These procedural protections reflect the quasi-criminal nature of contempt proceedings involving liberty.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Contempt — Committal Process and Requirements

text:
At trial in a construction defect case, the plaintiff's counsel seeks to introduce a computer-generated animation depicting how the roof collapse allegedly occurred. The defendant objects, arguing the animation is not real evidence and should not be admitted. Plaintiff's counsel argues it is admissible as demonstrative evidence.

What is the general test for admissibility of demonstrative evidence such as models, charts, animations, and diagrams at trial in Ontario?
opts: Demonstrative evidence is never admissible at civil trial; only documentary and viva voce evidence is admissible.

Demonstrative evidence such as computer animations, models, charts, and diagrams is admissible if: it is relevant to a matter at issue; it fairly and accurately represents what it purports to depict; and its probative value is not substantially outweighed by the risk of prejudice, undue consumption of time, or misleading the trier of fact.
Demonstrative evidence is only admissible if agreed to by both parties; unilateral opposition automatically renders it inadmissible.
Computer-generated animations and other reconstructions are always inadmissible because they are created by the party seeking to introduce them and are therefore inherently self-serving.
expl: Demonstrative evidence — including models, photographs, diagrams, charts, and computer-generated animations — is admissible in Ontario civil trials if it meets the general admissibility criteria. The key requirements are: (1) relevance to a matter in issue at trial; (2) fair and accurate representation of what it purports to show (established through foundational testimony from a witness with knowledge); and (3) probative value that outweighs any prejudicial effect, risk of misleading the trier of fact, or undue consumption of court time. Courts have admitted computer animations in complex litigation where they are properly authenticated and where they fairly represent the expert's opinion or a proven scenario. A computer animation's having been created by a party does not automatically render it inadmissible; the self-serving nature of evidence affects weight, not necessarily admissibility.
caseRef: Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23; Ontario civil trial practice
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Demonstrative Evidence at Trial — Admissibility and Conditions

text:
Vasquez Financial Services Ltd. obtains a noting in default against two defendants. The first defendant owes $35,000 under an unpaid promissory note — the exact amount is certain and undisputed. The second defendant is alleged to have negligently damaged the plaintiff's equipment and owes an amount of damages that must be assessed. Counsel must advise on how default judgment is obtained for each claim.

Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, how does the process for obtaining default judgment differ between a liquidated claim and an unliquidated claim after noting in default?
opts: The process is identical for both claims; the plaintiff must bring a motion to the court for default judgment in all cases.

For a liquidated claim, the plaintiff may obtain default judgment by filing a requisition with the registrar under r.19.02; for an unliquidated claim, the plaintiff must bring a motion to the court for assessment of damages under r.19.04.

For both liquidated and unliquidated claims, the plaintiff may obtain default judgment by requisition to the registrar, but the registrar will fix damages for unliquidated claims based on the plaintiff's affidavit.
There is no difference; default judgment for any amount of money is signed by the registrar automatically upon noting in default.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the process for obtaining default judgment differs based on whether the claim is liquidated or unliquidated. Under r.19.02, where the claim is for a debt or liquidated demand in money (i.e., an amount that is certain or ascertainable without assessment), the plaintiff may sign default judgment by filing a requisition with the registrar — no court motion is required. Under r.19.04, where the claim is for unliquidated damages (i.e., where the amount must be assessed — such as general damages for negligence), the plaintiff must bring a motion to the court and the court may proceed to assess the damages. The $35,000 promissory note is a liquidated claim under r.19.02; the equipment damage claim is unliquidated and must proceed under r.19.04.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.02; r.19.04
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Default Judgment — Liquidated vs Unliquidated Claims

text:
At trial in a wrongful dismissal action, the plaintiff calls the defendant's former human resources director as a witness. During examination in chief, the witness gives testimony that is clearly favourable to the defendant and contradicts what the plaintiff expected based on the witness's prior statements. Plaintiff's counsel asks the trial judge to declare the witness adverse.

Under s.23 of the Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, what is the effect of a court declaring a witness adverse, and what cross-examination tools become available to the calling party?
opts: Once declared adverse, the witness is removed from the stand and the party that called them is permitted to call a replacement witness on the same subject.

Once a witness is declared adverse under s.23 of the Evidence Act, the calling party may cross-examine the adverse witness, including putting prior inconsistent statements to them and asking leading questions; the witness's credibility may be impeached by the calling party.
A witness can only be declared adverse if they have a prior criminal record; unwelcome testimony alone is not a sufficient basis for an adverse declaration.
Once declared adverse, the witness's entire testimony is automatically struck from the record; the trial judge disregards all of it.
expl: Under s.23 of the Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, where a party who calls a witness finds that the witness is adverse — meaning hostile to the party's case, giving testimony inconsistent with prior statements, or otherwise acting contrary to the party's interests — the party may apply to the trial judge for a declaration that the witness is adverse. Once declared adverse, the calling party may cross-examine the adverse witness using all the tools available on cross-examination, including: asking leading questions; putting prior inconsistent statements to the witness; and generally impeaching the witness's credibility. A witness who gives unhelpful testimony is a common basis for the adverse declaration; a criminal record is not required.
caseRef: Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, s.23
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Adverse Witness — s.23 Evidence Act

text:
A court order requires the defendant to deliver a unique piece of industrial equipment to the plaintiff by a specified date. The defendant fails to deliver the equipment. The plaintiff's counsel asks what enforcement mechanism is available for an order to deliver specific personal property.

Under r.60.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what enforcement mechanism is available to enforce an order for the recovery or delivery of personal property?
opts: The only remedy for non-delivery of specific personal property ordered by the court is to bring a new action for the equivalent monetary value; there is no mechanism to compel actual delivery.

Under r.60.10, the judgment creditor may issue a writ of delivery, which directs the sheriff to seize the specific property from the judgment debtor and deliver it to the judgment creditor; where the property cannot be found, the writ may direct the sheriff to seize other property of the debtor to the assessed value.
An order for delivery of specific personal property is enforced by contempt motion only; there is no writ process available.
Orders for delivery of personal property are unenforceable in Ontario; only monetary judgments and injunctions may be enforced through court process.
expl: Under r.60.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party who is entitled to recover or is given possession of specific personal property under a court order may enforce the order by issuing a writ of delivery. The writ directs the sheriff to seize the specific property from the judgment debtor and deliver it to the judgment creditor. If the property cannot be found or has been disposed of, the writ of delivery may be supplemented to allow the sheriff to seize other property of the debtor to the assessed value of the specific property. This writ is particularly important where the property is unique (such as specialized equipment) and monetary damages would be an inadequate substitute.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.10
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Enforcement of Orders for Recovery of Personal Property

text:
The defendant in a contract action brings a motion to dismiss for delay under r.24.01. The action was commenced four years ago. The plaintiff has not served the statement of claim on the defendant within the time required and has not taken any step in the action for two years. The defendant argues that r.24.01 and r.48.14 are interchangeable.

What is the key distinction between a motion to dismiss for delay under r.24.01 and the registrar's dismissal process under r.48.14?
opts: Rule 24.01 and r.48.14 are interchangeable; either rule may be invoked whenever an action has been dormant for any period.

Rule 24.01 applies to delay in taking steps prior to set-down and may be invoked by a party on motion; r.48.14 is the registrar-initiated process triggered when an action has not been set down for trial within the prescribed period. Rule 24.01 requires proof that the delay is inordinate and inexcusable and has caused prejudice.
Rule 48.14 is available only to the defendant; r.24.01 may be used by either party.
Rule 24.01 requires a minimum of five years of delay; r.48.14 applies after only one year of inactivity.
expl: Rule 24.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) allows a defendant to bring a motion to dismiss for delay where the plaintiff has not taken steps required by the rules (e.g., serving the claim, serving the record) within the prescribed time. To succeed under r.24.01, the defendant must show: (1) inordinate delay; (2) the delay is inexcusable; and (3) the delay has caused or is likely to cause serious prejudice to the defendant. Rule 48.14, by contrast, is the registrar-initiated status hearing process that applies specifically when an action has not been set down for trial. The two rules address different stages and situations of delay. They are not interchangeable, and the tests differ.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.24.01; r.48.14
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Dismissal for Delay — r.24.01 vs r.48.14

text:
A judgment creditor files a writ of seizure and sale with the sheriff and directs the sheriff to seize and sell assets of the judgment debtor. The judgment debtor is an individual who works as a tradesperson. The debtor objects that certain items are exempt from seizure.

Under the Execution Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.24, which of the following categories of property is generally exempt from seizure under a writ of seizure and sale against an individual debtor?
opts: All personal property is exempt; only real property may be seized under a writ of seizure and sale.
No property is exempt from seizure under a writ; the writ gives the sheriff authority to seize all property of the judgment debtor without restriction.

Under the Execution Act, certain categories are exempt including household furnishings and appliances up to a prescribed value, tools and instruments necessary for the debtor's trade or profession up to a prescribed value, motor vehicles up to a prescribed value, and certain livestock; real property may be seized but the debtor's principal residence receives some protection.
Only the debtor's bank accounts are exempt; all tangible personal property may be seized.
expl: Under the Execution Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.24, certain categories of an individual judgment debtor's property are exempt from seizure under a writ of seizure and sale. Exemptions are designed to preserve a basic standard of living and the ability to earn income. Exempt categories include: household furnishings and appliances up to a prescribed value; tools and instruments necessary for the debtor's occupation or trade up to a prescribed value; motor vehicles up to a prescribed value; and certain livestock and farming property. These exemptions mean the sheriff must identify which specific items are exigible (subject to seizure) before proceeding. Corporate debtors do not benefit from these personal exemptions.
caseRef: Execution Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.24; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.07
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Writ of Seizure and Sale — Exigible Property and Exemptions

text:
A judgment creditor has obtained a judgment against an individual debtor for $200,000. The debtor has no real property but holds a significant portfolio of publicly traded shares through a brokerage account. The judgment creditor asks whether the shares can be reached through enforcement.

What enforcement mechanism is available to reach a judgment debtor's shares held in a brokerage account?
opts: Shares held in a brokerage account are exempt from enforcement in Ontario; only physical share certificates are exigible.

The judgment creditor may garnish the brokerage account or, where appropriate, obtain a charging order on the debtor's shares; garnishment of the account is typically available as the brokerage holds funds or securities on behalf of the debtor.
Shares in a brokerage account can only be reached through bankruptcy proceedings; civil enforcement mechanisms do not apply to investment accounts.
Shares may only be seized under a writ of seizure and sale if the judgment creditor personally presents the writ to each company in which the debtor holds shares.
expl: Shares held by a judgment debtor are exigible property under Ontario enforcement law. Where the shares are held in a brokerage account, the judgment creditor may serve a notice of garnishment on the brokerage firm as garnishee — the brokerage holds the shares (and any cash) for the account of the judgment debtor, and the garnishment attaches those assets. Alternatively, for shares held in corporate entities, the judgment creditor may seek a charging order on the debtor's interest in the shares. Under r.60.08, the garnishment process encompasses not only debts owed in money but can extend to intangible property held by third parties for the debtor's account, making it a flexible enforcement tool against investment accounts.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.08; Execution Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.24
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Charging Order on Shares — Enforcement Against Investment Assets

text:
A Superior Court judge makes an interlocutory order during the course of a commercial action refusing to grant the defendant leave to file additional affidavit evidence on a summary judgment motion. The defendant wishes to appeal the interlocutory order. Counsel must advise which court has jurisdiction.

Which court hears an appeal from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice in a civil proceeding, and is the appeal as of right or with leave?
opts: The Court of Appeal hears all interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court as of right.
The Divisional Court hears appeals from interlocutory orders of the Superior Court of Justice; the appeal requires leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act.
The Ontario Court of Justice hears interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court.
Interlocutory orders of the Superior Court are not subject to appeal until after the final judgment is made in the action.
expl: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice, with leave. An interlocutory order does not finally dispose of any issue in the action but is made in the course of the proceedings. The Court of Appeal does not have jurisdiction over interlocutory appeals from the Superior Court; that jurisdiction belongs to the Divisional Court. Leave is required — the appeal does not lie as of right — and the test for leave considers whether the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Interlocutory Appeals — Which Court Hears Them

text:
At trial in a shareholder oppression case, the trial judge finds that the defendant conducted a years-long campaign of deliberate financial manipulation and deceit against the minority shareholders. No offer to settle was made by either party. The plaintiff seeks substantial indemnity costs based solely on the defendant's reprehensible conduct in the underlying transaction.

May a court award substantial indemnity costs where no offer to settle has been made, based solely on the reprehensible nature of the defendant's conduct in the underlying transaction?
opts: No — substantial indemnity costs can only be awarded where a party has beaten an offer to settle under r.49.10; absent an offer, only partial indemnity is available.

Yes — under r.57.01, the court has discretion to award substantial indemnity costs where the conduct of a party — whether in the litigation itself or in the underlying transaction — has been reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous and merits sanction through an elevated costs award.
Substantial indemnity costs are only available upon motion under r.57.07 for a personal costs order against the lawyer; they are not available against a party for the party's own conduct.
No — the conduct of the parties in the underlying transaction is irrelevant to costs; only litigation conduct may justify substantial indemnity.
expl: Under r.57.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court has broad discretion in awarding costs and may award substantial indemnity costs in any circumstances where the court considers it appropriate, including where the conduct of the losing party — either in the litigation or in the underlying transaction — has been reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous. An offer to settle is one pathway to substantial indemnity costs under r.49.10(2), but it is not the only pathway. Courts have awarded substantial indemnity costs based on the defendant's oppressive, fraudulent, or egregious conduct in the underlying facts of the case. A years-long deliberate campaign of financial manipulation and deceit is precisely the kind of conduct that courts have recognized as justifying a substantial indemnity award.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Substantial Indemnity Without r.49 Offer — Reprehensible Conduct

text:
A Superior Court judge dismisses a motion for summary judgment. The moving party argues the dismissal of the summary judgment motion is a final order because the judge made findings on the substantive issues in the case. The respondent argues it is an interlocutory order because the action continues.

Is the dismissal of a motion for summary judgment a final order or an interlocutory order for purposes of determining the correct appellate court?
opts: The dismissal of a summary judgment motion is always a final order because it resolves the merits of the claim.

The dismissal of a motion for summary judgment is an interlocutory order because the action continues; the rights of the parties in the action have not been finally determined. The appeal lies to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act.
The dismissal of a summary judgment motion is a final order if the judge made any findings of fact in the process of dismissing the motion.
The dismissal of a summary judgment motion may be appealed as of right to the Court of Appeal because it was argued at a full hearing.
expl: The distinction between an interlocutory and a final order depends on the effect of the order, not on the length or nature of the hearing. An order is final if it finally disposes of the rights of the parties in the action; it is interlocutory if it does not. When a summary judgment motion is dismissed, the action continues to trial — the rights of the parties have not been finally determined. The dismissal of summary judgment is therefore an interlocutory order, and the appeal lies to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act. This is so even though the judge may have made factual findings in the course of dismissing the motion; those findings are not final because the issues proceed to trial. By contrast, if summary judgment is granted, that is a final order.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Distinguishing Interlocutory from Final Orders — Practical Application

text:
At trial in a commercial dispute, the plaintiff's counsel seeks to tender a fact into evidence. The fact was admitted by the defendant in the statement of defence — specifically, the defendant admitted that the contract was signed on the alleged date. The plaintiff argues this admission removes the need to adduce independent evidence on this point.

What is the evidentiary effect of an admission made in a party's pleading at trial?
opts: Admissions in pleadings are not binding at trial; a party may resile from any statement in its pleadings by simply choosing not to call evidence on the admitted point.

An admission in a party's pleading constitutes a formal admission of fact that is binding on the admitting party at trial; the opposing party need not call evidence to prove the admitted fact and the trial judge accepts it as established.
Admissions in pleadings are only binding if confirmed in writing by counsel on the day of trial; without such confirmation, the admission has no evidential effect.
Admissions in pleadings are treated as hearsay and must be authenticated by the party that made them before they are accepted as evidence.
expl: Under Ontario evidence and procedural law, an admission made in a party's pleading — whether in a statement of claim, statement of defence, or reply — constitutes a formal admission of fact. Formal admissions are binding on the party that made them and dispense with the need for opposing proof. The trial judge accepts formally admitted facts as established without the need for the opposing party to adduce evidence. In the scenario, the defendant's admission in the statement of defence that the contract was signed on the alleged date means the plaintiff need not call a witness or tender a document to prove this fact. A party wishing to withdraw a formal admission in a pleading must obtain leave of the court under r.51.05.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.51; Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Formal Admissions and Admissions in Pleadings

text:
Miriam claims she was exposed to toxic chemicals at a chemical plant. The exposure occurred in 2007, but she did not experience any symptoms until 2020, and only discovered the cause of her illness in 2021. She commences an action in 2023. The defendant argues the action is barred by the ultimate limitation period, even if not by the basic two-year period.

Under s. 15 of the Limitations Act, 2002, what is the ultimate limitation period, from what date does it run, and can it extinguish Miriam's claim?
opts: The ultimate limitation period is 10 years from the date the claim was discovered; Miriam's claim was discovered in 2021, so the ultimate period will not expire until 2031.

The ultimate limitation period is 15 years from the date the act or omission occurred. Since the exposure occurred in 2007, the ultimate period expired in 2022, barring Miriam's 2023 claim regardless of when she discovered it.
There is no ultimate limitation period in Ontario; the two-year basic period is the only limitation that applies.
The ultimate limitation period is 15 years from the date of discovery; since Miriam discovered her claim in 2021, the ultimate period will not expire until 2036.
expl: Under s. 15(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, no proceeding shall be commenced in respect of a claim after the 15th anniversary of the day on which the act or omission on which the claim is based took place. This ultimate period runs from the date of the act or omission — not from the date of discovery — and operates as an absolute bar regardless of when the claim was discovered. The chemical exposure in 2007 triggers the 15-year clock; it expires in 2022. Miriam's 2023 action is therefore barred by s. 15, even though she only discovered her claim in 2021 and the two-year basic period would otherwise have permitted her to sue until 2023.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 15(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | The Ultimate 15-Year Limitation Period

text:
Kowalczyk Logistics Inc. is preparing its affidavit of documents in a commercial freight damage action. Its paralegal identifies a category of GPS tracking logs that were automatically overwritten by the company's server system 90 days after generation, prior to the commencement of the litigation. The logs are plainly relevant to the dispute. Counsel asks how the GPS logs should be dealt with in the affidavit of documents.

Under r.30.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how should Kowalczyk deal with the GPS tracking logs in its affidavit of documents?
opts: The GPS logs need not be disclosed in the affidavit of documents because they no longer exist and the company cannot produce them.
The GPS logs must be listed in Schedule A because they are relevant and non-privileged, even though they no longer exist.

The GPS logs must be listed in Schedule C of the affidavit of documents, because they were formerly in Kowalczyk's possession, control, or power; the affidavit must also state when and how Kowalczyk ceased to have possession and the present location or fate of the documents, if known.
The GPS logs must be listed in Schedule B under a privilege claim, because producing them would be harmful to Kowalczyk's case.
expl: Under r.30.03(3) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), Schedule C of the affidavit of documents must list relevant documents that were formerly in the party's possession, control, or power but are no longer. For each document in Schedule C, the affidavit must state when and how the party ceased to have possession and, if known, the present location of the documents. The GPS logs — automatically overwritten before litigation commenced — were formerly in Kowalczyk's possession and are relevant to the dispute. They must be disclosed in Schedule C, not omitted from the affidavit altogether. The destruction or loss of relevant documents must be disclosed so the opposing party and the court are aware.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.03(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Affidavit of Documents — Schedule C and Former Possession

text:
The respondent successfully defends an appeal to the Court of Appeal. The respondent seeks costs of the appeal. The Court of Appeal also considers whether to award costs against a non-party litigation funder who funded the appellant's unsuccessful appeal.

Under the general costs principle on appeal, who is ordinarily entitled to costs when an appeal is dismissed, and may an appellate court award costs against a non-party?
opts: The successful respondent is entitled to costs as a matter of right; no non-party may ever be ordered to pay costs on appeal.

Under the costs-follow-the-event principle, the successful respondent is ordinarily entitled to costs of a dismissed appeal; an appellate court has jurisdiction to award costs against a non-party (such as a litigation funder) under its inherent jurisdiction and r.57.03, where the non-party has substantially controlled or funded the litigation and it is just to do so.
No costs are awarded on appeal; costs are only available at the trial level.
Costs on appeal are fixed at a flat rate of $5,000 for all appeals; no discretion exists.
expl: The costs-follow-the-event principle applies on appeal as it does at first instance: the successful party on appeal is ordinarily entitled to its costs of the appeal. Where an appeal is dismissed, the respondent who successfully defended the appeal is the successful party and is entitled to costs. Additionally, Ontario courts — including appellate courts — have jurisdiction to order costs against non-parties where the non-party has taken an active interest in the proceedings, substantially funded or controlled the litigation, and it is just to make such an order. Litigation funders who fund unsuccessful appeals have, in certain circumstances, been ordered to pay costs directly, particularly where the funder took active control of the litigation strategy.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01; r.57.03
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Costs on Appeal — General Rule and Non-Party Costs

text:
At trial, the plaintiff seeks to admit hospital records documenting the plaintiff's injuries. The custodian of the records is unavailable to testify in person. Plaintiff's counsel argues the records are admissible as business records under s.35 of the Evidence Act without requiring live testimony from the person who made each entry.

Under s.35 of the Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, what are the requirements for admitting a business record into evidence without calling the person who made the entries?
opts: Business records are admissible without any foundational testimony; any document labelled a 'business record' may be tendered without conditions.

Under s.35 of the Evidence Act, a business record is admissible to prove the truth of its contents if: it was made in the usual and ordinary course of a business; it was in the routine practice of the business to make such records; the record was made at the time of or shortly after the act, transaction, occurrence, or event recorded; and a person having personal knowledge of the matters recorded or who supervised the making of the record provides testimony about those requirements, or the party relying on the record provides notice to the opposing party.
Business records are inadmissible hearsay at trial and may only be referred to in argument, not tendered as evidence.
Business records may only be admitted if the person who personally created each entry appears to testify and be cross-examined.
expl: Section 35 of the Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23 provides a statutory exception to the hearsay rule for business records. A record made in the usual and ordinary course of business, where it was the routine practice of the business to make such records and the record was made at or shortly after the time of the event recorded, is admissible as evidence of the facts recorded. The record may be admitted without calling the individual who made each entry, provided the foundational requirements are established — typically through evidence from a custodian or supervisor with knowledge of the record-making practice, or through the notice procedure. Hospital records documenting injuries are a classic example of business records admissible under s.35.
caseRef: Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, s.35
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Business Records Under the Evidence Act — s.35

text:
Both parties have closed their cases, addressed the court in closing argument, and the trial judge has reserved judgment. One week later, the plaintiff's counsel receives a document that was not available during trial — a newly discovered internal memorandum that contradicts a key witness for the defendant. Counsel brings a motion to re-open the case to introduce the new evidence before judgment is released.

Under Ontario civil trial procedure, on what basis may a party seek to re-open a case after judgment has been reserved?
opts: Once judgment is reserved, no party may re-open the case for any reason; the trial judge is functus and must proceed on the record as it stood at the close of trial.

A party may re-open the case after judgment is reserved by establishing that there is new evidence that could not have been discovered with reasonable diligence before trial and that the evidence is material in the sense that it might reasonably have affected the outcome; the court has discretion to permit re-opening where the interests of justice require it.
A party may re-open the case as of right if it provides notice within seven days of the close of argument; no discretion is involved.
The case may only be re-opened if the new evidence is so conclusive that it would inevitably lead to a different result; a merely material document is insufficient.
expl: Under established Ontario civil trial practice, a trial judge retains jurisdiction to permit a party to re-open its case after evidence has closed and even after judgment is reserved, subject to a stringent discretionary test. The test requires: (1) the evidence was not available and could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence at the time of trial; (2) the evidence is material — meaning it might reasonably have affected the result; and (3) permitting re-opening is in the interests of justice, taking into account the risk of prejudice to the opposing party and the efficient administration of justice. The court is cautious about re-opening after judgment is reserved because of the risk of manufactured or tailored evidence and the delay it causes. Re-opening is not a right and is granted only in exceptional circumstances.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194; Ontario civil trial practice
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Re-Opening a Case After Judgment Reserved

text:
After the plaintiff notes the defendant in default, the defendant's newly-retained counsel contacts the plaintiff's lawyer and asks to be served with all subsequent documents in the proceeding and to participate in any motion for default judgment. The plaintiff's counsel argues that a defendant noted in default has no further rights in the proceeding.

Under r.19.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what rights does a defendant noted in default retain in the proceeding?
opts: A defendant noted in default has no further rights whatsoever in the proceeding; the defendant cannot participate in any way until the noting is set aside.

Under r.19.02, a defendant noted in default may not take any further steps in the proceeding except to move to set aside the noting in default or to participate in a reference or assessment of damages; the defendant is entitled to receive notice of and participate in any motion for default judgment.
A defendant noted in default may continue to file pleadings and participate in the proceeding as if no default had occurred; the noting in default is merely a procedural formality.

A defendant noted in default is entitled to participate fully in all steps of the proceeding including filing a statement of defence; the noting merely places the defendant on record as being in default.
expl: Under r.19.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who has been noted in default may not take any further steps in the proceeding without first obtaining an order setting aside the noting in default — except for specific permitted steps. The defendant may: (1) move to set aside the noting in default under r.19.08; and (2) attend and participate in any motion for default judgment to contest the quantum of damages, including on a reference for the assessment of damages. The defendant is entitled to notice of a motion for default judgment. The plaintiff's counsel's assertion that a defendant noted in default has no rights is incorrect; the defendant may contest the quantum of damages even while in default.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.02
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 15 — Default Proceedings | Effect of Noting in Default — Defendant's Rights

text:
The plaintiff's action is for $85,000 in unpaid invoices — a specific, fixed sum with a precise amount owed. The defendant is noted in default. The plaintiff's counsel asks whether a default judgment may be obtained without a motion before a judge.

Under r.19.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a plaintiff obtain default judgment as of right without a motion, and what type of claim qualifies?
opts: Default judgment may always be obtained as of right from the registrar once the defendant is noted in default; no motion is ever required.

Default judgment may be obtained as of right from the registrar under r.19.04 where the claim is for a liquidated demand — a specific amount of money that is fixed and ascertainable from the documents without the need for a trial or reference; the registrar signs judgment in the amount claimed.
Default judgment may only be obtained by motion before a judge, even for liquidated demands; the registrar has no authority to sign judgment.
Default judgment is not available until 60 days after the defendant is noted in default; a waiting period is required before any default judgment can be obtained.
expl: Under r.19.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a defendant has been noted in default and the plaintiff's claim is for a liquidated demand — meaning a specific sum of money that is certain or readily ascertainable from the plaintiff's documents, such as an unpaid invoice for a fixed amount — the plaintiff may obtain default judgment as of right from the registrar without a motion before a judge. The registrar signs the judgment in the amount claimed, plus costs and post-judgment interest. A claim for unpaid invoices of $85,000 is a paradigm example of a liquidated demand. No judicial hearing is required for the registrar to sign such a judgment.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.04
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 15 — Default Proceedings | Default Judgment — Liquidated Demand as of Right — r.19.04

text:
Beaumont Class Counsel LLP has been retained as class counsel in a certified environmental contamination class action. During settlement negotiations, counsel negotiates a settlement that provides meaningful compensation to class members with properties in Zone A (where contamination was severe) but provides minimal recovery to class members in Zone B (where contamination was lighter). Some Zone B class members object that the settlement does not adequately represent their interests. Class counsel argues it has a duty to the class as a whole and that any settlement providing a net benefit to the majority satisfies its obligations.

Which of the following best describes class counsel's duties in a class proceeding, particularly where different sub-groups of the class have potentially divergent interests?
opts: Class counsel owes duties only to the representative plaintiff, not to individual class members; class members must retain their own counsel if they wish to challenge the settlement.

Class counsel owes a duty to the class as a whole. Where different sub-groups have materially divergent interests, class counsel must consider those divergent interests and, where appropriate, ensure that the settlement is fair to all segments of the class — or disclose the conflict to the court; a settlement that systematically disadvantages a subgroup may not receive court approval.

Class counsel's only obligation is to obtain court approval of the settlement; once the court approves the settlement, all class members are bound regardless of whether their interests were adequately considered.

Class counsel may prioritize the interests of the most economically significant group of class members, as the obligation is to maximize the total recovery for the class as a whole without regard to internal allocation.
expl: Under s.29 of the Class Proceedings Act, 1992 (S.O. 1992, c. 6) and the professional obligations of class counsel, class counsel owes duties to the class as a whole. In a certified class proceeding, class counsel represents all class members, not only the representative plaintiff and not only the most vocal or economically significant sub-group. Where different segments of the class have materially divergent interests — for example, Zone A and Zone B class members with different contamination exposures — class counsel must address the potential for internal class conflicts. If interests are sufficiently divergent, the court may require separate representation for sub-groups. Court approval of a settlement is required under s.29 of the CPA, and the court will assess whether the settlement is fair, reasonable, and in the best interests of the class as a whole — including all sub-groups. A settlement that systematically disadvantages a sub-group without adequate justification risks being refused court approval.
caseRef: Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, s.29
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Class Proceedings | Class Counsel Duties — Duty to Class Members and Court

text:
A judgment creditor suspects that a third party company holds assets belonging to the judgment debtor — specifically, that the debtor transferred money to the third party just before the judgment was obtained. The judgment creditor asks whether the third party can be examined to discover information about these assets.

Under r.60.18 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may a judgment creditor examine a third party — not the judgment debtor — in aid of execution, and if so, on what basis?
opts: No — examinations in aid of execution are limited to the judgment debtor; third parties cannot be examined under r.60.18.

Yes — under r.60.18, the judgment creditor may examine any person who the creditor has reason to believe has knowledge of the judgment debtor's assets or who is in possession of assets belonging to the judgment debtor; this includes officers of the debtor corporation and third parties holding the debtor's assets.
Yes — but only with prior leave of the court and only after the judgment debtor has been examined and the examination has been exhausted.
Third parties holding debtor assets can only be reached through contempt proceedings, not through examination.
expl: Rule 60.18(6) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) provides that the judgment creditor may examine, without prior court order: the judgment debtor; in the case of a corporation, an officer, director, or employee with knowledge; and any other person who the creditor has reason to believe has knowledge of the debtor's assets or who is in possession of assets belonging to the debtor. This broad examination power extends to third parties and is particularly useful where the judgment creditor suspects pre-judgment transfers of assets to related parties. The creditor need not first exhaust examination of the debtor before moving to examine third parties.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.18(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Examination of Third Parties Holding Debtor's Assets

text:
A judgment creditor serves a notice of garnishment on Company X, alleging that Company X owes money to the judgment debtor. Company X disputes that it owes anything to the judgment debtor and does not pay the court in response to the notice of garnishment. The judgment creditor brings a motion to determine the issue.

Under r.60.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the procedure where a garnishee disputes that it owes money to the judgment debtor?
opts: A garnishee that disputes owing money to the judgment debtor has no obligation to respond to the notice of garnishment; it simply ignores the notice and the matter is resolved when the judgment creditor discovers the truth.

Under r.60.08, a garnishee that disputes the debt must file a garnishee's statement disputing the debt within 10 days of being served; if the dispute is not resolved by consent, the court determines the issue on a motion or reference, and the garnishee is bound by the determination.
If a garnishee disputes the debt, the notice of garnishment is automatically void and the judgment creditor must restart the process.
A garnishee that disputes the debt is automatically liable for the full amount of the judgment against the judgment debtor, as the dispute is treated as an admission that money was owed.
expl: Under r.60.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), when a notice of garnishment is served, the garnishee must serve a garnishee's statement within 10 days indicating the amount (if any) that the garnishee owes to the judgment debtor. If the garnishee disputes that any debt is owed, it states so in the garnishee's statement. The judgment creditor may then move for a court determination of the disputed issue. The court determines on a motion or through a reference whether the garnishee in fact owes money to the judgment debtor, and the garnishee is bound by the result. A garnishee cannot simply ignore a notice of garnishment; failure to respond may expose the garnishee to liability for the judgment amount.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.08
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Garnishment — Garnishee Disputing Debt Owed

text:
During the course of a trial, after one day of evidence has been heard, the defendant serves an offer to settle for $60,000. At the conclusion of the three-day trial, the plaintiff is awarded $45,000 — less than the defendant's offer. The defendant seeks cost consequences under r.49.10(1) from the date of the offer. The plaintiff argues the offer was made after the trial had commenced and cannot attract the r.49.10 cost consequences.

Under r.49.10(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may an offer to settle made after trial has commenced trigger cost consequences if the plaintiff fails to beat it?
opts: An offer to settle may only trigger r.49.10 cost consequences if it was made before the action was set down for trial; offers made closer to or during trial have no cost consequences.

Under r.49.10, an offer to settle may be made at any time, including during trial, and may trigger cost consequences if the plaintiff fails to obtain a result more favourable than the offer; the rule does not prohibit offers made after trial has commenced.
An offer made during trial is automatically inadmissible as evidence of settlement discussions and has no cost consequences under r.49.10.
Offers made after trial has commenced are treated as counteroffers to the proceedings and automatically settle the action on the terms of the offer.
expl: Under r.49.02(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may make an offer to settle at any time during the proceeding. Rule 49.10 applies to any written offer to settle that was not accepted and that was made at any time in the proceedings. There is no restriction limiting r.49.10 cost consequences to offers made before trial commenced. An offer made during trial — even on day one of a three-day trial — may trigger r.49.10 consequences if the result at trial does not beat the offer. The court may consider the timing of the offer in exercising its overall costs discretion under r.49.13, but the offer is valid and capable of triggering cost consequences under r.49.10.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.02(1); r.49.10
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Offer Timing — When Offer Must Be Made to Trigger r.49.10

text:
A plaintiff has received a status notice from the registrar under r.48.14 indicating that the action must be set down for trial within 90 days. The plaintiff is concerned about what will happen if they do not set the action down within the 90-day period and asks counsel to advise.

Under r.48.14, what happens if the plaintiff fails to set the action down for trial within 90 days of the status notice?
opts: The registrar issues a second status notice giving the plaintiff a further 90 days to set the action down.
The plaintiff is automatically found in contempt of court and subject to personal fine.
The registrar shall dismiss the action for delay, unless before the end of the 90-day period the parties have filed a consent to dismissal or obtained a court order extending the time.
The action is stayed — not dismissed — until the plaintiff brings a motion to lift the stay.
expl: Under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), once the registrar serves a status notice on the parties, the plaintiff has 90 days from service of the status notice to: (1) set the action down for trial; or (2) obtain an order by consent or on motion to extend the time or dismiss the action on agreed terms. If neither step is taken within 90 days, the registrar shall dismiss the action for delay. The dismissal is automatic upon expiry of the 90-day period without the required steps being taken. The plaintiff can avoid dismissal by promptly setting the action down for trial or by obtaining a court order extending the time, but the dismissal follows automatically if no action is taken.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.48.14
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Status Hearings — r.48.14 Trigger and Consequences

text:
During trial in a personal injury case, evidence emerges that was not anticipated in the pleadings. The plaintiff's counsel seeks leave to amend the statement of claim to add a new head of damage supported by the newly surfaced evidence. The defendant objects, arguing that amendments to pleadings are not permitted once a trial has commenced.

Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a party amend a pleading at trial, and what is the governing test?
