opts: Amendments to pleadings are never permitted once a trial has commenced; all pleadings are frozen at the close of the pleading stage.

Under r.26.01, a party may amend a pleading at any time on consent or with leave of the court; leave is granted on such terms as are just and, at trial, the overriding consideration is whether the amendment will cause prejudice to the opposing party that cannot be compensated by costs or an adjournment.
Amendments at trial are freely available as of right; no leave of court or consent is required once the trial has started.
Amendments to pleadings during trial require a unanimous agreement of all parties; no court order can override a party's objection to a mid-trial amendment.
expl: Under r.26.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may amend a pleading at any time before the close of pleadings on consent or with leave of the court; the rule continues to apply at the trial stage. Leave is granted on such terms as are just. At trial, courts are more circumspect: the key question is whether the amendment would cause non-compensable prejudice to the opposing party — that is, prejudice that could not be adequately compensated by costs or an adjournment. Where the evidence has already been led and the amendment merely conforms the pleading to the evidence, leave may readily be granted. Where the amendment would require significant new investigation or evidence by the opposing party, leave may be refused or granted with an adjournment.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Amendments at Trial — r.26 at Trial Stage

text:
The plaintiff served an offer to settle for $100,000 inclusive of costs. At trial, the plaintiff is awarded $100,000 in damages plus $18,000 in costs for a total judgment package of $118,000. The defendant argues that because the judgment is $100,000 — the same amount as the offer — it is 'as favourable as' the offer, and the defendant should not have to pay substantial indemnity costs after the offer date.

Under r.49.10(2), when the plaintiff's offer is for $100,000 'inclusive of costs' and the plaintiff recovers $100,000 in damages plus costs, is the judgment 'as favourable as' the offer?
opts: Yes — the damage award of $100,000 is exactly equal to the offer amount; the r.49.10(2) trigger is met only on damages, and the offer is considered 'as favourable as' the judgment.

No — where the plaintiff's offer is inclusive of costs, the comparison for r.49.10(2) purposes must include both the damages and costs awarded; a judgment of $100,000 in damages plus separately awarded costs of $18,000 represents a total recovery exceeding the $100,000 inclusive-of-costs offer, so the plaintiff has beaten the offer and r.49.10(2) applies.
An offer expressed 'inclusive of costs' cannot attract r.49.10 cost consequences at all; only offers that do not reference costs trigger r.49.10.
The comparison under r.49.10(2) excludes costs entirely; only the damages component of the judgment is compared to the offer.
expl: Under r.49.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the relevant comparison is between the terms of the offer and the outcome achieved at trial, taken as a whole. Where the plaintiff's offer is expressed 'inclusive of costs,' the comparison must take into account both the damages and costs components. A judgment of $100,000 in damages plus $18,000 in separately awarded costs represents a total recovery of $118,000. This exceeds the plaintiff's $100,000 inclusive-of-costs offer, meaning the plaintiff has beaten its own offer. Rule 49.10(2) therefore applies, entitling the plaintiff to partial indemnity costs to the date of the offer and substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer. The defendant cannot avoid the r.49.10(2) consequences by comparing only the damages award to the inclusive-of-costs offer.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | As Favourable As — Meaning Under r.49.10

text:
Osei & Partners LLP is retained to bring a product liability claim. The two-year limitation period will expire in ten days, but the statement of claim is not yet ready because the expert report is outstanding. Counsel considers whether there is a procedural mechanism to preserve the limitation period without immediately issuing a full statement of claim.

Under r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what document may a plaintiff issue to preserve the limitation period when the full statement of claim is not yet ready?
opts: A plaintiff may file a caveat with the court to preserve the limitation period; no originating process needs to be issued.

Under r.14.08, a plaintiff may issue a notice of action, which preserves the limitation period; the plaintiff then has 30 days from the date the notice of action is issued to serve a statement of claim on the defendant.
A plaintiff may apply for an extension of the limitation period from the court; extensions are routinely granted where the statement of claim is not ready.
The plaintiff must issue the full statement of claim to preserve the limitation period; no abbreviated document is available to hold the limitation date.
expl: Under r.14.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a plaintiff is unable to draft a full statement of claim before the limitation period expires, the plaintiff may issue a notice of action. A notice of action preserves the limitation period from the date it is issued. The plaintiff then has 30 days from the date of issuance of the notice of action to serve a statement of claim on the defendant. If the statement of claim is not served within 30 days, the notice of action may expire and no longer protect the plaintiff's position. This mechanism is particularly useful where supporting expert evidence or other documentation needed to draft the claim is not yet available.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.08
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Pleadings | Notice of Action — Preserving Limitation Period Under r.14.08

text:
A judgment creditor issues a notice of examination under r.60.18 requiring an officer of the judgment debtor corporation to attend for examination in aid of execution. The officer fails to attend on the scheduled date and provides no explanation. The judgment creditor seeks a remedy for the failure to attend.

What remedy is available to a judgment creditor when the person subject to a notice of examination under r.60.18 fails to attend?
opts: The only remedy is to re-issue a new notice of examination for a different date; no sanction is available for the initial failure to attend.

The judgment creditor may move for a contempt order against the person who failed to attend; failure to comply with a notice of examination is a contempt of court and may result in a warrant for the person's arrest or other sanctions.
The judgment creditor must first obtain a court order directing attendance before contempt proceedings can be commenced; a notice of examination alone does not attract contempt consequences.
The judgment creditor's only option is to commence a separate action for damages arising from the failure to attend the examination.
expl: Under r.60.18 and r.60.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a notice of examination in aid of execution issued under r.60.18 has the force of a court order for the purpose of compelling attendance. A person who fails to attend an examination in aid of execution without lawful excuse may be found in contempt of court. The judgment creditor may move for a contempt order, and the court may issue a warrant for the person's arrest (a bench warrant) to compel attendance, or impose other contempt sanctions including fines. This mechanism ensures that the examination process is enforceable.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.18; r.60.11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Examination in Aid — Contempt for Non-Attendance

text:
At the conclusion of a successful summary judgment motion, the moving party's counsel asks the judge to fix costs immediately. The judge asks whether a costs outline has been filed. The respondent's counsel argues that a costs outline is not required for a summary judgment motion and costs should simply be awarded in the amount the judge deems appropriate.

Under r.57.01(6) and the Practice Direction Regarding Costs, when is a costs outline required and what does it contain?
opts: A costs outline is required only at trial; it is not required for interlocutory motions including summary judgment motions.

Under r.57.01(6), a party who intends to seek costs at the conclusion of a hearing must serve a costs outline on every other party; the costs outline sets out the fees and disbursements claimed and is required for all contested hearings where costs may be awarded, including summary judgment motions.
A costs outline is optional; a party may seek costs without one and the judge will estimate costs at whatever amount seems reasonable.
A costs outline must be approved by the opposing party before it can be filed; without opposing consent, costs cannot be fixed at the hearing.
expl: Under r.57.01(6) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the associated Practice Direction Regarding Costs, a party who intends to seek costs at a contested hearing — including summary judgment motions, interlocutory motions, and trial — must serve a costs outline on the other parties before the hearing concludes. The costs outline sets out the fees and disbursements claimed, including the hours spent, the hourly rates, and the disbursements. It allows the court to fix costs summarily without referring the matter to assessment. Courts expect costs outlines to be filed; failure to file may result in costs not being fixed at the hearing or a reduced award. The respondent's position that a costs outline is not required for summary judgment motions is incorrect.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Costs Outline — Required for Fixing Costs at Hearing

text:
The plaintiff discontinues an action against the defendant. The plaintiff then discovers that the same claim can be re-framed more effectively. The plaintiff's counsel asks whether the plaintiff can recommence an action on the same claim after discontinuance, and whether the limitation period has been tolled during the period the original action was pending.

Under r.23.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the effect of discontinuance on a plaintiff's ability to recommence an action on the same claim?
opts: Discontinuance permanently bars any subsequent action on the same claim; it operates as a res judicata.

Under r.23.05, a plaintiff who has discontinued an action may recommence it, but only with leave of the court if more than one action has previously been discontinued against the same defendant on the same claim.
Discontinuance has no effect on limitation periods; the limitation clock stopped when the original action was commenced and recommencement is available at any time.

Discontinuance does not operate as a bar to subsequent proceedings on the same claim, but the limitation period continues to run during the period the action is pending and is not tolled; a plaintiff who discontinues and recommences must ensure the recommenced action is within the limitation period.
expl: Under r.23.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, discontinuance does not operate as a permanent bar to recommencing an action on the same claim — it is not res judicata. However, under the Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, there is no general provision suspending or tolling the limitation period during the pendency of a proceeding that is subsequently discontinued. The limitation period is generally not tolled or suspended during the period an action is pending; it continues to run. If the plaintiff discontinues and the limitation period has expired before recommencement, the new action will be time-barred. The practical risk of discontinuance is that if significant time has passed, the limitation period may have expired.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.23.05; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Discontinuance — Effect on Limitation Periods

text:
The plaintiff notes the defendant in default. Three days after being noted in default — and before any default judgment is obtained — the defendant delivers a statement of defence. The plaintiff argues that the late-delivered defence should be disregarded because the defendant is already in default and the plaintiff intends to proceed to default judgment.

Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, may a defendant deliver a statement of defence after being noted in default but before default judgment is obtained, and what effect does this have?
opts: A defendant noted in default may never file a statement of defence without first obtaining an order setting aside the noting; the late defence is a nullity and must be struck.

Under r.19.03, a defendant noted in default who delivers a statement of defence after noting but before default judgment is obtained may do so; the delivery of the defence after noting in default does not automatically set aside the noting, but the plaintiff may not obtain default judgment if a defence has been filed. The defendant may need to obtain an order setting aside the noting to regularize the proceeding.
A defendant noted in default may file a defence at any time and the filing automatically sets aside the noting in default; no court order is required.
Once noted in default, the defendant is permanently barred from delivering any defence and the only recourse is to move to set aside the default judgment after it is obtained.
expl: Under r.19.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who has been noted in default may, with the consent of the plaintiff's counsel or leave of the court, deliver a statement of defence at any time before judgment has been obtained. In practice, where a defendant delivers a defence after being noted in default but before judgment, the plaintiff may accept the defence (in effect setting aside the noting by conduct) or may bring a motion to strike the late defence, or the defendant may move to have the noting set aside under r.19.08 to regularize the proceeding. The filing of a defence alone does not automatically set aside the noting in default; the noting remains until formally set aside. However, the plaintiff typically cannot obtain default judgment while a defence has been delivered.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.03
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 15 — Default Proceedings | Defence Filed Late After Noting But Before Judgment

text:
At a murder trial, the Crown's key witness recants her earlier statement identifying the accused as the shooter. Her earlier statement was made under oath at a grand jury proceeding (in a foreign jurisdiction) and was audio-recorded but not video-recorded. She had the opportunity to explain the inconsistency on cross-examination at trial.

Under R. v. K.G.B., can this prior statement be admitted as substantive evidence despite being audio-recorded rather than video-recorded?
opts: No — K.G.B. strictly requires video recording; audio recording is insufficient regardless of circumstances.
Yes — the K.G.B. criteria are satisfied: oath/solemn affirmation, reliable record (audio meets this where video is unavailable), and opportunity to cross-examine.
No — statements made in foreign proceedings can never be admitted under K.G.B. as they were not made in Canadian proceedings.
Yes — once the witness recants, any prior statement she made automatically becomes admissible for its truth.
expl: In R. v. K.G.B., the SCC required that the prior statement be made under oath and that there be a reliable record of it, with the paradigm case being a video recording that allows the trier of fact to observe demeanour. However, the court recognized that the video-recording requirement reflects the need for a 'reliable memorialization' of the statement. Post-K.G.B. case law has accepted that audio recordings can constitute a reliable record where video is not available, provided the audio recording adequately captures the content of the statement. On these facts, the statement was made under oath at a formal proceeding (equivalent to solemnity), was audio-recorded (reliable record), and the witness had the opportunity to explain the inconsistency at trial. Courts applying the principled approach have admitted audio-recorded statements where the K.G.B. spirit is otherwise satisfied. The audio recording may satisfy the 'reliable record' requirement in the circumstances.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Inconsistent Statements — K.G.B. Audio Recording

text:
The Crown applies for a dangerous offender (DO) designation against an offender convicted of aggravated assault, his third serious personal injury offence. Psychiatric evidence indicates a severe personality disorder with low impulse control and a pattern of brutal violence. The accused's counsel argues the Crown must prove a pattern of behaviour showing a failure to restrain violent impulses.

Under s. 753(1)(a) of the Criminal Code, what must the Crown establish to support a dangerous offender designation for an offender convicted of a serious personal injury offence?
opts: The Crown must prove that the offender has a diagnosed mental disorder and that the disorder will cause future offending.

The Crown must establish, by evidence beyond a reasonable doubt, that the offence constitutes a pattern of behaviour showing a failure to restrain violent impulses to the point of brutal violence, or that the conduct shows a failure to restrain sexual impulses likely to cause injury.
The Crown must prove that the offender is likely to reoffend within 24 months of release on the basis of actuarial risk assessments.
The Crown must establish prior convictions for at least five serious personal injury offences.
expl: Section 753(1)(a) of the Criminal Code provides that the court may designate an offender as a dangerous offender where the offence constitutes a serious personal injury offence and the court is satisfied that the offender constitutes a threat to the life, safety, or physical or mental well-being of other persons on the basis of a pattern of behaviour by the offender showing a failure to restrain violent impulses to the degree of brutal and repeated violence or a failure to restrain sexual impulses likely to cause injury. The Crown must prove this beyond a reasonable doubt. The dangerous offender designation results in an indeterminate sentence (detention without a fixed end date), subject to periodic parole board review.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 753(1)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Dangerous Offender Designation — s. 753

text:
The accused is convicted in the Ontario Court of Justice of a summary conviction offence and sentenced to 60 days' intermittent custody. She believes the sentence is excessive and wishes to appeal.

Under s. 813 of the Criminal Code, may the accused appeal the sentence in a summary conviction matter, and is leave of the court required?
opts: The accused may not appeal the sentence in a summary conviction matter — only the Crown may appeal a sentence in such proceedings.
The accused may appeal the sentence, but leave of the summary conviction appeal court is required.
The accused may appeal the sentence to the summary conviction appeal court as of right, without leave.
The accused must first apply to the Ontario Court of Justice to vary the sentence before appealing to the summary conviction appeal court.
expl: Section 813(a) of the Criminal Code provides that the defendant in summary conviction proceedings may appeal to the summary conviction appeal court against the conviction, against the sentence, or against a verdict of not criminally responsible on account of mental disorder. The appeal is as of right — no leave is required for either a conviction appeal or a sentence appeal by the defendant under s. 813. In Ontario, the summary conviction appeal court is the Superior Court of Justice. This contrasts with the Crown's right to appeal a sentence under s. 676(1)(d) (indictable) which requires leave. Summary conviction sentence appeals by the defendant are as-of-right under s. 813.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 813(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Summary Conviction Sentence Appeal — s. 813

text:
The accused was arrested for theft and was not advised of his right to counsel under s. 10(b) of the Charter. Police then questioned him for 45 minutes. He made a full confession. At trial, the Crown seeks to admit the confession. The accused applies to have the statement excluded under s. 24(2) of the Charter. The Crown argues the statement was voluntary and therefore admissible under the common law confessions rule.

If the statement was voluntarily made but was obtained in breach of the accused's s. 10(b) Charter right, can the Crown rely on voluntariness alone to ensure admissibility?
opts: Yes — a voluntary statement is always admissible regardless of any Charter violation.
No — Charter compliance and common law voluntariness are distinct requirements; a voluntary statement may still be excluded under s. 24(2) if the Charter breach is serious.
Yes — s. 24(2) applies only to physical evidence, not to statements.
No — a statement obtained in breach of s. 10(b) is automatically excluded regardless of the Grant analysis.
expl: Charter compliance and common law voluntariness are two separate and independent requirements for the admissibility of a statement. A statement that is voluntary under the Oickle confessions rule may nonetheless be excluded under s. 24(2) of the Charter if it was obtained in breach of the accused's Charter rights (such as s. 10(b) right to counsel). The Crown must satisfy both tests. Voluntariness goes to the common law confessions rule; s. 24(2) applies the Grant balancing framework to determine whether, despite voluntariness, the Charter breach was sufficiently serious that admitting the evidence would bring the administration of justice into disrepute. Conversely, a statement may be Charter-compliant but still excluded if it was involuntary.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(b), s. 24(2); R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32; R. v. Oickle, 2000 SCC 38
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | s. 24(2) and Confessions Rule — Independent Tests

text:
The accused is charged with sexually assaulting three different complainants on separate occasions over a two-year period. All three incidents involved the accused meeting vulnerable women at a bus station, offering them accommodation, and then sexually assaulting them in the same manner. The Crown seeks to introduce evidence of all three incidents as similar fact evidence to prove the identity of the perpetrator and a common system.

Under R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56, which of the following factors most strongly supports admissibility of the evidence of all three incidents as similar fact evidence?
opts: The fact that the accused was convicted of two of the three incidents previously.
The degree of similarity between the incidents — the same location, same modus operandi, same victim profile — which provides strong circumstantial evidence beyond mere propensity.
The fact that the Crown has three complainants rather than one increases reliability.
The long time span between offences reduces prejudice and therefore increases admissibility.
expl: Under R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56, the admissibility of similar fact evidence turns on whether the probative value substantially outweighs the prejudicial effect. A key factor in assessing probative value is the degree of similarity — or 'striking similarity' — between the prior acts and the charged conduct. Where the similar acts share a distinctive modus operandi (same location, same method, same victim profile), the inference available from the similarity goes beyond mere propensity and points to a specific system, plan, or identity. This significantly increases probative value. The Handy court emphasized that the more similar the prior acts, the more likely the inference they support is identity or system rather than mere propensity. On these facts, the strikingly similar modus operandi (bus station, accommodation offer, same assault pattern) is the strongest factor supporting admissibility.
caseRef: R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Similar Fact Evidence — Striking Similarity and Modus Operandi

text:
The accused raises the defence of non-insane automatism at trial for aggravated assault. He claims that a sudden and unexpected blow to his head immediately before the assault caused a brief period of involuntary action. The trial judge must determine whether to leave the defence to the jury.

Under R. v. Stone, [1999] 2 SCR 290, who bears what burden of proof on the defence of non-insane automatism?
opts: The Crown must disprove automatism beyond a reasonable doubt once the accused raises an air of reality.
The accused bears the persuasive burden of establishing non-insane automatism on a balance of probabilities.
The accused bears only an evidential burden (air of reality); the Crown then bears the persuasive burden to disprove automatism beyond a reasonable doubt.
The accused must prove automatism beyond a reasonable doubt to displace the presumption of voluntary action.
expl: In R. v. Stone, [1999] 2 SCR 290, the Supreme Court departed from the usual evidential burden/persuasive burden structure for defences and held that for automatism, the accused bears a persuasive burden of proof on a balance of probabilities. This was justified on the grounds that: (a) there is a strong presumption that actions are voluntary; and (b) automatism claims are susceptible to fabrication and courts require more than a bare air of reality. Therefore, unlike defences such as self-defence or duress (where the accused bears only an evidential burden), non-insane automatism requires the accused to establish the defence on a balance of probabilities.
caseRef: R. v. Stone, [1999] 2 SCR 290
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Automatism — Burden of Proof

text:
A 17-year-old is convicted under the YCJA of assault causing bodily harm, which is a violent offence. The sentencing judge is considering a deferred custody and supervision order (DCSO) as an alternative to immediate custody. Defence counsel argues that a DCSO would allow the accused to remain in the community while completing school.

Under the YCJA, which of the following is a statutory prerequisite before the court may impose a deferred custody and supervision order?
opts: A DCSO may only be imposed for non-violent offences; the violent nature of assault causing bodily harm categorically bars a DCSO.
A DCSO is available for any offence and requires only that the accused consent to the conditions.

A DCSO may be imposed only if the sentence that would otherwise be imposed is custody, and the court must be satisfied the young person's presence in the community would not create an undue risk to public safety.
A DCSO requires the Crown's consent and a supporting recommendation in the pre-sentence report.
expl: A deferred custody and supervision order (DCSO) under s. 42(2)(p) of the YCJA allows a court to impose a period of community supervision in lieu of immediate custody. The conditions for a DCSO are set out in s. 42(5): (a) the young person is not found guilty of a presumptive offence or an offence for which an adult would be liable to more than two years' imprisonment would be inadequate; and (b) the court must be satisfied that the DCSO is consistent with the purpose and principles of youth sentencing and the young person's presence in the community on the terms and conditions proposed would not create an undue risk to public safety. The DCSO is functionally a suspended custody sentence — if breached, the young person can be brought back before the court and committed to custody. Assault causing bodily harm is not excluded categorically, though it is a violent offence that will require careful consideration of the undue risk criterion.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Deferred Custody and Supervision Order — YCJA s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5)

text:
Two hours after being robbed at knifepoint, the victim called a friend and calmly described the perpetrator's appearance in detail, including the accused's distinctive tattoo. This telephone call was recorded by the friend. The victim subsequently died in an unrelated accident before trial. The Crown seeks to admit the recorded call.

The Crown argues the recorded call is admissible as res gestae. Which of the following most accurately assesses this argument?
opts: The recorded call is admissible as res gestae because the victim described an exciting or traumatic event.

The recorded call does not qualify as res gestae because two hours elapsed between the robbery and the call, and the victim was calm — indicating deliberate reflection rather than spontaneous utterance; the Crown should rely on the principled hearsay approach instead.
The recorded call is admissible as a dying declaration because the victim subsequently died.
The recorded call is inadmissible because recordings are not admissible hearsay under any exception.
expl: The res gestae exception requires that the statement be made contemporaneously with and in the immediate stress of the startling event, leaving no opportunity for deliberate reflection. On these facts, two hours elapsed between the robbery and the call, and the victim was calm during the call. These circumstances indicate that the victim had the opportunity for deliberate reflection, which defeats the res gestae rationale — the absence of time for fabrication. The Crown's best avenue is the principled hearsay approach (Khan/Starr/Khelawon), relying on necessity (victim deceased) and threshold reliability (the recorded nature of the call provides circumstantial reliability, and the level of detail in the description may provide additional reliability indicators). Necessity is clearly satisfied by the victim's death.
caseRef: R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531; R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57; R. v. Clark (2004), 182 CCC (3d) 1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Res Gestae vs Principled Approach

text:
At a sentencing hearing for fraud, defence counsel submits that the accused entered an early guilty plea, saving court resources and the victims from testifying. Counsel argues this should be treated as a significant mitigating factor.

Under s. 718.2(e) of the Criminal Code and established sentencing principles, is an early guilty plea a recognized mitigating factor?
opts: No — a guilty plea merely relieves the Crown from proving its case; it carries no mitigating weight at sentencing.
Yes — an early guilty plea is a recognized mitigating factor that may significantly reduce sentence, reflecting remorse, saving court resources, and sparing victims from testifying.
A guilty plea is relevant only if the accused has no prior criminal record.
A guilty plea can reduce the sentence by a fixed one-third deduction as required by the Criminal Code.
expl: An early guilty plea is a well-established mitigating factor in Canadian sentencing law. Courts recognize the plea as indicating: (a) remorse on the part of the accused; (b) a willingness to take responsibility; (c) saving judicial resources (court time, prosecution costs); and (d) sparing victims the trauma of testifying. While no fixed discount applies in Canada (unlike some jurisdictions), the timing and circumstances of the plea are significant — an early guilty plea, before the case has incurred significant trial preparation costs, carries greater weight than a last-minute plea. Guilty pleas are also relevant to rehabilitation prospects. The Criminal Code does not mandate a specific percentage discount, but appellate courts have recognized that significant mitigating weight should be given to genuine early guilty pleas.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 718.2; R. v. Wong, 2018 SCC 25
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Mitigating Factors — Guilty Plea

text:
An accused charged with sexual assault seeks to obtain two categories of evidence: (1) the complainant's prior sexual activity with a third party occurring the night before the assault, and (2) the complainant's records from a psychiatric hospital where she was treated after the assault.

Which statutory regime governs each category of evidence?
opts: Both categories are governed by s. 276 of the Criminal Code.
The prior sexual activity is governed by s. 276; the psychiatric records are governed by the common law and require only a demonstration of relevance.
The prior sexual activity is governed by s. 276 (rape shield); the psychiatric records are governed by s. 278.1 (third-party records regime).
Both categories are governed by s. 278.1 as records that may undermine the complainant's credibility.
expl: These two categories of evidence are governed by distinct statutory regimes. Section 276 of the Criminal Code is the 'rape shield' provision that governs evidence of a complainant's prior sexual activity — including sexual activity with other persons. Evidence that the complainant had sexual activity with a third party the night before the assault falls squarely within s. 276. The accused must bring a s. 276.1 application and the judge conducts a voir dire under s. 276.2. By contrast, the complainant's psychiatric hospital records are third-party records held by a person or organization other than the parties. These are governed by s. 278.1 through 278.91 of the Criminal Code, which establishes the two-stage production process. The two regimes are distinct: s. 276 addresses prior sexual activity evidence; ss. 278.1+ address records held by third parties.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276, s. 278.1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | s. 276 vs s. 278.1 — Distinct Regimes

text:
The accused is serving a federal penitentiary sentence. He applies for habeas corpus to the Ontario Superior Court, claiming that prison authorities have placed him in administrative segregation without proper procedural safeguards, violating his rights under s. 7 of the Charter.

Which of the following correctly describes the availability of habeas corpus as a remedy in this situation?
opts: Habeas corpus is unavailable to a federal inmate because the Federal Court has exclusive jurisdiction over challenges to federal penitentiary administration.
Habeas corpus is unavailable once a person has been lawfully convicted and sentenced — it can only be used to challenge pre-trial detention.

Habeas corpus is available in the Superior Court to challenge the legality of a change in the conditions of custody, such as administrative segregation, where the change amounts to a deprivation of residual liberty.
Habeas corpus is an extraordinary remedy available only after all statutory appeal rights have been exhausted.
expl: Habeas corpus is a common law remedy preserved by s. 10(c) of the Charter (right to have the validity of detention determined). The Supreme Court has confirmed that provincial Superior Courts retain jurisdiction to hear habeas corpus applications from federal inmates — concurrent with the Federal Court — because habeas corpus is a fundamental supervisory jurisdiction: R v Miller, [1985] 2 SCR 613. Habeas corpus is available not only to challenge initial detention but also to challenge a change in the conditions of custody that constitutes a deprivation of residual liberty (e.g., placement in administrative segregation, transfer to a higher-security institution). A federal inmate who is placed in administrative segregation without procedural fairness suffers a change in conditions that may engage habeas corpus jurisdiction. The remedy is available even to convicted prisoners for challenges to the legality of the manner of their detention.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(c); R v Miller, [1985] 2 SCR 613
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Habeas Corpus — Residual Liberty and Custodial Conditions

text:
A 16-year-old is serving a two-year non-custodial youth sentence. After serving six months, he applies for a review of his sentence, asking that the conditions be varied because his circumstances have significantly improved.

Under s. 94(1) of the YCJA, after what period may a young person apply for a review of a non-custodial youth sentence?
opts: After one year of the sentence has elapsed.
After six months of the sentence has elapsed or after the person has served one-half of the sentence, whichever comes first.
At any time, provided there is a material change in circumstances.
After six months of the sentence has elapsed.
expl: Section 94(1) of the YCJA provides that the youth justice court may review a youth sentence imposed on a young person, on application by the young person, the young person's parent, the Attorney General, or the provincial director, if six months have elapsed since the youth sentence was imposed. Section 94(2) allows a review at any time with leave of the youth justice court if there are exceptional circumstances. The standard waiting period before a non-custodial sentence can be reviewed on the young person's application is six months. This reflects the YCJA's emphasis on adaptability — youth sentences can be modified to reflect changed circumstances, but with the six-month threshold to prevent repeated applications disrupting the sentence structure.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 94(1), s. 94(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Review of Youth Sentences — YCJA s. 94

text:
The accused is convicted of fraud over $5,000 and sentenced to two years less a day, to be served in the community as a conditional sentence. The Crown successfully argues that the trial judge erred. The Court of Appeal allows the Crown's sentence appeal and substitutes a two-year federal penitentiary sentence. The accused wishes to remain out of custody while he seeks leave to appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.

Under s. 679(1)(b) of the Criminal Code, what must an accused establish to obtain a release order pending an appeal to the Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada?
opts: The accused must demonstrate that the appeal raises a novel question of law not previously decided by the Supreme Court of Canada.
The accused must show that the appeal is not frivolous, that he will surrender into custody in accordance with the order, and that detention is not necessary in the public interest.
The accused must post sufficient surety to guarantee appearance and demonstrate a reasonable prospect of success on the appeal.
The accused need only show that the sentence is manifestly excessive and that an immediate appeal has been filed.
expl: Section 679(3) of the Criminal Code sets out the three-part test for release pending a conviction or sentence appeal. The judge of the Court of Appeal (or SCC) shall order that the accused be released if the accused establishes: (a) that the appeal or application for leave to appeal is not frivolous; (b) that the accused will surrender into custody in accordance with the terms of the order; and (c) that the detention of the accused is not necessary in the public interest. The 'public interest' component in (c) involves balancing the reviewability of the decision (which informs the need to review before enforcement) against the enforceability interest (the public interest in immediate execution of lawful sentences). The accused bears the burden of satisfying all three criteria under s. 679(3).
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Release Pending Appeal — s. 679

text:
A 14-year-old is arrested and charged with assault. She comes from a chaotic home environment and has no stable housing. A youth worker suggests that pre-trial detention would benefit her because a youth facility would give her access to counselling and programming unavailable in the community. The Crown seeks detention on this basis.

Under s. 29(1) of the YCJA, which of the following correctly states the restriction on pre-trial detention of a young person?
opts: A young person may be detained before trial if detention would benefit the young person by providing access to programs or services unavailable in the community.
A young person may not be detained before trial unless a custodial youth sentence could be imposed and the offence appears on a statutory schedule.
A young person shall not be detained in a youth custody facility as a substitute for appropriate child protection, mental health, or other social measures.
Pre-trial detention is unavailable for young persons; release on bail must always be granted to a person under 18 years of age.
expl: Section 29(1) of the YCJA provides that a youth justice court judge or a justice shall not detain a young person in custody prior to sentencing as a substitute for appropriate child protection, mental health, or other social measures. This provision codifies the principle that the youth justice system must not serve as a surrogate for other social service systems that have failed the young person. Even if detention would provide beneficial programming, using pre-trial detention for that purpose is expressly prohibited by s. 29(1). The youth justice court applies Criminal Code bail principles as modified by YCJA principles of restraint, but the s. 29(1) prohibition is an additional statutory bar that overrides access-to-services rationales.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 29(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Pre-Trial Detention of Young Persons — YCJA s. 29

text:
Henri is convicted of a sexual offence involving a child. He has prior convictions for similar offences and psychological assessments indicate a high likelihood of reoffending due to a paraphilic disorder. The Crown applies for a long-term offender (LTO) designation under s. 753.1 of the Criminal Code.

Under s. 753.1 of the Criminal Code, which of the following criteria must be satisfied before a court can designate an offender as a long-term offender?
opts: The court must be satisfied that the offender is a substantial risk to reoffend, but no minimum sentence is required before the LTO designation is made.

The court must be satisfied that: (1) it would be appropriate to impose a sentence of imprisonment of two years or more; (2) there is a substantial risk that the offender will reoffend; and (3) there is a reasonable possibility of eventual control of the risk in the community.
The LTO designation requires prior designation as a dangerous offender; it is only available as an alternative to a dangerous offender finding.
The LTO designation can only be made where the Crown proves beyond a reasonable doubt that the offender will reoffend within two years of release.
expl: Section 753.1(1) of the Criminal Code provides that the court may designate an offender as a long-term offender if: (a) it would be appropriate to impose a sentence of imprisonment of two years or more; (b) there is a substantial risk that the offender will reoffend; and (c) there is a reasonable possibility of eventual control of the risk in the community. The LTO designation is distinct from the dangerous offender designation — it is typically applied where the risk is significant but there is hope for eventual management in the community through supervised release. Upon LTO designation, the court imposes a penitentiary sentence plus a long-term supervision order of up to 10 years in the community following release.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 753.1(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Long-Term Offender Designation — s. 753.1

text:
Bridget initially elected to be tried by a superior court judge and jury for an indictable fraud charge. A preliminary inquiry has been scheduled but has not yet commenced. Two weeks before the preliminary inquiry, Bridget's lawyer advises her to change to trial by provincial court judge alone because the evidence is weak and a quicker resolution would be beneficial. Crown counsel does not consent to the re-election.

Under s. 561 of the Criminal Code, may Bridget re-elect to provincial court judge alone before the preliminary inquiry begins, and does Crown consent determine the outcome?
opts: No — re-election is only available after a preliminary inquiry has been held; it cannot occur before the PI begins.
Yes — Bridget may re-elect without Crown consent because re-elections before the preliminary inquiry are freely available to the accused.
Yes — but only with Crown consent, which Bridget does not have; without Crown consent, the re-election cannot proceed before the preliminary inquiry.
No — the re-election must occur at the first appearance; it cannot occur two weeks before the PI.
expl: Section 561 of the Criminal Code governs re-elections. Under s. 561(1)(b), where an accused has elected a mode of trial that requires a preliminary inquiry (such as superior court modes) and wishes to re-elect before the completion of the preliminary inquiry, the accused may do so — but only with the consent of the Crown. If the Crown withholds consent, the accused cannot re-elect before the preliminary inquiry concludes. After the preliminary inquiry is completed (whether by committal or discharge), re-election to a lower mode does not require Crown consent. On these facts, without Crown consent, Bridget cannot re-elect before the PI; she must wait until after the preliminary inquiry concludes.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 561(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Re-Election Before Preliminary Inquiry — s. 561

text:
Joseph is convicted of assault. Evidence at trial established that he targeted the victim because of the victim's sexual orientation. The sentencing judge must determine the appropriate sentence.

Under s. 718.2(a)(i) of the Criminal Code, how does the victim's sexual orientation affect the sentencing analysis?
opts: The victim's sexual orientation is irrelevant to sentencing because it is a personal characteristic of the victim, not the offender.
Evidence that the offence was motivated by bias, prejudice, or hate based on sexual orientation is a statutory aggravating factor that must increase the sentence.
The victim's sexual orientation is a mitigating factor because it suggests the offence was motivated by personal animus rather than general criminal propensity.
Section 718.2 requires the judge to apply the same sentence as a comparable offence not motivated by hate; the victim's characteristics are irrelevant.
expl: Section 718.2(a)(i) of the Criminal Code provides that evidence that the offence was motivated by bias, prejudice, or hate based on, among other grounds, sexual orientation, is a statutory aggravating factor that the sentencing court must consider and that shall be treated as an aggravating circumstance. The enumerated grounds in s. 718.2(a)(i) include race, national or ethnic origin, language, colour, religion, sex, age, mental or physical disability, sexual orientation, gender identity and expression. The presence of a hate motivation does not merely permit an increase — the court is required to treat it as an aggravating factor. Failure to consider a statutory aggravating factor is an error in principle.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 718.2(a)(i)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Aggravating Factors — s. 718.2(a)(i) Hate Motivation

text:
Clarence was denied bail by a justice of the peace. He wishes to have the detention order reviewed. His lawyer advises him to bring an application to have the order reviewed by a superior court judge.

Under which provision of the Criminal Code may a detained accused apply to a superior court judge to review the detention order?
opts: Section 515(10) — by re-arguing the three grounds of detention before the same justice.
Section 520 — the accused may apply to a superior court judge to review the detention order and seek release.
Section 521 — the Crown applies to a superior court judge to confirm the detention order.
Section 675 — by filing a notice of appeal to the Court of Appeal.
expl: Section 520 of the Criminal Code provides a mechanism for an accused who has been detained in custody to apply to a superior court judge to review the detention order and seek release on bail. The accused must give notice of the application to the Crown. A s. 520 review allows the superior court judge to exercise a fresh discretion on whether detention is justified, considering all the circumstances. The review is not a pure appeal of the lower court's decision on a deferential standard; the judge hears the matter anew. Section 521, by contrast, is the Crown's application to review a release order that the Crown considers inappropriate.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 520
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Bail Review — s. 520 and s. 521

text:
Tariq was convicted at age 16 of theft under $5,000 and received a reprimand as his youth sentence. He is now 22 years old and has had no further contact with the justice system. A prospective employer conducts a criminal record check. Tariq's youth conviction was recorded in the RCMP repository.

Under the YCJA, what is the status of Tariq's youth record at age 22, given that he received a reprimand and has been offence-free?
opts: The youth record remains accessible indefinitely because it was entered in the RCMP national repository.
The youth record is sealed and inaccessible; the access period for a reprimand ends three months after the reprimand was pronounced.
The youth record is automatically destroyed two years after the sentence was completed.
The youth record can only be sealed if Tariq makes a formal court application after turning 18.
expl: Under s. 119(2)(a) of the YCJA, where the young person received a reprimand as the youth sentence, the period during which persons are permitted access to records expires three months after the reprimand was pronounced. After this access period closes, the record is sealed and persons listed in s.119(1) can no longer access it. Since Tariq received a reprimand at age 16 and is now 22, the three-month access period has long expired. The record is effectively sealed. For more serious youth sentences, the access period is longer (e.g., three years after a non-custodial sentence is completed, five years after custody sentences end). The scheme reflects the YCJA principle that youth records should not follow young persons into adulthood.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 119(2)(a), s. 128
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Youth Records — Access Periods — YCJA s. 119

text:
The accused is charged with sexual assault. He claims that he honestly believed the complainant was consenting to sexual activity. The complainant testified that she said 'no' clearly twice before the accused continued. At the close of the defence case, the trial judge must determine whether to leave the honest belief in consent defence to the jury.

What is the correct legal test for whether the trial judge should leave the honest belief in consent defence to the jury?
opts: The trial judge must leave the defence to the jury if there is any evidence that the accused subjectively believed the complainant was consenting, regardless of the basis for that belief.

The trial judge should leave the honest belief in consent defence to the jury only if there is an air of reality to the defence — meaning there is evidence upon which a properly instructed jury acting reasonably could acquit.
The defence must be left to the jury because it is the jury's exclusive role to assess credibility and the subjective state of the accused.
The trial judge must first determine whether the accused's belief was reasonable before leaving the defence to the jury.
expl: The honest belief in consent defence for sexual assault requires an air of reality before it can be put to the jury. The air of reality test (from R. v. Cinous, 2002 SCC 29, applied to all defences) asks whether there is evidence upon which a properly instructed jury acting reasonably could acquit based on the defence. The accused must point to evidence supporting each element of the defence. For sexual assault, s. 273.2 of the Criminal Code further provides that the honest belief defence is unavailable if the accused: (a) took no reasonable steps to ascertain consent; or (b) the belief arose from recklessness or wilful blindness. On these facts, the complainant said 'no' twice — this virtually eliminates the air of reality for honest belief in consent. The trial judge has a gatekeeping role and need not leave the defence if there is no air of reality.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 273.2; R. v. Ewanchuk, [1999] 1 SCR 330; R. v. Cinous, 2002 SCC 29
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Mistake of Fact — Honest Belief in Consent (Sexual Assault)

text:
The Crown seeks to admit a hearsay statement from a young child complainant who is now unavailable to testify. At the voir dire, the trial judge hears evidence that the child was exposed to leading questions by a social worker before making the statement, and that the child's account changed significantly between first disclosure and the recorded statement. The Crown argues the statement is nonetheless sufficiently reliable to be admitted.

At the voir dire stage of a hearsay admissibility ruling, should the trial judge consider the child's inconsistent prior accounts as evidence bearing on threshold reliability?
opts: No — inconsistencies go to weight, not admissibility; the trial judge must not consider them at the voir dire stage.

Yes — evidence undermining the circumstances of the making of the statement (including leading questions and changing accounts) is relevant to threshold reliability and may cause the judge to find that admission requirements are not met.
No — threshold reliability is assessed only by reference to the formal conditions of the statement (under oath, video-recorded), not its contents.
Yes — but only if the inconsistencies are proved beyond a reasonable doubt by the defence.
expl: Under R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57 and R. v. Khan, threshold reliability is assessed by examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement. Circumstances that call into question the reliability of the statement — such as leading questions by an interviewer, suggestibility of the witness, significant changes in the account between disclosures, and the conditions of the interview — are highly relevant to threshold reliability. A trial judge may conclude that the circumstances are so contaminated (leading questions, inconsistency) that the statement lacks adequate circumstantial guarantees of reliability and must be excluded. This is different from the judge independently weighing ultimate reliability (which is for the trier of fact); the judge is assessing whether the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement provide adequate substitutes for the traditional safeguards.
caseRef: R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57; R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531; R. v. B.(K.G.), [1993] 1 SCR 740
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Hearsay — Threshold Reliability and Contaminating Circumstances

text:
Renata is charged with second degree murder after fatally stabbing her spouse. She claims that moments before the stabbing, her spouse taunted her with graphic details of his affairs and threatened to take their children away. She says she 'snapped' and acted in a sudden fit of rage. She seeks to rely on the provocation defence under s. 232 of the Criminal Code.

Under s. 232 of the Criminal Code, which of the following correctly states the elements required for the provocation defence?
opts: The accused must prove on a balance of probabilities that she was provoked and lost self-control.

The provocation must consist of an unlawful act or an insult; the accused must have acted in the heat of passion before having time to cool; and the provocation must be sufficient to deprive an ordinary person of self-control.
Any wrongful conduct by the victim, whether or not unlawful, can constitute provocation, and the accused need only show a subjective loss of control.
Provocation is a complete defence that results in acquittal if established on a balance of probabilities by the accused.
expl: Section 232 of the Criminal Code provides that culpable homicide that would otherwise be murder may be reduced to manslaughter if the accused was provoked. Under s. 232(2), a wrongful act or insult is provocation if it is sufficient to deprive an ordinary person of the power of self-control, and if the accused acted on it on the sudden and before there was time for their passion to cool. The test is partly objective (whether the provocation was sufficient to deprive an ordinary person of self-control — assessed by the objective ordinary person standard) and partly subjective (whether the accused actually did lose self-control and acted suddenly). Note that provocation is a partial defence only — it reduces murder to manslaughter; it is not a complete defence and does not result in acquittal.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 232(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Provocation — s. 232

text:
During jury selection in a murder trial, the trial judge exercises the 'stand aside' power to temporarily set aside a potential juror after neither party objects to the juror. Crown counsel asks the judge to explain the stand-aside power as it currently exists after the 2019 amendments.

Under the Criminal Code as amended by Bill C-75, which of the following correctly describes the stand-aside power during jury selection?
opts: The stand-aside power allows the Crown to set aside a juror without reason; the juror is permanently excluded from the panel.

The stand-aside power is available to the accused only, replacing the abolished peremptory challenge, and allows the accused to temporarily set aside jurors without reason until the full panel is exhausted.

The trial judge has the power to direct a juror to stand aside for reasons of maintaining public confidence in the administration of justice or to maintain the order and dignity of the proceedings; the juror may be recalled if no other jurors remain.
The stand-aside power operates identically to the former peremptory challenge and is available to both the Crown and the accused without limit.
expl: Following the abolition of peremptory challenges by the 2019 amendments (Bill C-75), Criminal Code s. 633 grants trial judges the power to order a juror to stand aside. Under s. 633, the trial judge may direct a juror to stand aside for reasons of maintaining public confidence in the administration of justice or to maintain the order and dignity of the proceedings. This is a judicial power, not a party power. A juror who is stood aside may be recalled if no other jurors remain unexamined. The stand aside is temporary — it is not a permanent exclusion. It is designed to allow the court to manage the panel effectively, not to replicate the abolished peremptory challenge in the hands of parties.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 633; S.C. 2019, c. 25 (Bill C-75)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Jury Selection — Stand Aside Power (post-Bill C-75)

text:
The accused is convicted of impaired driving causing death and sentenced to eight years' imprisonment. He appeals his sentence to the Court of Appeal, arguing that eight years is outside the appropriate range for such an offence in comparable cases and that the sentencing judge placed too much weight on denunciation at the expense of his prospects for rehabilitation.

Under the standard of appellate review for sentence appeals established in R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64, on what basis may the Court of Appeal intervene in a sentence imposed by a trial judge?
opts: The Court of Appeal may intervene whenever the sentence differs from the median sentence in comparable cases by more than 20%.
The Court of Appeal may substitute its own sentence whenever it disagrees with the weight the trial judge assigned to the sentencing principles.
The Court of Appeal may intervene only if the sentencing judge committed an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence, or if the sentence is demonstrably unfit.
The Court of Appeal has a de novo sentencing function and is not bound by deference to the trial judge's sentencing discretion.
expl: In R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64, the Supreme Court affirmed that sentencing judges have broad discretion and that courts of appeal should show deference to trial judges' sentencing decisions. The Court of Appeal may intervene in a sentence only in two circumstances: (1) where the sentencing judge committed an error in principle — including an error in the applicable legal principles, failure to consider a relevant factor, or erroneous consideration of an aggravating or mitigating factor — AND that error had an impact on the sentence; or (2) where the sentence, even absent an identifiable error, is demonstrably unfit. A mere disagreement with the weight assigned to sentencing principles or factors is not a basis for appellate intervention — the sentencing judge's weighing is entitled to deference within the proper range.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 675(1)(b.1); R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Accused Sentence Appeal — Standard of Review

text:
A first-time offender pleads guilty to mischief. The Crown and defence agree that a restorative justice approach, including participation in a community conference where the offender faces the victim and community members, would be beneficial. The sentencing judge must consider whether restorative justice principles are relevant.

Under s. 718(e) and (f) of the Criminal Code, which sentencing objectives are consistent with restorative justice principles?
opts: Denunciation and deterrence — restorative justice is not a recognized sentencing objective in the Criminal Code.
Providing reparations for harm done to victims or the community, and promoting a sense of responsibility in offenders and acknowledgment of the harm done to victims and the community.
Separation from society and rehabilitation — restorative justice is a form of community-based incapacitation.
Incapacitation — restorative justice programs are only appropriate where the offender is separated from the community.
expl: Section 718(e) and (f) of the Criminal Code include restorative justice-oriented objectives: (e) to provide reparations for harm done to victims or to the community; and (f) to promote a sense of responsibility in offenders, and acknowledgment of the harm done to victims and to the community. These two objectives directly reflect restorative justice principles — repairing relationships, making the offender face the consequences of their actions, and providing the victim and community with recognition of the harm done. Community conferences and circle sentencing are examples of restorative justice processes that advance these objectives.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 718(e), s. 718(f)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Restorative Justice — s. 718(e) and (f)

text:
Vera was charged with fraud in April 2023. Her trial in a superior court was completed in November 2025 — approximately 31 months after the charge was laid. Defence counsel brought a s. 11(b) Charter application arguing the delay was unreasonable. Crown argues the case was unusually complex and that institutional delay accounts for the excess.

Under R. v. Jordan, 2016 SCC 27, which statement best describes how the court determines whether the delay is presumptively unreasonable?
opts: The court conducts a four-factor balancing test, weighing the length of delay, the reason for the delay, waiver of delay, and prejudice to the accused.

Under Jordan, a ceiling of 18 months for provincial court cases and 30 months for superior court cases applies; delay beyond the ceiling is presumptively unreasonable unless exceptional circumstances justify it.
The delay is unreasonable if it exceeds 24 months in all courts regardless of the complexity of the case.
The accused must prove actual prejudice from the delay before a s. 11(b) violation can be found.
expl: In R. v. Jordan, 2016 SCC 27, the Supreme Court replaced the Morin balancing framework with a presumptive ceiling approach. The ceiling is 18 months from charge to trial for cases tried in provincial court, and 30 months for cases requiring a preliminary inquiry or tried in superior court. Delay beyond the ceiling is presumptively unreasonable, and the Crown must rebut the presumption by establishing exceptional circumstances — such as an unforeseeable discrete event or genuinely exceptional complexity. On Vera's facts, the 31-month delay in a superior court case exceeds the 30-month ceiling, making the delay presumptively unreasonable. The Crown's complexity argument is relevant to the exceptional-circumstances analysis, not to the initial presumption.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(b); R. v. Jordan, 2016 SCC 27
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Section 11(b) — Trial Within a Reasonable Time

text:
The accused is charged with assault. At trial, psychiatrists testify that he suffers from a major mental disorder that, at the time of the act, caused him to believe he was defending himself against alien invaders and that he did not appreciate that what he was doing was morally or legally wrong.

Under s. 16(1) of the Criminal Code, on what ground is the accused's NCR defence based?
opts: The accused did not commit the physical act of the offence (actus reus) because his body was controlled by the mental disorder.
The accused is not criminally responsible because a mental disorder rendered him incapable of appreciating the nature and quality of the act or of knowing that it was wrong.
The accused acted in an automatism-like state and therefore lacked the voluntary act required for criminal liability.
The accused is not criminally responsible because he lacked the motive to commit the assault.
expl: Section 16(1) of the Criminal Code provides that no person is criminally responsible for an act committed while suffering from a mental disorder that rendered them incapable either of: (a) appreciating the nature and quality of the act or omission, or (b) knowing that the act or omission was wrong. 'Wrong' in this context means morally wrong (R. v. Chaulk, [1990] 3 SCR 1303). On the facts, the accused's delusional belief that he was defending against aliens shows incapacity to know the act was wrong. The defence is NCR (not criminally responsible) by reason of mental disorder, not an absence of actus reus or automatism.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 16(1); R. v. Chaulk, [1990] 3 SCR 1303
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Not Criminally Responsible by Reason of Mental Disorder — s. 16

text:
The accused is charged with possession of cocaine. He brings a voir dire under s. 24(2) of the Charter, alleging that police conducted an unlawful search of his vehicle without a warrant and without reasonable grounds, thereby violating his s. 8 rights. The trial judge must determine who bears the initial burden of proof on the voir dire.

On a s. 24(2) voir dire, who bears the burden of establishing a Charter breach, and who bears the burden on the question of exclusion?
opts: The Crown bears the burden of establishing that no Charter breach occurred and that the evidence should be admitted.

The accused bears the burden of establishing the Charter breach on a balance of probabilities; once a breach is established, the court weighs the Grant factors without any further burden on either party.

The accused bears the burden of establishing the Charter breach on a balance of probabilities; the Crown then bears the burden of establishing that the evidence should be admitted on a balance of probabilities.
There is no burden of proof at a voir dire — the judge conducts an inquisitorial investigation.
expl: On a s. 24(2) voir dire, the threshold question is whether a Charter right was infringed. The accused bears the burden of establishing the Charter breach on a balance of probabilities — this is the general civil standard for the precondition to s. 24 relief. Once the breach is established, the Grant framework requires the court to balance the three lines of inquiry (seriousness of breach, impact on accused's interests, society's interest in adjudication on merits) to determine whether exclusion is warranted. This balancing is not a burden exercise — the court conducts an independent assessment of all three factors. There is no secondary burden on the Crown to 'prove' the evidence should be admitted; rather, the court weighs the evidence of breach and all relevant Grant factors and reaches a conclusion on balance.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | s. 24(2) Voir Dire — Burden of Proof

text:
The accused is charged with second degree murder. He argues provocation as a partial defence, claiming that the victim's taunts about his mental health drove him to a rage. Expert evidence establishes that the accused has a particularly sensitive temperament due to a diagnosed anxiety disorder. Defence asks the trial judge to instruct the jury that the 'ordinary person' for the provocation test should be endowed with the accused's anxiety disorder and heightened sensitivity.

Under s. 232 of the Criminal Code, should the trial judge instruct the jury to apply an 'ordinary person' standard that includes the accused's heightened sensitivity from his anxiety disorder?
opts: Yes — the ordinary person for the provocation test must be assessed with all the accused's personal characteristics, including mental health conditions.
No — the ordinary person standard is purely objective with no personal characteristics of the accused imported; the accused's anxiety disorder is irrelevant to the provocation inquiry.

No — under s. 232(2), the ordinary person standard is given a modified objective character: the ordinary person shares the accused's age and sex and relevant circumstances, but is not endowed with the accused's peculiar sensitivity, mental conditions, or temperamental characteristics that reduce self-control.
Yes — but only if the anxiety disorder was caused by the victim's prior conduct.
expl: Under s. 232(2) of the Criminal Code, the test for the objective component of provocation is whether the wrongful act or insult was sufficient to deprive an 'ordinary person' of the power of self-control. The Supreme Court in R. v. Hill, [1986] 1 SCR 313 addressed what characteristics are attributed to the ordinary person. The ordinary person shares the same age and sex as the accused, as well as other contextually relevant characteristics (for example, a history of abuse by the victim may be relevant). However, the ordinary person is not endowed with the accused's peculiar idiosyncrasies, unusual sensitivity, or mental conditions that reduce their capacity for self-control. Importing the anxiety disorder would undermine the objective component of the test. The accused's sensitivity goes to the subjective component (did the accused actually lose control?) but not to the objective standard.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 232(2); R. v. Hill, [1986] 1 SCR 313
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Provocation — Ordinary Person Standard

text:
The defence seeks to call a linguist as an expert witness to testify that the threatening letters sent to the victim share stylistic features inconsistent with those of the accused's known writing samples. The trial judge must determine whether to admit the expert evidence.

Under R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9, which of the following correctly sets out the four criteria for admissibility of expert evidence?
opts: Relevance; necessity in assisting the trier of fact; absence of an exclusionary rule; and the proper qualification of the expert.
Relevance; reliability of the expert's methodology; independence of the expert; and sufficient peer review of the field.

The expert must hold a university degree in a relevant field; the opinion must be published in a peer-reviewed journal; the field must be generally accepted in the scientific community; and the evidence must be helpful to the trier of fact.

The field of expertise must be recognized by Canadian courts; the expert must be qualified by the judge; the expert must not offer opinion on the ultimate issue; and the probative value must outweigh prejudice.
expl: In R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9, the Supreme Court of Canada set out four criteria for the admissibility of expert opinion evidence: (1) relevance — the evidence must be relevant to an issue in the case; (2) necessity in assisting the trier of fact — the evidence must be beyond the ordinary knowledge of the jury and necessary to assist them in reaching a decision; (3) the absence of any exclusionary rule — no rule of evidence must preclude the evidence; and (4) a properly qualified expert — the witness must be shown to possess special knowledge, experience, skill, or training relevant to the matter. These four criteria from Mohan remain the foundational framework, though subsequent cases like R. v. J.-L.J. and White Burgess Langille Inman have elaborated on the reliability/threshold reliability analysis within these criteria.
caseRef: R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Expert Opinion Evidence — Mohan

text:
The accused was convicted of robbery and his appeal was dismissed by a panel of five judges of the Court of Appeal, with two judges dissenting on a question of law. He wishes to further appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.

Under s. 691(2) of the Criminal Code, does the accused have an as-of-right appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada in these circumstances, and if so, on what basis?
opts: No — an accused may only appeal to the SCC by obtaining leave to appeal on a question of public importance under s. 40 of the Supreme Court Act.
Yes — a dissent in the Court of Appeal on any question creates an as-of-right appeal to the SCC regardless of whether the dissent is on a question of law.
Yes — under s. 691(2), where a judge of the Court of Appeal dissents on a question of law, the accused has an as-of-right appeal to the SCC on that question of law.
No — as-of-right appeals to the SCC from criminal convictions were abolished; all criminal appeals to the SCC require leave.
expl: Section 691(2) of the Criminal Code provides that a person who is convicted of an indictable offence and whose conviction is affirmed by the Court of Appeal may appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada without leave, as of right, if a judge of the Court of Appeal dissented on a question of law. The as-of-right appeal under s. 691(2) is limited to the question(s) of law on which the dissent occurred. This is a significant jurisdictional provision — a single dissenting judge on a question of law opens the door to the Supreme Court of Canada without the need for leave. By contrast, where there is no dissent on a question of law, the accused must seek leave under s. 40 of the Supreme Court Act on the basis that the appeal involves a question of public importance.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 691(2); Supreme Court Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. S-26, s. 40
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Supreme Court of Canada Appeal — s. 691

text:
An officer stopped a vehicle and asked the driver to produce a licence and registration as part of a routine check. The officer, unaware that a recent court decision had narrowed the scope of the authorizing provision, proceeded to search the vehicle under what he genuinely believed was a lawful power. Drugs were found. The Crown concedes the search violated s. 8 but argues the officer acted in good faith.

Under the first Grant line of inquiry (seriousness of Charter-infringing conduct), how does the officer's good faith affect the analysis?
opts: Good faith by the officer automatically results in admission of the evidence regardless of the other Grant lines.
Good faith is irrelevant to the s. 24(2) analysis — only the objective seriousness of the constitutional violation matters.
Good faith is a relevant factor under the first Grant line that reduces the seriousness of the breach and may weigh toward admission of the evidence, but it does not guarantee admission.
Good faith only applies if the officer sought and received legal advice before conducting the search.
expl: Under the first Grant line of inquiry, the court assesses the seriousness of the Charter-infringing conduct. This includes: whether the breach was deliberate, wilful, or flagrant; whether it was an understandable mistake given the state of the law; or whether it fell in a grey area. Good faith — meaning the officer genuinely and reasonably believed their conduct was lawful — reduces the culpability of the state conduct and accordingly reduces the seriousness of the breach under the first line. Where the law was unclear or recently changed, a good-faith error is less culpable than a deliberate disregard of constitutional requirements. However, good faith is not a trump card that automatically results in admission. All three Grant lines must be balanced, and good faith under the first line must be weighed against the second line (impact on accused's interests) and third line (society's interest in adjudication on the merits).
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R. v. Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | s. 24(2) Grant First Line — Good Faith

text:
