Police suspected Marcus of drug dealing but had no evidence. An undercover officer persistently contacted Marcus for several weeks, each time requesting that Marcus obtain cocaine for the officer. Marcus initially refused repeatedly but eventually agreed and provided cocaine to the undercover officer. Marcus is charged with trafficking. He raises the defence of entrapment.

Under the entrapment doctrine established in R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, which of the following correctly describes the legal framework and the available remedy if entrapment is established?
opts: Entrapment is a complete defence that, if proven, results in an acquittal because it negates the accused's criminal intent.
Entrapment constitutes an abuse of process; if established, the court must enter a stay of proceedings — this is the only available remedy.
Entrapment is available only where police had absolutely no reasonable suspicion of the accused before initiating contact.
Entrapment negates the actus reus of the offence because the illegal act was induced by state agents, resulting in an acquittal.
expl: In R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, the Supreme Court held that entrapment in Canada is not a substantive defence that negates mens rea or actus reus, but is instead an abuse of process doctrine. If entrapment is established, the court must enter a stay of proceedings — not an acquittal. Two forms of entrapment were recognized: (1) opportunity-based entrapment, where the state provides an opportunity to commit a crime without reasonable suspicion or bona fide inquiry that the person is involved in criminal activity; and (2) inducement-based entrapment (conduct-based), where the state goes beyond providing an opportunity and actually induces or persuades a person who would not otherwise have committed the offence. On these facts, Marcus's repeated refusals followed by the officer's persistent approach suggests conduct-based entrapment. The remedy for entrapment, if established, is exclusively a stay of proceedings — not an acquittal — because the abuse of process analysis is directed at the conduct of the state, not the guilt of the accused.
caseRef: R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903; Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies | Entrapment — Abuse of Process — Stay of Proceedings

text:
Police received no specific intelligence that Viktor was engaged in drug trafficking. An undercover officer approached Viktor on the street and, over three weeks, asked Viktor to sell him cocaine on eleven separate occasions. Viktor refused ten times. On the eleventh request, Viktor agreed and sold a small quantity of cocaine. Viktor is charged with trafficking.

Under R. v. Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, does Viktor have a viable entrapment defence?
opts: No — Viktor completed the transaction voluntarily on the eleventh request; the repeated requests are irrelevant once a voluntary sale occurs.
No — entrapment is only available when the accused was threatened or coerced by police; Viktor was merely asked, not forced.

Yes — entrapment may be established where police provided the opportunity to commit an offence without a reasonable suspicion or bona fide inquiry, or where police used persistent persuasion amounting to inducement that overstepped the accused's free will.
Yes — entrapment is established whenever police initiate contact with a suspect who had no prior criminal record.
expl: In R. v. Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, the Supreme Court of Canada recognized entrapment as a defence that can lead to a stay of proceedings rather than an acquittal (it is a remedy for abuse of process, not a true defence that negates mens rea or actus reus). Entrapment may be established in two situations: (1) the police provided an opportunity to commit an offence without acting on a reasonable suspicion that the person was already engaged in criminal activity or pursuant to a bona fide inquiry; or (2) although acting on a reasonable suspicion or bona fide inquiry, the police went beyond providing an opportunity and actually induced the commission of the offence through persistent persuasion or other conduct that goes beyond what a reasonable person would resist. On these facts, there was no prior intelligence about Viktor, and eleven requests (ten of which were refused) constitutes persistent inducement that may satisfy either branch.
caseRef: R. v. Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Entrapment — Mack Test

text:
The Crown calls a forensic accountant to give opinion evidence that based on his analysis, the accused 'defrauded the company' through a series of disguised transactions. Defence objects that the expert is offering an opinion on the ultimate issue — the very question the jury must decide — and should be precluded from testifying on this point.

Under Canadian law, may an expert witness offer an opinion on the ultimate issue to be decided by the trier of fact?
opts: No — the ultimate issue rule categorically prohibits experts from expressing opinions on the issues the trier of fact must decide.
Yes — in Canadian criminal law, an expert may offer an opinion on the ultimate issue, subject to the Mohan criteria; the jury is not bound by the expert's opinion.
Yes — but only if the accused consents to the expert testifying on the ultimate issue.
No — in criminal cases, expert evidence on the ultimate issue must be excluded to preserve the jury's function.
expl: The traditional common law 'ultimate issue rule' prohibited experts from opining on the very question the trier of fact must decide (e.g., 'the accused is guilty' or 'the accused defrauded the company'). However, in R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9, the Supreme Court did not include this prohibition as one of the four admissibility criteria. Canadian courts have generally moved away from a categorical prohibition on ultimate issue opinions. An expert may offer opinions that touch on the ultimate issue in criminal proceedings, provided the Mohan criteria are satisfied (relevance, necessity, no exclusionary rule, qualified expert). The jury retains the function of weighing and accepting or rejecting the expert's opinion — the expert's view on the ultimate issue is evidence for the jury, not binding instruction. The trial judge may use limiting instructions where appropriate.
caseRef: R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9; R. v. J.-L.J., 2000 SCC 51
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Expert Evidence — Ultimate Issue Rule

text:
A youth justice court judge is determining the appropriate response for a 15-year-old charged with a first offence of mischief. The judge considers the underlying principles of the YCJA to guide the decision.

Under s. 3 of the YCJA, which of the following is a declared principle of the youth criminal justice system?
opts: Young persons must be held accountable through intervention that is proportionate to the seriousness of the offence and the degree of responsibility of the young person.
Young persons who commit offences should be treated identically to adult offenders to promote uniformity in the criminal justice system.
The primary objective of the youth criminal justice system is general deterrence of youth crime in the community.
Parents bear criminal responsibility for offences committed by young persons under their care.
expl: Section 3(1)(b)(ii) of the YCJA establishes that the youth criminal justice system must ensure that young persons are subject to meaningful consequences and are held accountable through interventions that are proportionate to the seriousness of their offences and the degree of their responsibility. The Declaration of Principle in s. 3 includes: rehabilitation and reintegration of young persons; fair and proportionate accountability; protection of the public; special considerations for young persons due to their reduced moral culpability and greater needs; timely intervention; and reinforcement of societal values. The YCJA explicitly rejects identical treatment with adults and emphasizes individualized, rehabilitative responses. General deterrence is not the primary objective — rehabilitation and reintegration are central.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 3(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Declaration of Principle — YCJA s. 3

text:
A 17-year-old is convicted of manslaughter. The young person has a diagnosed mental disorder that contributed significantly to the offending behaviour. The Crown applies for an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision (IRCS) order, arguing it is the most appropriate sentence.

Under s. 42(7) of the YCJA, which of the following is a prerequisite before the court may impose an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision (IRCS) order?
opts: The offence must be a first-degree murder, second-degree murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, or aggravated sexual assault, and the young person must be suffering from a mental illness or disorder, a psychological disorder, or an emotional disturbance.
The IRCS order requires the prior consent of the young person and approval of the provincial director for the relevant treatment program.
The IRCS order is available for any serious violent offence and requires only a pre-sentence report recommending treatment.
The IRCS order requires that an adult sentence application has been made and rejected before it may be considered as an alternative.
expl: Section 42(7) of the YCJA establishes the conditions for an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision (IRCS) order. The court may impose an IRCS order only if: (a) the young person has been convicted of a first-degree murder, second-degree murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, or aggravated sexual assault; (b) the young person is suffering from a mental illness or disorder, a psychological disorder, or an emotional disturbance; (c) a plan of treatment or set of programmes has been developed for the young person, and there are reasonable grounds to believe the plan or programme would reduce the risk of the young person repeating the offence or committing a serious violent offence; and (d) the provincial director has determined that an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision program is available to the young person. IRCS is a specialized custodial sentence designed to address mental-health-linked serious violent offending in young persons.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 42(2)(r), s. 42(7)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Intensive Rehabilitative Custody and Supervision — YCJA s. 42(7)

text:
Omar is released on bail with a condition that he not consume alcohol. He is stopped by police who smell alcohol on his breath. The officer believes Omar has breached his bail condition. The officer wishes to arrest Omar for the breach.

Under what authority may a peace officer arrest a person who is believed to have breached a condition of their release order?
opts: The officer must first obtain a warrant from a justice before arresting Omar for the breach.
Under s. 524 of the Criminal Code, a peace officer who has reasonable grounds to believe the accused has breached a condition of their release may arrest the accused without warrant.
The officer may arrest Omar only if the breach involves a condition related to contact with the victim.
Breach of a bail condition is not an independent ground for warrantless arrest; the officer must charge Omar with a new criminal offence related to the breach.
expl: Section 524 of the Criminal Code provides that a peace officer who believes on reasonable grounds that an accused has contravened or is about to contravene a condition of their release order may arrest the accused without a warrant. After arresting the accused, the officer must bring the accused before a justice as soon as practicable. The justice then determines whether the bail should be revoked or varied, or whether the accused should be released on the same or different conditions. Breach of a bail condition is a standalone basis for warrantless arrest; it is not necessary that the accused commit a new criminal offence.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 524, s. 145(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Breach of Bail Conditions — s. 524

text:
The accused is charged with sexual assault. He seeks production of the complainant's therapy records from a private therapist, arguing they may contain statements by the complainant that contradict her account of the assault. The complainant objects to disclosure of her therapy records.

Under s. 278.1 of the Criminal Code, which of the following correctly describes the process for an accused to obtain records of a complainant held by a third party?
opts: The accused is entitled to the records as of right upon demonstrating that the records may be relevant to a trial issue.

The accused must apply to the trial judge with written notice; the judge conducts a two-stage analysis: first, whether the records should be produced to the judge, and then whether the records (or parts thereof) should be produced to the defence.
The accused must obtain the consent of the complainant before the records can be produced.
Third-party records of complainants are categorically inadmissible in sexual assault proceedings.
expl: Sections 278.1 to 278.91 of the Criminal Code govern the production of records of complainants and witnesses in sexual offence proceedings. Section 278.3 requires the accused to bring a written application with notice to the Crown, the complainant, and the record holder. Section 278.5 then provides a two-stage process: Stage 1 — the judge reviews the application and, if satisfied that the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or the competence of a witness to testify, orders the record produced to the judge for inspection. Stage 2 — the judge examines the record in camera and, after considering the accused's rights to a full answer and defence (s. 7 Charter) balanced against the complainant's privacy rights (ss. 7 and 8 Charter), may order production of some or all of the record to the accused. Therapy records are expressly recognized in s. 278.1 as records to which this regime applies.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Third-Party Records — s. 278.1 to 278.91 Criminal Code

text:
A trial judge instructs the jury: 'A reasonable doubt is an ordinary everyday doubt — the sort of doubt that would cause a reasonable person to hesitate in important affairs of life.' Defence counsel objects, arguing this instruction misstates the law on reasonable doubt.

Is defence counsel correct that this instruction misstates the law on reasonable doubt, and why?
opts: No — the 'everyday doubt' instruction is the approved Lifchus formulation and accurately states the law.
Yes — the 'hesitate in important affairs of life' formulation was specifically identified in R. v. Lifchus as an improper definition that must not be given to jurors.
No — any commonsense description of reasonable doubt is acceptable as long as it conveys that the standard is higher than balance of probabilities.
Yes — the instruction is wrong because it fails to state that proof beyond a reasonable doubt is proof to an absolute certainty.
expl: In R. v. Lifchus, [1997] 3 SCR 320, the Supreme Court of Canada identified the 'hesitate in important affairs of life' formulation as problematic and specifically cautioned against it. The concern is that this instruction incorrectly equates the criminal standard with the kind of everyday caution used in business or personal decisions — which may be based on probability, self-interest, or emotional concerns, rather than the reason and common sense required in criminal adjudication. The Lifchus model charge directs that a reasonable doubt is: (a) not based on sympathy or prejudice; (b) based on reason and common sense; (c) logically connected to the evidence or the absence of evidence; (d) not proof to an absolute certainty; and (e) more than an imaginary or frivolous doubt. The 'everyday hesitation' formula conflates the criminal and civil standards and was specifically flagged as potentially misleading.
caseRef: R. v. Lifchus, [1997] 3 SCR 320; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(d)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Reasonable Doubt — Lifchus Direction

text:
Nathan, a contractor, removed lumber from a construction site believing he was owed money by the developer and that the lumber was collateral for an unpaid debt. He had no legal security interest in the lumber and no right to seize it as payment. Nathan is charged with theft. His counsel argues colour of right.

Which of the following correctly states the colour of right doctrine as it applies to Nathan's situation?
opts: Colour of right is available only where the accused had a legally valid claim of right, such as a registered lien or court order.

Colour of right applies where the accused honestly believed he had a legal entitlement to the property — the belief need not be reasonable, but it must be an honest belief in a legal (not merely moral) right.
Colour of right is available whenever the accused was owed money by the owner of the property taken; the moral claim is sufficient.
Colour of right is not available for property offences involving lumber or building materials; it applies only to personal property.
expl: Colour of right under s. 322(1)(a) of the Criminal Code provides a defence to theft where the accused honestly believed he had a legal right or title to the property taken. The key requirements are: (1) the belief must be honest (subjective test — not required to be reasonable); and (2) the belief must be in a legal right, not merely a moral claim. A belief that one is morally owed money and is 'entitled' to take property as compensation does not constitute colour of right unless the accused genuinely believed he had a legal entitlement (for example, a mistaken belief in a lien, legal title, or contractual right). On these facts, Nathan's belief that he could seize lumber as payment for a debt may or may not qualify — the court would assess whether he honestly believed in a legal right (not just moral entitlement) to take the lumber.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 322(1)(a); R. v. Lilly, [1983] 1 SCR 794
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Colour of Right — s. 322(1)(a)

text:
The accused is charged with robbery. He did not disclose his alibi defence to the Crown before trial. At trial, the accused takes the stand and testifies that at the time of the robbery he was at a restaurant 40 km away with three friends. The Crown had no opportunity to investigate the alibi before trial.

Which of the following correctly states the legal consequence of the accused's failure to disclose his alibi before trial?
opts: The alibi evidence must be excluded because the accused failed to provide advance disclosure as required by the Criminal Code.

The failure to disclose an alibi before trial does not affect the admissibility of the evidence, but the trier of fact may draw an adverse inference from the late disclosure and scrutinize the alibi more carefully.
The Crown may apply for a mistrial due to the late disclosure of the alibi.
The accused is deemed to have waived the alibi defence by failing to disclose it; the trial judge must direct the jury to disregard the alibi evidence.
expl: In Canadian law, failure to disclose an alibi defence before trial does not render the alibi evidence inadmissible. The alibi is always admissible because the accused has a constitutional right to present a full answer and defence. However, the common law and case law (including R. v. Cleghorn, [1995] 3 SCR 175) recognize that late disclosure of an alibi is a circumstance the trier of fact can take into account when assessing the weight and credibility of the alibi. The trier of fact may draw an adverse inference from the fact that the alibi was not disclosed promptly — if the alibi were genuine, the accused would normally have raised it at the earliest opportunity. The Crown may also draw attention to the late disclosure in cross-examination and argument. The jury should be instructed that late disclosure may affect credibility, not that the alibi must be disbelieved.
caseRef: R. v. Cleghorn, [1995] 3 SCR 175; R. v. Letourneau (1994), 87 CCC (3d) 481 (Ont. CA)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Alibi — Advance Disclosure and Adverse Inference

text:
A 16-year-old with one prior police caution is charged with theft over $5,000, an indictable offence. The Crown is considering diverting the matter through a formal extrajudicial sanctions (EJS) program under the YCJA rather than proceeding with the charge.

Under s. 10 of the YCJA, which of the following is a mandatory precondition before an extrajudicial sanction may be used?
opts: The young person must have no prior criminal record or prior extrajudicial measure of any kind.
The young person must accept responsibility for the act or omission that forms the basis of the offence alleged.
The Crown must consent in writing to the EJS and authorize the specific program to be used.
The offence must be a summary conviction offence — extrajudicial sanctions are unavailable for indictable offences.
expl: Section 10(2) of the YCJA sets out the mandatory conditions that must be satisfied before an extrajudicial sanction (EJS) may be used. Section 10(2)(b) requires that the young person accept responsibility for the act or omission that forms the basis of the offence alleged. Other conditions under s. 10(2) include: (a) the sanction must be part of a program authorized by the Attorney General; (c) the young person must freely consent; (d) the young person must have had the opportunity to consult with counsel before consenting; and (f) the acceptance of responsibility is not admissible in evidence against the young person in any civil or criminal proceeding. Notably, EJS is not categorically barred for indictable offences — there is no offence-type restriction. A prior police caution does not automatically foreclose EJS, though it is a relevant factor.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 10(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Extrajudicial Sanctions — YCJA s. 10

text:
A court has determined that a wife is entitled to compensatory spousal support. In determining the amount and duration of support, the court must consider the statutory factors listed in the Divorce Act. The husband argues that only the wife's current financial need is relevant; the wife argues that the court must consider a broader set of factors.

Under DA s.15.2(4), which factors must a court consider in determining the amount and duration of a spousal support order?
opts: The court must consider only the financial needs of the claimant spouse.
The court must consider only the means of the payor spouse and the financial needs of the claimant.

Under DA s.15.2(4), the court must consider the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of each spouse, including: the length of cohabitation; the functions performed by each spouse during cohabitation; and any order, agreement, or arrangement relating to support of either spouse.
The court must first apply the SSAG formula and may not consider any factors beyond the SSAG calculation.
expl: Divorce Act s.15.2(4) requires the court to consider 'the condition, means, needs and other circumstances' of each spouse, and specifically directs the court to consider: (a) the length of time the spouses cohabited; (b) the functions performed by each spouse during cohabitation; and (c) any order, agreement, or arrangement relating to support of either spouse. These factors are broader than financial need alone. The SSAG are advisory tools that assist with quantum, but they do not replace the statutory factors in s.15.2(4). The court must engage with the statutory framework before (and in addition to) consulting the SSAG.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(4)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Spousal Support Factors — DA s.15.2(4)

text:
Priya earns $95,000 per year as a software engineer. She decides to leave her position and pursue painting full-time, earning approximately $8,000 per year. She and her former partner share two children, and he brings a child support variation application. Priya argues her income for Guidelines purposes is $8,000. Her former partner argues the court should impute income to her.

Under FCSG s.19(1), on what grounds may the court impute income to Priya, and what level of income may it impute?
opts: The court cannot impute income to a parent who has changed careers; career choices are a matter of personal autonomy and the court must accept actual income.

Under FCSG s.19(1)(a), the court may impute income where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed; if Priya's career change results in significantly lower income and is not appropriate given her education, experience, and the ability to earn more, the court may impute income at a level reflecting her notional earning capacity — typically the income she was earning or could reasonably earn.
The court must impute the maximum possible income to Priya regardless of her career decision.
Income imputation is only available for unemployed persons, not for those who are self-employed in a new career.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.19(1)(a) permits the court to impute income to a paying spouse who is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is 'reasonable' given the circumstances (such as illness, disability, or a reasonable career change that is not designed to avoid child support). Courts assess whether the career change is genuine and reasonable, considering the parent's education, skills, prior income, and the impact on the children. Priya's dramatic income reduction from $95,000 to $8,000 — a career choice she had the freedom to make personally — is subject to scrutiny if it appears connected to avoiding child support obligations. The court would typically impute income at or near Priya's previous earning level unless she can justify the change as reasonable and not motivated by avoiding support.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Imputing Income — FCSG s.19(1)(a)

text:
Margaret has sole decision-making responsibility for her two children and primary parenting time. She plans to move with the children from Toronto to Vancouver for a new job. She informs her children's father, Henri, of her plans two weeks before the intended move date. Henri objects and argues the notice was insufficient.

What are the notice requirements for relocation under the Divorce Act, and has Margaret complied?
opts: There is no statutory notice requirement for relocation under the Divorce Act; Margaret may move at any time without notice.

Under DA s.16.92, a person who plans to relocate with a child must provide 60 days' advance written notice to every other person with parenting time or decision-making responsibility; two weeks' notice is insufficient.
Notice of 30 days is sufficient; Margaret's two-week notice is slightly short but courts routinely overlook technical notice deficiencies.
The notice requirement only applies where the parent is moving to another country, not within Canada.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.16.92(1), a person who has parenting time or decision-making responsibility and who plans to relocate a child must give at least 60 days' written notice of the proposed relocation to every other person with parenting time, contact, or decision-making responsibility. The notice must include the date of the proposed relocation, the new address, and a proposed revised parenting schedule. Margaret's two-week notice falls far short of the mandatory 60-day requirement. The 60-day notice applies to relocations within Canada and internationally.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.92(1) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Relocation — Notice Requirements DA s.16.92

text:
Warren separated from his spouse three years ago. During the marriage, he incurred $80,000 in joint debts to cover the family's living expenses (mostly credit card and line of credit) that he is solely responsible for under their separation agreement. He pays $1,800/month on these debts. He earns $72,000. He argues the debt obligation creates undue hardship in paying the full table amount of child support of $1,300/month.

Does Warren's debt obligation to service marriage-related debts qualify as a listed circumstance under FCSG s.10(2)?
opts: No — debt obligations are not listed in s.10(2) and cannot ground an undue hardship claim.

Yes — under FCSG s.10(2)(b), unusually high debts reasonably incurred to support the family prior to separation or to earn a living are a listed s.10(2) circumstance that may ground an undue hardship claim; Warren must still satisfy the household standard of living comparison test.
Any debt, however incurred, qualifies as a listed circumstance under s.10(2).
Marriage-related debts are always the responsibility of the recipient of child support, not the payor.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.10(2)(b) lists 'unusually high debts reasonably incurred to support the family prior to separation or to earn a living' as a circumstance that may ground an undue hardship claim. Warren's $80,000 in debt incurred to support the family during the marriage, for which he is solely responsible, potentially qualifies under this provision. However, the analysis is two-step: (1) the s.10(2)(b) circumstance must be established; and (2) Warren's household standard of living must be lower than the recipient's. The debts must be 'unusually high' relative to his income — here, $80,000 in debt ($1,800/month payments on a $72,000 income) may qualify as unusually high. The second (household comparison) element still requires separate analysis.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 10(2)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Undue Hardship — Debt Obligations FCSG s.10(2)(b)

text:
A parenting order grants sole parenting time and decision-making to the mother. The father has no parenting time under the order, only supervised contact which has rarely occurred. The mother gives proper 60-day notice of her intention to relocate from Toronto to Calgary. The father objects. He argues the court must conduct a full best interests analysis weighing the relocation.

Under DA s.16.94, what is the applicable rule where the person opposing the relocation has no parenting time?
opts: Any person with any prior connection to the child may block a relocation by objecting; the father's objection must be fully heard.

Under DA s.16.94, where the person opposing the relocation has no parenting time under any order or agreement, the relocating parent may relocate without having to justify the relocation to the court; however, the non-parenting parent may apply to the court to prohibit the relocation.
Where the opposing parent has no parenting time, their objection is automatically dismissed and the relocating parent may proceed.
A parent with no parenting time has no standing to oppose a relocation; their objection cannot be heard.
expl: Divorce Act s.16.94 provides a special rule where the person opposing the relocation does not have parenting time under any court order or agreement: in that case, the relocating parent may proceed with the relocation without needing to first obtain court authorization or justify the move, but the opposing person retains the right to apply to the court to prohibit the relocation. This reflects the policy that a parent who is fully outside the children's day-to-day life should not have a de facto veto over the primary parent's mobility. The father may apply to court to block the relocation, but the burden analysis is different from where the opposing party has significant parenting time.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.94 (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Relocation — No Parenting Time — DA s.16.94

text:
A contested divorce application has been filed. The parties are disputing spousal support and parenting time. Trial is 14 months away. A junior lawyer advises the client that the court can only make support and parenting orders after the divorce is granted. A senior partner corrects this advice.

Under the Divorce Act, when may a court make orders for spousal support, child support, and parenting in a divorce proceeding?
opts: The court may only grant corollary relief orders after the divorce has taken effect; interim orders for support or parenting cannot be made while the marriage subsists.

Under the Divorce Act, a court may make interim orders for support and parenting at any stage of the divorce proceedings — including before the divorce itself is granted; the court may grant a divorce and corollary relief simultaneously, or grant corollary relief before or after the divorce.
Support and parenting orders can only be made once both parties have filed all their evidence for trial; no interim relief is available.
The court can make interim parenting orders before divorce but not interim support orders; support orders require the divorce to be granted first.
expl: The Divorce Act (ss.15.1, 15.2, 16) expressly permits the court to make interim and final orders for child support, spousal support, and parenting at any stage of the proceedings — before, during, or after the grant of the divorce. Interim orders under these sections are the standard mechanism for addressing immediate needs of the parties and children during the litigation period. The junior lawyer's advice is incorrect: the court has jurisdiction to make interim corollary relief orders while the divorce proceeding is pending, regardless of whether the divorce has been granted yet. The marriage subsisting is not a bar to an interim support or parenting order in divorce proceedings.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Interim Corollary Relief Orders — DA ss.15.1(2), 15.2(2), 16(1)

text:
A 13-year-old discloses to a school guidance counsellor that her mother's boyfriend has been sexually touching her for several months. The mother is unaware. The guidance counsellor consults with the principal and they decide to speak to the mother first before contacting CAS, believing involving the mother initially will be more helpful to the child.

What obligation does the school guidance counsellor have under CYFSA, and is the plan to speak to the mother first legally appropriate?
opts: The counsellor may appropriately consult with professionals before reporting to CAS; involving the mother first is a reasonable approach in the circumstances.

Under CYFSA s.125(1), the duty to report to CAS arises immediately upon reasonable grounds to suspect the child is in need of protection; the duty is personal and direct — it cannot be delegated or discharged by reporting to the mother or a supervisor first; the counsellor must report forthwith to CAS regardless of the consultation with the principal.
The duty to report applies only to health care professionals, not to school staff.
The counsellor should first obtain the child's written consent before contacting CAS, as the child is 13 and has the right to privacy.
expl: CYFSA s.125(1) imposes a mandatory personal duty on every person to report to a society (CAS) forthwith upon reasonable grounds to suspect that a child is or may be in need of protection. The child's disclosure of sexual abuse is clearly reasonable grounds to suspect a s.74(2)(b) (sexual abuse) or s.74(2)(c) (sexual exploitation) situation. The duty is personal — it cannot be discharged by reporting to a supervisor, principal, or parent first. CYFSA s.125(5) makes clear that professionals cannot delegate or defer the report. Speaking to the mother first (whose partner is the alleged abuser) is particularly problematic as it may compromise the child's safety and the integrity of any investigation. The counsellor and principal are both personally obligated to report.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 125(1), s. 125(5)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Duty to Report — CYFSA s.125 — Cannot Delegate

text:
A supervision order was made six months ago requiring a family to participate in parenting programs and addiction counselling. The parents have completed the required programs and the father has maintained sobriety. CAS acknowledges the family's progress. The parents apply for a review of the supervision order to have it terminated. CAS opposes, arguing the order cannot be reviewed until its expiry.

Under the CYFSA, may a child protection order be reviewed or varied before its expiry?
opts: Child protection orders cannot be reviewed until their expiry date; parties must wait until the order expires before applying for any change.

Under CYFSA s.116(6) and related provisions, a child protection order may be reviewed before its expiry by application of any party; the court may vary, terminate, or extend the order where circumstances warrant, applying the best interests of the child as the overriding consideration.
Only CAS may apply to review a protection order; parents have no standing to request a review.
Reviews of protection orders can only be initiated by the court on its own motion, not by application of any party.
expl: The CYFSA provides mechanisms for review of child protection orders before their expiry. Under CYFSA s.116(6) and the statutory review provisions, any party (including parents, CAS, or the child) may bring an application to review a protection order. The court may vary, extend, or terminate the order based on material change in circumstances and the child's best interests. A family's genuine participation in required programs and demonstrated progress — such as completed programs and sustained sobriety — is precisely the type of positive change that may warrant an early review and potential termination of a supervision order. Requiring parties to wait until expiry would be inconsistent with the child welfare principle of timeliness and least restrictive intervention.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 116(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Review of Protection Orders — CYFSA s.116(6)

text:
Two competing arguments are raised on behalf of common-law partners at separation. The first partner, Owen, argues that his contribution of $45,000 toward the purchase of a home registered in his partner Cynthia's name alone gives rise to a resulting trust — he paid and therefore retains a beneficial interest proportionate to his financial contribution. Cynthia argues that the better analysis is unjust enrichment and constructive trust, because over 10 years she contributed extensive domestic labour that equally benefited Owen.

What is the fundamental distinction between a resulting trust claim and a constructive trust (unjust enrichment) claim in this context?
opts: A resulting trust and a constructive trust are the same remedy and produce identical outcomes in all cases.
A resulting trust arises from the express agreement of the parties to hold property on trust; a constructive trust arises from statute.

A resulting trust arises from a gratuitous transfer where the transferor retains the beneficial interest proportionate to the financial contribution made at the time of acquisition; it is based on the intent inferred at the moment of transfer; a constructive trust arises from the court's remedy for unjust enrichment, which may take into account a broader range of contributions (including labour) over the entire relationship to determine the proportionate interest.
A resulting trust requires proof of all three elements of unjust enrichment; a constructive trust does not.
expl: A resulting trust and a constructive trust are distinct equitable doctrines. A resulting trust arises where property is transferred to one party who did not provide full consideration — the law presumes the beneficial interest results back to the transferor in proportion to their financial contribution at the time of acquisition. The focus is on the initial transfer and the financial contribution made at that moment. A constructive trust, by contrast, is the remedy imposed by a court to address unjust enrichment — it can take into account a broader range of contributions over the entire relationship (financial and non-financial, including domestic labour) and is not limited to the proportion of the initial purchase price. Owen's resulting trust claim is based solely on his $45,000 initial contribution; Cynthia's constructive trust argument encompasses the full scope of contributions over 10 years.
caseRef: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Resulting Trust vs. Constructive Trust — Distinct Doctrines

text:
A teacher reports her suspicion that a child in her class is being physically abused at home. The report is made in good faith. After CAS investigates, the family's lawyer demands to know who made the report, threatening to sue the teacher for defamation and breach of privacy if the reporter's identity is revealed. The teacher is worried about the consequences of having made the report.

Under the CYFSA, is a person who makes a report to CAS in good faith protected from civil liability, and is their identity kept confidential?
opts: Reporters have no protection from civil liability; they may be sued for defamation if the report turns out to be unfounded.

Under CYFSA s.125(6), a person who makes a report to CAS in good faith is protected from civil liability (including defamation suits) for making the report; CYFSA s.125(8) provides that the reporter's identity is protected and shall not be disclosed without their consent, subject to narrow exceptions.
Reporter identity is only protected in anonymous reports; the teacher's identity is automatically disclosed because she is a named professional.
Protection from liability applies only to health professionals, not to teachers.
expl: CYFSA s.125(6) provides statutory protection from civil liability for reporters who make a report under s.125 in good faith. A person who makes a bona fide report — even if the investigation does not substantiate the concern — cannot be sued for defamation, breach of privacy, or other civil wrongs arising from the report. CYFSA s.125(8) protects the reporter's identity: CAS shall not disclose the identity of a person who made a report to the Society without the reporter's consent. These protections are essential to encouraging reporting and are a foundational feature of the mandatory reporting regime. Narrow exceptions may allow disclosure in court proceedings where it is necessary in the interests of justice.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 125(6), s. 125(8)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Reporter Immunity — CYFSA s.125(6) and s.125(8)

text:
Theo lost his job four years ago and his income dropped from $75,000 to $22,000. He did not bring a variation application at the time, hoping to find equivalent employment. He recently found new employment at $65,000. His former spouse April now applies to increase child support based on his new income. Theo counter-claims for a retroactive reduction for the four years of reduced income. April argues the retroactive reduction should not be granted.

Under the DBS v SRG framework, is a retroactive reduction of child support available where a payor had lower income but delayed bringing a variation application?
opts: Retroactive reductions are always available for any period of lower income; Theo automatically receives a four-year retroactive reduction.

Under DBS v SRG, a retroactive reduction is available for a payor who had reduced income; the court applies the four DBS factors in reverse (delay, recipient's conduct, child's circumstances, hardship from retroactivity); Theo's four-year delay is a significant negative factor; courts may grant a partial retroactive reduction but are unlikely to grant the full four years given the length of the delay and the fact that April and the children relied on the higher support.
Retroactive reductions are never available; child support may only be varied prospectively.
A payor who finds new employment cannot seek any retroactive reduction for the period of lower income; the claim is barred.
expl: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court confirmed that retroactive relief can be sought by payors as well as recipients. The same four-factor framework applies: (1) the payor's reason for the delay in bringing the application; (2) the recipient's conduct (did April benefit from the higher support in a way that cannot be undone?); (3) the circumstances of the children; and (4) hardship to the recipient from a retroactive reduction. Theo's four-year delay is a significant negative factor — payors are expected to bring variation applications promptly when circumstances change. Courts may grant a partial retroactive reduction (e.g., beginning one year before the formal application) but are unlikely to grant the full four years given the detrimental reliance of the recipient and children on the support payments received.
caseRef: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG Payor Reduction Claim

text:
Bianca and Stefan are in contested divorce proceedings that are expected to take 18 months to resolve. Bianca left her part-time employment to care for their children during the marriage. She has few immediate resources. She brings a motion for interim spousal support pending the trial. Stefan argues that interim support should not be granted because Bianca has not yet established her full entitlement at trial.

What is the legal standard governing interim spousal support in divorce proceedings?
opts: Interim spousal support cannot be granted before trial; entitlement must be fully established at a final hearing first.

Interim spousal support on a divorce application is governed by a summary assessment of the evidence; the court does not conduct a full assessment of entitlement but looks at the apparent or prima facie entitlement and immediate need; the purpose is to prevent serious hardship during the period before final determination.
Interim spousal support is granted as of right whenever the parties have been separated for more than six months.
Interim support is set at exactly 50% of the SSAG range and is not subject to the court's discretion.
expl: Interim spousal support orders are available under the Divorce Act (and FLA for non-divorce proceedings) pending the final determination of the application. Courts assess interim support on a summary basis — without a full trial — looking at: (1) an apparent or prima facie entitlement based on the available evidence; (2) the immediate financial needs of the claimant and the ability of the other party to pay; and (3) the need to avoid undue hardship pending the final order. The SSAG may be used as a reference point for quantum. Interim orders are explicitly understood to be temporary and may be adjusted at trial; the court does not resolve all disputed facts at the interim stage.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2; Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Interim Spousal Support in Divorce Proceedings

text:
A parenting order was made five years ago when the child was 8, granting primary residence to the mother. The child is now 13 and clearly expresses a strong preference to live primarily with the father. The father applies to vary the parenting order. The mother argues the child's changed preferences are not a material change under Gordon v Goertz.

Can a child's maturing preference — as the child ages from 8 to 13 — constitute a material change justifying variation of a parenting order?
opts: A child's changing preferences can never constitute a material change; only external circumstances (such as a parent moving or remarrying) qualify.

Under Gordon v Goertz and DA s.16(3)(e), a child's maturing preference — particularly where the child has matured significantly and can articulate clear, considered reasons for a preference — may constitute a material change in circumstances if the strength and basis of the preference were not reasonably foreseeable when the original order was made; courts give increasing weight to the views of older, more mature children.
A child's expressed preference is automatically dispositive at age 13; the court must follow it.
A child's preferences can only be raised at a full review after 10 years, not through a variation application.
expl: Courts have recognized that a child's maturing preferences — especially where the child was quite young (8) at the time of the original order and is now a teenager (13) — can constitute a material change of circumstances for Gordon v Goertz purposes. The relevant question is whether the strength and nature of the child's current preference was reasonably foreseeable at the time of the original order. A young child's parenting arrangement made at age 8 may not have fully anticipated what the child's preferences would be at 13 with the capacity to articulate nuanced reasons. However, the child's preference is not determinative even at 13 — DA s.16(3)(e) requires it to be given 'due weight' consistent with age and maturity, but the court still balances all best interests factors before making any change.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(3)(e), s. 17; Gordon v Goertz, [1996] 2 SCR 27
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Child's Maturing Preference — Material Change Variation

text:
After 4 years of cohabitation, Michelle qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 and applies for spousal support. The court must determine which support entitlement grounds apply. Dan's counsel argues that common-law spousal support is limited to economic hardship caused by the separation and cannot be based on compensatory grounds (i.e., compensation for career sacrifices or role-merger during the relationship).

What entitlement grounds apply to a qualifying common-law spousal support claim under the FLA, and is compensatory entitlement available?
opts: Common-law spousal support under the FLA is limited to needs-based (non-compensatory) grounds only; compensatory entitlement is available only under the Divorce Act.

FLA spousal support entitlement for qualifying common-law partners is assessed on the same grounds as for married spouses — compensatory, non-compensatory (needs-based), and contractual entitlement grounds all apply; a qualifying common-law spouse who sacrificed career advancement to support the family relationship may establish compensatory entitlement just as a married spouse would.
Common-law spousal support under the FLA is always compensatory only; needs-based entitlement is not available.
The FLA provides no independent entitlement analysis; courts simply apply SSAG without first determining entitlement for common-law partners.
expl: Once a person qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29, their spousal support claim is assessed under Part III of the FLA on the same substantive basis as a married spouse's claim. The FLA s.33 support obligation and the factors and objectives in ss.33(8) and (9) apply fully to qualifying common-law spouses. Entitlement may be established on compensatory grounds (where the applicant suffered economic disadvantage from the relationship, such as career sacrifice to raise children or support the other's career), non-compensatory grounds (where the applicant has needs and the respondent has the means to contribute), or contractual grounds. Dan's counsel's argument that compensatory entitlement is only available under the Divorce Act is incorrect.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, ss. 29, 33(8), 33(9)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | FLA Spousal Support — Entitlement Grounds for Common-Law Partners

text:
In contested parenting proceedings, credible evidence is adduced that the father engaged in a pattern of coercive and controlling behaviour toward the mother during the marriage, including monitoring her communications, controlling finances, and making threats. The father was never criminally charged. He argues the absence of physical violence and criminal charges means the court cannot treat these behaviours as family violence for parenting purposes.

Under DA s.16(3)(j), what specific factors does the court consider when assessing family violence, and does the absence of physical violence or criminal charges limit the court's analysis?
opts: Family violence is limited to physical assault; coercive control and financial abuse are not forms of family violence under the Divorce Act.

Under DA s.16(3)(j), family violence includes any conduct that is violent or threatening or that constitutes a pattern of coercive and controlling behaviour or causes the other person to fear for their safety; the court considers the nature, seriousness, frequency, and impact of the violence; physical violence and criminal charges are not prerequisites.
The court can only consider family violence if there is a criminal conviction; absent a conviction, the civil court must disregard allegations of abuse.
Coercive control is relevant only as a factor in spousal support, not in parenting proceedings.
expl: Divorce Act s.16(3)(j) defines 'family violence' broadly to include any conduct — whether or not criminally prosecuted — that is violent, threatening, constitutes a pattern of coercive and controlling behaviour, or causes the other person to fear for their safety or that of another person. The definition expressly includes financial control, emotional abuse, and psychological abuse. Under s.16(4), when assessing family violence, the court must consider specific factors: the nature, seriousness, and frequency of the violence; whether there is a pattern; the physical, emotional, and psychological harm caused; and any steps the perpetrator has taken to address the behaviour. The civil standard of proof (balance of probabilities) applies; a criminal conviction is not required.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(3)(j), s. 16(4) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Family Violence Assessment — DA s.16(3)(j) and s.16(4)

text:
A spousal support order was made under the Divorce Act as corollary relief in a divorce judgment. Three years later, the recipient seeks to increase the support, arguing there has been a material change in circumstances. She files an application under the Family Law Act rather than under the Divorce Act. The payor argues she must proceed under the Divorce Act to vary a Divorce Act order.

Which statute governs the variation of a spousal support order that was made as corollary relief under the Divorce Act?
opts: The recipient may proceed under either the Divorce Act or the Family Law Act, at her election, to vary a corollary relief order.
The Family Law Act governs all post-separation support variations in Ontario regardless of which statute the original order was made under.

Variation of a corollary relief order made under the Divorce Act is governed by Divorce Act s.17, not the Family Law Act; the application must be brought under the DA; DA s.17 requires proof of a material change in the condition, means, needs, or other circumstances of either former spouse since the original order.
No variation of a corollary relief order is possible; the original order is final and binding.
expl: Divorce Act s.17 expressly governs the variation of corollary relief orders made under the Divorce Act — including spousal support orders under s.15.2, child support orders under s.15.1, and parenting orders under s.16. A party seeking to vary a DA corollary relief order must bring the application under DA s.17 and must prove a material change in the condition, means, needs, or other circumstances of either former spouse (for support orders) or the children (for parenting orders) since the making of the original order. The FLA cannot be used to vary a DA order. This is a critical procedural point for practising family lawyers: use the same statute as the original order for variation.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Variation of Corollary Relief — DA s.17

text:
At the time of the original spousal support order, Nora was employed. Unknown to the court — and concealed by Nora — she had already been diagnosed with a serious illness that would affect her ability to work. Two years later, her health deteriorated significantly and she now seeks a variation to increase support, relying on the illness as the material change. The payor argues the illness cannot be a material change because it existed at the time of the original order.

Under the Willick standard for material change, must the alleged change have occurred after the original order was made?
opts: The material change may have existed before the original order, provided the court was not aware of it at the time.

Under Willick v. Willick, [1994] 3 SCR 670, a material change must be one that: (a) could not have been reasonably anticipated at the time of the original order; and (b) if known at the time, would have led to a different order; a condition that existed at the time of the original order and was concealed by a party is not a new material change — the proper remedy may be to set aside the original order for non-disclosure, not variation.
Any change after the original order, however foreseeable, constitutes a material change and automatically entitles the applicant to variation.
The Willick standard applies only to parenting orders, not to spousal support orders.
expl: In Willick v. Willick, [1994] 3 SCR 670 and Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that a material change for variation purposes must be a change that: (1) was not within the reasonable contemplation of the parties at the time of the original order; and (2) if known at the time, would likely have led to a different order. Where the alleged 'change' — here, the illness — actually existed at the time of the original order but was not disclosed, it does not constitute a new post-order change. The appropriate remedy for the payor would be to seek to set aside the original order on the ground of material non-disclosure, not to resist a variation. If Nora concealed the illness, the original order may be vulnerable to being set aside entirely.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Willick v. Willick, [1994] 3 SCR 670; Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Material Change — Willick Standard

text:
A physician in a hospital has reasonable grounds to suspect a child patient has been physically abused. She informs the hospital's social work department and assumes they will make the required report to CAS. She does not personally contact CAS. The hospital's social worker also fails to report. Neither report is made for two weeks. The question arises whether the physician has discharged her duty to report.

Under CYFSA s.125, has the physician discharged her mandatory reporting obligation by informing the hospital's social work department?
opts: Yes — a physician may delegate the reporting obligation to the hospital's social work department, which is designated to handle such reports.

No — under CYFSA s.125(5), the duty to report is personal and non-delegable; no professional or occupational duty or privilege supersedes the duty to report; the physician cannot discharge her personal obligation by reporting to a colleague or department instead of to CAS directly; she remains personally liable for the failure to report.
Yes — informing a supervisor or social work team is an acceptable method of fulfilling the duty to report.
The physician's obligation is only to form a reasonable suspicion; once she informed the social work team, her obligation was fulfilled.
expl: CYFSA s.125(5) expressly provides that the duty to report is personal and non-delegable: no professional or occupational duty or any duty of confidentiality to which a person is subject overrides the duty to report. Critically, a professional cannot discharge the reporting obligation by informing a supervisor, employer, social work department, or colleague — each person with reasonable grounds must personally report directly to CAS. The physician's notification to the hospital social work department does not satisfy her personal obligation. She remains personally liable for the failure to report and may face penalties under the CYFSA. This is one of the most practically important features of the mandatory reporting regime for professionals.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 125(5)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Non-Delegable Duty to Report — CYFSA s.125(5)

text:
A child is 16. The parents divorced when the child was 5 and no child support order was ever made. The custodial parent now applies under the Divorce Act for a child support order. The other parent argues the claim is statute-barred because it has been more than 11 years since the divorce, and Ontario's general limitation period of two years has long expired.

Is a first-time application for child support under the Divorce Act subject to a limitation period that would bar the claim?
opts: Yes — the general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars the claim; 11 years is far beyond the limitation period.

No — under the Divorce Act, there is no limitation period that bars an initial application for child support; a court may make a child support order at any time; child support is the right of the child and parental delay cannot extinguish it.
Yes — applications for child support under the Divorce Act must be brought within six years of the divorce.
No — but only if the parent is claiming on behalf of an Indigenous child; limitation periods on child support are waived for Indigenous children only.
expl: There is no limitation period under the Divorce Act that bars an initial application for child support. The Divorce Act (DA ss.15.1) confers jurisdiction on a court to make a child support order at any time during the marriage, upon divorce, or afterward. Unlike spousal support (where the two-year limitation under the Limitations Act, 2002 may apply to delay), child support is treated differently because it belongs to the child — not the parent. A parent's delay in applying does not extinguish the child's right to support. Courts may consider retroactivity separately (using the DBS v SRG framework), but the claim itself is not time-barred. The two-year Limitations Act, 2002 period does not apply to override the DA's jurisdiction to grant child support.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.1; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Child Support Limitation — No Bar Under Divorce Act

text:
Daniel and Sarah separated two months ago. No parenting order has been made. Daniel has been caring for the children approximately equally. Sarah now gives 60-day notice of her intention to relocate with the children from Hamilton to Halifax. Daniel objects. They go to court on the relocation issue. The question is who bears the burden of proof.

Under Divorce Act s.16.93, who bears the burden of proof where there is no existing parenting order when a parent seeks to relocate with the children?
opts: The parent opposing the relocation always bears the burden of proving relocation is not in the child's best interests.

Under DA s.16.93(1), where there is no existing parenting order (or where parenting time is approximately equal), the burden falls on the relocating parent to establish that the proposed relocation is in the child's best interests.
There is no burden of proof in relocation proceedings; the court conducts a neutral inquiry.
Where there is no existing order, the relocating parent has an automatic right to move; the objecting parent bears the burden.
expl: Divorce Act s.16.93(1) provides that where the parties have substantially equal parenting time — or where there is no existing order — the relocating parent bears the burden of establishing that the proposed relocation is in the child's best interests. This contrasts with DA s.16.93(2), which shifts the burden to the objecting parent where the relocating parent has the majority of parenting time. On these facts, Sarah and Daniel have approximately equal (de facto 50/50) care since separation, and there is no formal order. Therefore, Sarah as the relocating parent bears the burden of proving the relocation to Halifax serves the children's best interests under the Barendregt framework.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.93(1) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Relocation — Burden of Proof DA s.16.93(1)

text:
A society wardship order was made nine months ago for a 7-year-old child. The parents have since completed all required programs, maintained stable housing, and addressed the concerns that led to the initial order. CAS acknowledges the progress but opposes a status review, arguing it is too soon. The parents want to apply for a review to terminate the wardship and return the child.

Under the CYFSA, can the parents apply for a status review of the society wardship order, and on what grounds?
opts: Only CAS may apply for a status review; parents have no standing to bring a status review application.

Under the CYFSA, any party to the child protection proceeding — including the parents — may apply for a status review; the court may vary, terminate, or extend the existing protection order if circumstances have changed materially since the order was made; the parents' demonstrated progress may constitute changed circumstances warranting a review.
A status review can only be brought after a protection order has been in place for a minimum of two years.
Status reviews are only available for Crown wardship orders, not society wardship orders.
expl: The CYFSA provides for status reviews of protection orders. Any party to the proceedings — including parents, CAS, the child, or an Indigenous community — may bring a status review application. The court conducts a review to determine whether the order should continue, be varied, or be terminated. The standard is the child's best interests in light of the current circumstances. Parents who have made genuine progress — completing programs, maintaining stable housing, addressing the identified protective concerns — may bring a status review to demonstrate that continued wardship is no longer necessary or that a less restrictive order is appropriate. There is no mandatory minimum waiting period before a status review may be brought.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 116(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Status Review — CYFSA Any Party May Apply

text:
Lisa and Dan cohabited for 7 years and meet the FLA s.29 definition of spouses. On separation, Lisa seeks spousal support from Dan. Dan's counsel argues that the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) apply only to married couples proceeding under the Divorce Act and cannot be used to calculate support for common-law partners under the FLA.

Do the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) apply to spousal support claims by common-law partners under the Family Law Act?
opts: No — the SSAG are only applicable under the Divorce Act and cannot be used in FLA support proceedings for common-law partners.

Yes — the SSAG apply to spousal support claims under both the Divorce Act (for married spouses) and the Family Law Act (for qualifying common-law partners); courts routinely apply the SSAG ranges to FLA support claims and departing from SSAG ranges requires justification.
Yes — but for common-law partners the SSAG ranges are automatically reduced by 50% to account for the absence of formal marriage.
No — common-law partners must apply their own provincial SSAG formula developed exclusively for Ontario FLA claims.
expl: The Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) were designed to apply to spousal support claims under both the Divorce Act and provincial support legislation such as the Family Law Act. The SSAG preface and the case law confirm that the guidelines apply to qualifying common-law partners who meet the provincial 'spouse' definition with the same force as they apply to married spouses. Ontario courts regularly use SSAG formulas — both the 'without child support' and 'with child support' formulas — to determine the range of appropriate support amounts and duration for FLA claims. There is no discounting of the SSAG ranges for common-law status; the qualifying threshold of being a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 is what matters.
caseRef: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, updated 2016); Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | SSAG Apply to Common-Law Spousal Support

text:
After a high-conflict divorce, a judge orders parenting coordination for the parties under the Family Law Rules and Ontario's parenting coordination framework. The parents frequently disagree on scheduling and extracurricular activities. The parenting coordinator (PC) is a mental health professional with experience in family conflict. One parent argues the PC has no authority to make binding decisions and can only make recommendations.

What is the role of a parenting coordinator in Ontario, and can a PC make binding decisions?
opts: A parenting coordinator is a mediator only; they can never make binding decisions and all decisions require court approval.

A parenting coordinator in Ontario fulfils a hybrid role: first attempting to assist parents to resolve disputes through a mediation-like process; and where that fails, the PC may make binding decisions on day-to-day parenting disputes within the scope authorized by the court order or the parties' agreement, subject to the right of either party to apply to court to review the PC's decision.
A parenting coordinator has unlimited authority to make binding decisions on all parenting matters, including changing primary parenting time.
Parenting coordinators are only permitted in Ontario if both parties consent in writing and a judge specifically approves the appointment.
expl: Parenting coordination in Ontario is a dispute resolution process designed for high-conflict families. A parenting coordinator performs two functions: (1) facilitative — assisting the parents to resolve parenting disputes through a mediation-like process; and (2) determinative — where the parties cannot agree, making a decision on the specific dispute within the scope of authority granted by the court order or parenting coordination agreement. PC decisions are binding on the parties but are subject to court review on application by either party. PC authority is limited to the scope set out in the court order or agreement — major decisions (changing primary parenting arrangements) typically remain with the court.
caseRef: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 17(8.1)-(8.2); Ontario Parenting Coordination Guidelines
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Parenting Coordination — Role and Binding Decisions

text:
Before signing a separation agreement, both parties exchanged financial statements. William disclosed his home and bank accounts but did not disclose a significant outstanding debt — a personal guarantee for $300,000 on a business loan — despite being asked about liabilities. His spouse Sandra signed the agreement not knowing about the guarantee. The equalization payment in the agreement heavily favoured William. Sandra later discovers the undisclosed guarantee and seeks to set aside the agreement.

Under FLA s.56(4)(a), what is the legal significance of William's failure to disclose the $300,000 guarantee, and how does it affect the separation agreement?
opts: Non-disclosure of debts is not a ground for setting aside under FLA s.56(4)(a); only non-disclosure of assets is covered.

Under FLA s.56(4)(a), a court may set aside a domestic contract where a party failed to disclose significant assets or significant debts or other liabilities existing when the domestic contract was made; a $300,000 personal guarantee is a significant liability, and its non-disclosure despite a direct inquiry constitutes a strong ground for setting aside the agreement.
Non-disclosure of a contingent liability (a guarantee) is not sufficient to set aside a separation agreement because the guarantee may never be called.
The agreement cannot be set aside because Sandra had the opportunity to conduct independent inquiry and obtain her own financial information.
expl: Family Law Act s.56(4)(a) provides that a court may set aside a domestic contract or a provision where a party 'failed to disclose to the other significant assets or significant debts or other liabilities existing when the domestic contract was made.' The provision expressly covers both assets and debts/liabilities — not only assets. A personal guarantee of $300,000 is a 'significant liability' within the meaning of s.56(4)(a), particularly where it was not disclosed despite a direct inquiry about liabilities. The non-disclosure of this significant liability is a clear statutory ground for seeking to set aside the agreement. The court has discretion and will consider all circumstances, including the impact on the equalization result had the guarantee been disclosed.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Setting Aside — Non-Disclosure of Significant Debts FLA s.56(4)(a)

text:
Julia and Michael entered into a cohabitation agreement two years ago before moving in together. The agreement addressed property division and support in the event their relationship ended. They have since decided to marry. Julia's lawyer advises her that the cohabitation agreement automatically becomes void on marriage and must be renegotiated. Michael's lawyer disagrees.

Under the Family Law Act, what is the effect of marriage on a pre-existing cohabitation agreement?
opts: A cohabitation agreement is automatically void upon marriage; the parties must enter into a new marriage contract to have any contractual protections.
Under FLA s.53, a cohabitation agreement entered into by persons who subsequently marry does not become void on the marriage; it is deemed to be a marriage contract.
A cohabitation agreement survives marriage unchanged but loses its contractual force; it becomes only advisory.
A cohabitation agreement converts to a separation agreement upon marriage, applicable only if they subsequently separate.
expl: Family Law Act s.53(2) provides that if persons who have entered into a cohabitation agreement marry each other, the agreement is deemed to be a marriage contract. This automatic conversion means that upon marriage, the cohabitation agreement takes on the legal status and effect of a marriage contract, without the need for renegotiation or a new document. Julia's lawyer's advice that the agreement becomes void is incorrect. The parties should review the terms to confirm they remain appropriate and obtain legal advice, but the existing agreement is legally preserved by s.53(2).
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 53(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Cohabitation Agreement Converting to Marriage Contract — FLA s.53(2)

text:
Lucy and David separated in March 2021. Lucy did not apply for spousal support at the time. She also did not apply for child support for their three children, relying instead on informal arrangements. By March 2026, she consults a lawyer about both claims. The lawyer must advise whether both claims may be out of time.

What is the limitation period for a spousal support claim under the FLA compared to a child support claim under the Divorce Act or FCSG?
opts: Both spousal support and child support claims under the FLA are subject to the same two-year limitation period with no exceptions.

Spousal support under the FLA is subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation from discovery; however, child support claims benefit from special protection — under the FCSG, no limitation period restricts the court from making an initial child support order at any time, as the child's right to support is paramount and may not be waived by the delay of a parent.
Both spousal support and child support claims have no limitation period whatsoever; they may be brought at any time.
Child support is subject to a six-year limitation period; spousal support has no limitation period.
expl: The limitation period framework for support claims in family law is asymmetric. Spousal support claims under FLA Part III are subject to the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period from discovery. A claim to obtain an initial spousal support order may be time-barred if the claimant unreasonably delayed bringing the application. Child support, by contrast, is treated differently: courts have consistently held that delay by a custodial parent in seeking child support does not disentitle the child from support. Child support belongs to the child, not the parent, and a parent's failure to apply promptly does not bar an initial child support order. Lucy's child support claim can still be made; her spousal support claim faces a more serious limitation argument, depending on when her claim was discovered.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4; Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175
