lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Limitation Period — Spousal Support vs Child Support

text:
In divorce proceedings, Michel seeks spousal support and child support. His spouse Clara has not provided complete financial disclosure. Michel's lawyer advises that the Divorce Act imposes a mandatory financial disclosure obligation. Clara argues there is no obligation to disclose financial information unless specifically ordered by the court.

What is the financial disclosure obligation in divorce proceedings under the Divorce Act and the Family Law Rules, and what are the consequences of non-disclosure?
opts: Financial disclosure in divorce proceedings is purely voluntary; courts may only compel disclosure upon a specific motion by the other party.

Under the Divorce Act and Family Law Rules, parties to divorce proceedings involving corollary relief (support or parenting) have a mandatory obligation to provide complete financial disclosure; failure to disclose may result in adverse inferences, cost sanctions, and the court drawing negative conclusions about the non-disclosing party's financial position.
Financial disclosure is required only if the court makes an explicit order; there is no standing obligation on parties.
Financial disclosure is required only for the payor of support, not for the recipient.
expl: Financial disclosure is a foundational obligation in divorce proceedings involving corollary relief. The Family Law Rules (r. 13) require parties to serve and file a financial statement in prescribed form when seeking or responding to claims for support or property. The Divorce Act and the Federal Child Support Guidelines (s.21) impose specific obligations to provide annual income information. Failure to comply can result in the court drawing adverse inferences against the non-disclosing party, imposing costs sanctions, making orders on the best available information, or striking pleadings in egregious cases. Full and frank financial disclosure is a mandatory, ongoing obligation — not a matter of choice or court discretion.
caseRef: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 13; Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 21; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Financial Disclosure in Divorce Proceedings

text:
Elena and Marco separated five years ago. A child support order was made at that time based on Marco's income of $80,000. Marco's income increased to $130,000 three years ago but he never disclosed this to Elena or the court. Elena discovered the income increase six months ago and now seeks retroactive child support for the past four years. Marco argues that retroactive support should not go back more than one year.

Under the DBS v SRG framework, to what extent may Elena's retroactive child support claim extend given Marco's non-disclosure?
opts: Retroactive child support cannot go back more than one year in any case.

Under DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the general presumptive period for retroactive child support is three years before the date of the formal claim; however, where the payor engaged in blameworthy conduct — specifically, failure to disclose income increases — the court may extend the retroactive period beyond three years; Marco's four-year non-disclosure is blameworthy conduct that may justify going back further than the three-year presumption.
Retroactive support automatically covers the full period of underpayment regardless of any time limit.
Non-disclosure of income increases is not blameworthy conduct for DBS purposes; Marco must have actively lied about income for the extended period to apply.
expl: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court of Canada held that retroactive child support may be awarded and established a general presumptive starting point: retroactive support should not normally go back more than three years before the date of the formal claim. However, where the payor engaged in 'blameworthy conduct' — which expressly includes failing to disclose income increases, as payors are under a positive obligation to do so — the court may award retroactive support beyond the three-year presumptive period. Marco's failure to disclose a $50,000 income increase for four years is blameworthy conduct, potentially justifying retroactive support going back the full four years to when the increase occurred. The retroactive award is not punitive; it is a recognition of what should have been paid.
caseRef: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG Four Factors

text:
Helen and George were married for 6 years and have now separated. During the marriage, George treated Helen's 8-year-old daughter from a previous relationship as his own child — paying for her school activities, taking her to appointments, and acting as a parental figure. George has no biological children. Helen now seeks child support from George for her daughter under the Divorce Act. George argues he has no legal obligation to pay support because he is not the child's biological father.

Under what legal basis might George be obligated to pay child support for Helen's daughter in a Divorce Act proceeding, and what standard governs?
opts: George has no obligation; only biological parents or legal adoptive parents are required to pay child support.

Under FCSG s.5 and Divorce Act s.2(2), a step-parent who stands in the place of a parent may have an obligation to pay child support; the amount is determined by the court considering all relevant circumstances, as the table amount may not reflect the appropriate level of step-parent support given that the biological parent may also have an obligation.
A step-parent is automatically equally liable for child support as the biological parent, and George must pay the full table amount.
George's child support obligation arises only if he formally adopted the child.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.2(2), a person who stands in the place of a parent (in loco parentis) has obligations similar to those of a parent during the period they occupy that role. Federal Child Support Guidelines s.5 specifically addresses step-parent child support: where the 'spouse' (in the DA context, a person who stands in place of a parent) is obligated to pay support, the court may award an amount that the court considers appropriate having regard to the Guidelines and any other parent's legal duty to support the child. The court balances the step-parent's resources against the biological parent's obligation, often resulting in a lesser amount than the full table amount. Six years of acting as a de facto parent supports a finding that George stands in the place of a parent.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 5; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Step-Parent Child Support — FCSG s.5 and DA s.2(2)

text:
Arthur transfers a condominium unit into joint registration with his partner Vera during their 5-year common-law relationship, without any written agreement. When they separate, Vera claims she owns a 50% interest as a gift; Arthur claims the transfer was made for administrative convenience and he intended to retain the full beneficial interest. The question is which presumption applies.

What presumption applies to Arthur's gratuitous transfer of an interest in the condominium to Vera, and who bears the burden of rebutting it?
opts: The presumption of advancement applies; Vera is presumed to have received a gift; Arthur must prove otherwise.

Because Arthur and Vera are not married, the presumption of resulting trust applies to the gratuitous transfer; the law presumes Arthur did not intend to gift the beneficial interest; Vera bears the burden of proving Arthur intended a gift, which may be rebutted by evidence of donative intent.
No presumption applies between common-law partners; the court decides on a balance of probabilities without any default presumption.
The presumption of advancement applies after two years of cohabitation, converting the resulting trust presumption.
expl: The presumption of advancement (gift) applies between married spouses and from parent to child — where a gratuitous transfer is made between these parties, the law presumes it was intended as a gift and the transferor must rebut that presumption. For transfers between persons who are not in these relationships — including common-law partners — the presumption of resulting trust applies. As confirmed in Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795, the presumption of resulting trust means the recipient holds the transferred property on trust for the transferor; the recipient (Vera) must prove donative intent to keep the property. The length of cohabitation does not change the applicable presumption.
caseRef: Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Presumption of Resulting Trust — Common-Law vs. Married Spouses

text:
Phillip secretly encouraged his wife Vera to begin an affair with a colleague by arranging for them to meet repeatedly and providing Vera with opportunities and privacy to be alone with the colleague. Vera did have the affair. Phillip now seeks a divorce on the ground of adultery.

What doctrine, if any, bars Phillip's divorce application on the ground of Vera's adultery?
opts: Collusion — because Phillip arranged for the affair, constituting a fabrication of the divorce ground.
Condonation — because Phillip forgave Vera's adultery by continuing to live with her after learning of it.

Connivance under DA s.11(1)(b) — Phillip actively encouraged or wilfully allowed Vera's adultery, which bars him from using that adultery as the ground for divorce; the court must take connivance into account and determine whether it would be in the public interest to proceed.
No bar applies — the adultery is real and Phillip may rely on it regardless of how it came about.
expl: Connivance under Divorce Act s.11(1)(b) refers to conduct by the petitioner that facilitates or encourages the respondent's adultery — such as wilfully allowing or setting up opportunities for the adultery to occur. Unlike collusion (fabrication of the ground), connivance involves actual adultery that was facilitated by the petitioning spouse. Phillip's deliberate arrangement of meetings and privacy to encourage the affair constitutes connivance. The court must take this into account under s.11(1)(b) and apply s.11(2) to determine whether it would be in the public interest to grant the divorce notwithstanding the connivance.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 11(1)(b), s. 11(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Bars to Divorce — Connivance DA s.11(1)(b)

text:
Cheryl lives in Ontario with her two children and has a child support order requiring her former spouse Daniel to pay $1,400/month. Daniel has moved to British Columbia and claims he is no longer subject to the Ontario order. Cheryl's lawyer must advise her on how to enforce the Ontario order against Daniel in British Columbia.

How does Ontario's interjurisdictional support enforcement framework address the situation where a payor has moved to another province?
opts: The Ontario order becomes unenforceable once Daniel leaves Ontario; Cheryl must obtain a new order from the BC courts.

Under Ontario's Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act) and the corresponding BC legislation, a support order made in Ontario may be registered and enforced in BC without the need to re-litigate the support; the Family Responsibility Office (FRO) in Ontario may coordinate enforcement with the BC reciprocating authority.
Cheryl must personally travel to BC and commence a new child support proceeding there.
An Ontario support order is automatically void once the payor leaves Ontario; it has no legal effect in another province.
expl: Ontario's Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act), S.O. 2002, c. 13, provides a mechanism for registering and enforcing Ontario support orders in other Canadian provinces (and some foreign countries). Ontario has reciprocal enforcement arrangements with all other Canadian provinces. Cheryl can register the Ontario order in British Columbia through the ISO Act mechanism, which allows BC authorities to enforce the order as if it were a BC order. The Family Responsibility Office (FRO) in Ontario coordinates enforcement and can work with its BC counterpart. Daniel cannot escape the Ontario order merely by crossing a provincial border. No new BC proceeding or fresh child support determination is required for enforcement of the existing order.
caseRef: Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 13; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Interjurisdictional Support — ISO Act

text:
Derek earns $75,000/year and has two children from his current marriage who live with him. The Federal Child Support Guidelines table amount for his two children from his former marriage would be $1,200/month. He argues that paying $1,200/month in addition to supporting his new family creates undue hardship and he should pay less. His former spouse disputes this.

Under FCSG s.10, what must Derek establish to succeed on an undue hardship claim, and what additional requirement must be satisfied?
opts: Derek must prove that paying the table amount would leave him with less than $500/month for personal expenses.

Under FCSG s.10, the court may award a different amount where the table amount would cause undue hardship to either spouse; Derek must: (1) establish one of the specified circumstances in s.10(2) (including unusually high debts or legal duties to support another person); and (2) show that his household standard of living is lower than the recipient's household standard of living.
Derek's new family obligations automatically establish undue hardship, requiring a reduction of child support.
Undue hardship is established solely by showing that the table amount exceeds 20% of the payor's income.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.10 provides that a court may award a different amount where the table amount would cause undue hardship to the paying or receiving spouse. Two cumulative elements must be established: (1) one of the circumstances listed in s.10(2), which includes unusually high debts from the marriage, unusually high expenses for exercising parenting time, and legal duties to support another person (such as children from a subsequent relationship); and (2) the household standard of living test — the court will not grant an undue hardship claim if the payor's household standard of living is higher than the recipient's. Derek's support obligations to his new family may qualify under s.10(2)(d) (legal duty to support other children), but he must also show his household standard of living is not higher than his former spouse's.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 10(1), s. 10(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Undue Hardship — FCSG s.10

text:
A private therapist is treating an adult patient who discloses that they are aware of ongoing physical abuse of a child next door — not a patient of the therapist. The therapist is uncertain whether the duty to report under the CYFSA applies because neither the child nor the perpetrator is a patient, and the information comes from a third party's disclosure.

Does the duty to report under CYFSA s.125 apply to the therapist in this scenario?
opts: The duty to report only applies where the professional has a direct professional relationship with the child at risk.

Under CYFSA s.125(1), the duty to report arises for every person who has reasonable grounds to suspect that a child is or may be in need of protection; the duty is not limited to persons with a professional relationship with the child; the therapist's reasonable grounds — based on what the patient disclosed — trigger the obligation to report forthwith to CAS.
The duty to report is limited to health care professionals treating the child directly; a therapist treating an adult has no obligation.
The therapist should assess whether the patient's disclosure is reliable before deciding whether to report; the duty only applies after independent verification of the concern.
expl: CYFSA s.125(1) imposes the mandatory reporting obligation on every person — not only on persons with a professional relationship with the child. The trigger is 'reasonable grounds to suspect' that a child is or may be in need of protection. The therapist has received information from a patient suggesting ongoing physical abuse of a child. If the therapist has reasonable grounds to suspect the child is in need of protection (which the patient's disclosure may provide), the duty to report applies regardless of the fact that the child is not a patient and the information came from a third party. The therapist cannot substitute personal judgment about reliability for the legal obligation to report to CAS.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 125(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Universal Duty to Report — CYFSA s.125(1)

text:
Nina and Oscar cohabited for 4 years in a conjugal relationship and separated in March 2022. Nina did not apply for spousal support at the time of separation. In April 2024, Nina consults a lawyer and asks about a spousal support claim under the FLA. Oscar argues the claim is statute-barred.

What is the limitation period for a common-law spousal support claim under the Family Law Act, and has it expired for Nina?
opts: There is no limitation period for spousal support claims under the FLA; claims may be brought at any time.

Under FLA Part III and the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim for spousal support by a common-law partner must be brought within two years of separation; Nina separated in March 2022 and her April 2024 consultation is approximately two years later — she is at or near the limitation period and must act immediately.
Common-law support claims are subject to the same limitation periods as equalization claims under FLA s.7(3).
Common-law partners have six years from separation to bring a support claim.
expl: Spousal support claims by common-law partners under the Family Law Act (Part III) are subject to the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period. For FLA s.29 support claims by common-law spouses, the limitation period runs from the date of separation — when the claimant knew or ought to have known they had a claim for support. Nina and Oscar separated in March 2022. As of April 2024, Nina is approximately two years post-separation. She is at the edge of the limitation period and must act immediately to preserve her claim. If any time beyond April 2024 passes before an application is issued, the claim may be statute-barred. The FLA s.7(3) specific limitation periods apply only to equalization of net family property, not to support claims.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Common-Law Spousal Support Limitation

text:
Gillian and Derek cohabited for 10 years and separated in January 2020. Gillian made significant contributions to Derek's home during the relationship. In February 2023, Gillian brings a claim for unjust enrichment and a constructive trust over Derek's home. Derek argues the claim is statute-barred under the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year limitation period.

Are common-law property claims based on unjust enrichment and constructive trust subject to the Limitations Act, 2002, and does the discovery rule apply?
opts: Common-law property claims based on unjust enrichment are not subject to any limitation period; they may be brought at any time.

Under the Limitations Act, 2002, unjust enrichment and constructive trust claims by common-law partners are subject to the two-year basic limitation period from the date the claim was 'discovered'; the claim is discovered when the claimant knew or ought to have known she had a claim, that the claim is appropriate to pursue, and the identity of the respondent; discovery typically occurs at separation when the unjust enrichment becomes apparent.
Common-law property claims are governed by the ten-year ultimate limitation period only; the two-year basic period does not apply.
Constructive trust claims have a six-year limitation period matching the FLA equalization limitation.
expl: Unjust enrichment and constructive trust claims by common-law partners are civil claims governed by the Limitations Act, 2002. The basic limitation period is two years from the date the claim was discovered. Discovery occurs when the claimant knew or ought to have known: (a) that the act or omission occurred; (b) that the claimant suffered a loss from it; (c) that the claim is appropriate to pursue; and (d) the identity of the respondent. For common-law property claims, discovery typically occurs at separation when the unjust enrichment crystallizes and the parties' contributions to the property become clear. Gillian and Derek separated in January 2020; her February 2023 claim is approximately three years post-separation — she must argue that the discovery of her claim was delayed beyond January 2020 for any reason to avoid limitation.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4, s. 15
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Common-Law Property Claims — Unjust Enrichment Limitation

text:
Terrence has accumulated $9,000 in child support arrears over 18 months. He is currently employed as a courier and holds a valid Ontario driver's licence. The FRO Director has served notice under FRSAEA s.34 that his driver's licence will be suspended unless he pays the arrears or makes arrangements. Terrence argues that suspending a courier driver's licence is disproportionate and requests a hardship exemption.

Under FRSAEA s.34, what is the process for driver's licence suspension for support arrears, and how may a payor challenge the suspension?
opts: The FRO must obtain a court order before suspending a driver's licence; the Director cannot act unilaterally.

Under FRSAEA s.34, the Director may give a payor notice of intention to have the Registrar suspend the payor's driver's licence; the payor then has 30 days to pay the arrears in full or make an arrangement satisfactory to the Director; if neither occurs, the Director may direct the Registrar to suspend the licence; the payor may apply to court for an order respecting the notice.
Driver's licence suspension is automatic the moment arrears accumulate; no notice is required.
Driver's licence suspension applies only where arrears exceed $20,000; Terrence's arrears of $9,000 are below the threshold.
expl: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.34 provides a two-step process: (1) the Director gives the payor written notice of the intention to have the driver's licence suspended; (2) the payor has 30 days from the notice to pay the arrears in full or make arrangements satisfactory to the Director; if the payor fails to do so, the Director may direct the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to suspend the licence. The payor may apply to court to challenge the notice or seek relief, but must do so within the notice period. There is no minimum arrears threshold in the statute for licence suspension — the Director has broad discretion. Terrence's hardship argument (employment as a courier) would be presented to the court on any such application.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 34
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Driver's Licence Suspension — FRSAEA s.34

text:
A 3-year-old child has been in CAS care for 21 consecutive months due to the parents' severe addiction issues. The parents have made limited progress. CAS is seeking Crown wardship. The parents argue that a society wardship should be extended instead of proceeding to Crown wardship.

What are the criteria and time limits governing Crown wardship under the CYFSA?
opts: Crown wardship may be ordered only where the parents have been convicted of a criminal offence involving the child.

Under CYFSA s.116(1)(d) and s.120, a court may make an order for Crown wardship where the child cannot be adequately protected through a supervision or society wardship order; CYFSA s.120 imposes cumulative time limits on CAS custody — generally no more than 12 months for children under 6 — after which the court must proceed to Crown wardship or return the child home.
Crown wardship is only available for children aged 12 or older who can consent to permanent ward status.
Crown wardship is available only upon parental consent; contested Crown wardship applications are not permitted.
expl: Under the CYFSA, Crown wardship (s.116(1)(d)) is ordered where the court determines the child cannot be adequately protected through less restrictive means. The CYFSA also imposes cumulative time limits on temporary CAS care: for children under 6 years of age, the cumulative time in CAS temporary care cannot generally exceed 12 months (s.120(3)); once the child has been in CAS care for the maximum permitted period without a plan for return, the court must proceed to Crown wardship or return the child to parental care. Given that this 3-year-old has been in care for 21 months — approaching or exceeding the permitted maximum — CAS may be legally required to seek Crown wardship rather than further temporary orders.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 116(1)(d), s. 120(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Crown Wardship — CYFSA s.116(1)(d) and Cumulative Time Limits

text:
A child support order was made eight years ago requiring the payor to pay $1,200/month. The payor fell behind on payments and has accumulated $72,000 in arrears. The recipient wants to enforce the arrears through court proceedings. The payor argues that enforcement of arrears is subject to the general two-year limitation period, which has expired for older arrears.

What limitation period applies to the enforcement of child support arrears in Ontario?
opts: The two-year general limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars enforcement of arrears that are more than two years old.

Under the Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 and the FRSAEA enforcement framework, there is no limitation period for proceedings to enforce a court order; once child support arrears accrue under a court order, they constitute a court-order debt that can be pursued at any time — neither the two-year basic period nor any other limitation period bars enforcement; the FRO may enforce arrears without a new court order.
There is no limitation period on enforcement of child support arrears under any circumstances.
Enforcement of arrears must commence within one year of each missed payment or the right to enforce that payment is lost.
expl: Child support arrears under a court order are a form of judgment debt. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 provides that there is no limitation period in respect of a proceeding to enforce a court order. This means that child support arrears arising under a court order can be enforced at any time — there is no two-year or ten-year bar on enforcement of a court order's arrears. The Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996 (FRSAEA) provides additional enforcement mechanisms through the FRO (Family Responsibility Office), including licence suspension and credit reporting, without any limitation bar on the arrears. The payor's argument that a two-year limitation applies is incorrect in the context of enforcing an existing court order's arrears.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Limitation Period — Child Support Arrears Enforcement

text:
A professional engineer moves from Ontario to British Columbia seeking employment. British Columbia requires all engineers practising in the province to hold a B.C. licence. The engineer argues that the provincial licensing requirement infringes her mobility right to pursue gainful employment anywhere in Canada under s. 6(2)(b) of the Charter. British Columbia argues that its licensing requirement is a reasonable professional standard, not a violation of mobility rights.

Under s. 6(2)(b) of the Charter, what is the scope of the right of Canadian citizens and permanent residents to pursue gainful employment in any province?
opts: Section 6(2)(b) gives every Canadian citizen and permanent resident an absolute right to work in any profession in any province without meeting any provincial requirements.

Section 6(2)(b) guarantees the right of Canadian citizens and permanent residents to pursue the gaining of a livelihood in any province; however, under s. 6(3)(a), provincial laws of general application that do not discriminate against persons on the basis of their previous province of residence are not affected by s. 6(2). A B.C. licensing requirement that applies equally to all persons — including B.C. residents — does not violate s. 6(2)(b).
Section 6(2)(b) applies only to interprovincial movement; once a person has moved to a province and taken up residence, their rights under s. 6(2)(b) are spent.
Section 6(2)(b) is subject to s. 33; provinces may use the notwithstanding clause to override mobility rights.
expl: Section 6(2)(b) of the Charter guarantees that every citizen of Canada and every person who has the status of a permanent resident of Canada has the right to pursue the gaining of a livelihood in any province. However, s. 6(3)(a) provides that ss. 6(2)(a) and (b) do not preclude any law or practice of general application in force in a province unless it is intended to, or has the effect of, discriminating against persons on the basis of their previous province of residence. A professional licensing requirement that applies equally to all persons seeking to practise engineering in B.C. — regardless of where they previously resided — does not violate s. 6(2)(b) because it does not discriminate on the basis of previous province of residence. Section 6 is not in the group of rights subject to s. 33.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 6(2)(b), s. 6(3)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 6 — Mobility Rights — Right to Move and Take Up Residence

text:
A construction company submits a bid on a provincial government contract. A competitor submits a FIPPA access request for all documents related to the successful bid, including the winning company's pricing breakdown, proprietary cost estimates, and project methodology. The government institution has notified the winning company of the request. The winning company opposes disclosure, arguing the information is commercial and was provided in confidence.

Under s. 17 of FIPPA, what test must be satisfied for a third party to successfully claim the third-party information exemption?
opts: Any information provided by a private party to a government institution is automatically exempt from disclosure; no further test is required.

Under FIPPA s. 17, the third-party information exemption applies where disclosure of the record could reasonably be expected to: (1) prejudice significantly the competitive position of the third party; (2) interfere with contractual or other negotiations; (3) result in similar information no longer being supplied to the institution; or the information was supplied in confidence and was treated as confidential by both parties, and disclosure could result in material financial loss to the third party.
Section 17 applies only to government records relating to pharmaceutical companies; it has no application to construction industry records.
Section 17 is a mandatory exemption that requires refusal of access to all records submitted by private parties to government institutions, without any further analysis.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 17(1), an institution shall refuse to disclose a record that reveals a trade secret or commercial, financial, labour relations, scientific, or technical information supplied in confidence to the institution by a third party, where the disclosure could reasonably be expected to: (a) prejudice significantly the competitive position or interfere with the contractual or other negotiations of the third party; (b) result in similar information no longer being supplied to the institution; or (c) result in undue loss or gain to any person, group, committee, or financial institution or agency. The third party must establish all elements: the information is commercial in nature, was supplied in confidence, and disclosure would cause the described harm.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 17(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA Section 17 — Third-Party Commercial Information

text:
A provincial law disqualifies persons who have been convicted of a criminal offence and are currently serving a sentence of imprisonment from voting in provincial elections. An imprisoned person challenges the law as a violation of his right to vote under s. 3 of the Charter. The government argues the law is justified under s. 33.

Can a provincial legislature use the s. 33 notwithstanding clause to override s. 3 democratic rights to disenfranchise prisoners?
opts: Yes. A provincial legislature may use s. 33 to override any Charter right, including s. 3 voting rights.

No. Section 3 democratic rights are expressly excluded from the scope of the s. 33 notwithstanding clause; a province cannot use s. 33 to override the right to vote. The disenfranchisement of prisoners must be justified under s. 1 of the Charter.
Yes. The right to vote under s. 3 is a political right, not a fundamental right, and is therefore subject to legislative override by the legislature of the province.
No, but the federal Parliament may use s. 33 to override s. 3 rights; only provinces are barred from doing so.
expl: Section 33 of the Charter permits Parliament or a provincial legislature to invoke the notwithstanding clause in respect of a provision of the Charter that is included in s. 2 or ss. 7 to 15. Section 3 (democratic rights — right to vote and right to stand for election) is not included in the list of rights that can be overridden by s. 33. As confirmed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Sauve v Canada (Chief Electoral Officer), 2002 SCC 68, the right to vote under s. 3 is fundamental to Canadian democracy and cannot be overridden using s. 33. Any restriction on prisoner voting rights must be justified under s. 1 of the Charter.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 3, s. 33; Sauve v Canada (Chief Electoral Officer), 2002 SCC 68
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 3 — Right to Vote and Right to Stand for Election

text:
The federal government enacts a fisheries regulation that limits the annual salmon catch of a First Nation whose members have exercised an Aboriginal fishing right for generations. The regulation prioritizes commercial fishing licences over the First Nation's food fishing right. The First Nation challenges the regulation as an unjustified infringement of their s. 35 Aboriginal right. The Crown argues the regulation is justified because conservation of salmon is a valid legislative objective.

Under the Sparrow justification framework, which of the following MOST accurately states the Crown's burden once an infringement of a s. 35 right has been established?
opts: Once an infringement is established, the Crown must prove the regulation promotes conservation; no additional priority requirement applies, and commercial fishing interests may be prioritized over Aboriginal food fishing.

Under R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, once infringement of a s. 35 right is established, the Crown bears the burden of justifying the infringement by showing: (1) the infringement is in furtherance of a valid legislative objective (e.g., conservation, resource management, protection of public safety — though objectives must be compelling and substantial); and (2) the infringement upholds the honour of the Crown, which includes the requirement that Aboriginal rights be given priority over other user groups. Prioritizing commercial fishing licences over Aboriginal food fishing rights would undermine the priority requirement and would not justify the infringement under Sparrow.

Under Sparrow, the Crown is not required to demonstrate any priority for Aboriginal rights; it need only show the regulation serves a valid public interest, and courts will defer to the Crown's resource management choices.

The Sparrow justification test mirrors the s. 1 Oakes test; the Crown must show the regulation is prescribed by law and proportional to the government's objective. Conservation is always a proportionate objective for fisheries regulations.
expl: R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, established a two-stage justification test for Crown infringement of s. 35 rights. Stage 1 asks whether the Crown has a valid legislative objective — the Court was clear that not all objectives will qualify; the objective must be compelling and substantial (conservation is a recognized valid objective, but mere economic convenience is not). Stage 2 requires the Crown to satisfy the honour of the Crown, which includes the critical requirement that Aboriginal rights be given priority over other user groups in resource allocation. This is the exam trap: many candidates know 'conservation' is a valid objective and assume it automatically justifies the infringement. But Sparrow's priority requirement means that even where conservation is the objective, Aboriginal food fishing must be given priority in the allocation of the conservation-limited resource over commercial fishing interests. A regulation that gives priority to commercial licences over Aboriginal food fishing fails at Stage 2 of Sparrow, regardless of the validity of the conservation objective.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations | Sparrow Justification — Crown Burden and Priority

text:
An Ontario regulatory tribunal conducts a hearing into alleged professional misconduct by a pharmacist. During the hearing, a compliance officer testifies in chief about a confidential internal report prepared by the regulator. The report contains serious factual allegations against the pharmacist that were not included in the original notice of hearing. The pharmacist's counsel is not given access to the report and is not given an opportunity to cross-examine the compliance officer on its contents before it is admitted as evidence. The tribunal relies on the report in issuing its decision to suspend the pharmacist.

Under the audi alteram partem rule (hear the other side), what procedural obligation arises when a tribunal relies on adverse evidence that was not previously disclosed to the affected party?
opts: No procedural obligation arises because the evidence was introduced through oral testimony; the pharmacist had an opportunity to hear the evidence at the hearing.

Under the audi alteram partem rule, a party affected by adverse evidence must have a meaningful opportunity to know the case against them and to respond; where a tribunal relies on significant adverse evidence — particularly evidence not previously disclosed and not subject to cross-examination — the party is entitled to access that evidence and to challenge it before the tribunal relies on it.
The audi alteram partem rule requires only that the hearing be held in public; confidentiality of internal reports is a complete answer to any disclosure obligation.
The audi alteram partem rule is satisfied by the mere holding of a hearing; no obligation to disclose specific documents arises.
expl: The audi alteram partem rule — hear the other side — is a core component of natural justice and procedural fairness. Under this rule, a party has the right to know the full case against them and to have a meaningful opportunity to respond to adverse evidence before an adverse decision is made. As recognized in Kane v Board of Governors of the University of British Columbia, [1980] 1 SCR 1105, and Baker v Canada, [1999] 2 SCR 817, a party must be provided with any significant adverse evidence on which the tribunal proposes to rely, and must be given a real opportunity to challenge and contradict that evidence. Relying on a confidential internal report without disclosure or cross-examination is a serious breach of the audi alteram partem rule.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Kane v Board of Governors of the University of British Columbia, [1980] 1 SCR 1105
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Audi Alteram Partem — Right to Respond to Adverse Evidence

text:
A federal law provides social assistance benefits that are set at a lower rate for persons with disabilities who can work part-time versus those who cannot work at all. A claimant with a disability who can work part-time receives lower benefits despite having significantly higher disability-related expenses. She argues the distinction perpetuates the historical disadvantage of persons with disabilities.

Under the substantive equality approach to s. 15 of the Charter, why is formal equality — treating similarly situated people the same — insufficient to avoid a s. 15 violation?
opts: Formal equality is sufficient; as long as the government applies the same rules to everyone in the same category, s. 15 is satisfied.

Under the substantive equality approach affirmed in Withler v Canada and Fraser v Canada, s. 15 demands that laws recognize and accommodate the actual circumstances, needs, and disadvantages of claimants — not just that they be treated identically to others. A law that treats different groups in a formally neutral way may nonetheless perpetuate disadvantage if it fails to account for material differences in circumstances.
Substantive equality means every person must receive the same dollar amount of benefits regardless of individual circumstances.
Substantive equality is satisfied as long as the law was enacted with good intentions toward disadvantaged groups.
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada adopted a substantive equality approach to s. 15 in Andrews v Law Society of BC, [1989] 1 SCR 143, and developed it through Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12, and Fraser v Canada (Attorney General), 2020 SCC 28. Substantive equality recognizes that treating unequal situations the same — formal equality — can itself be discriminatory where it fails to account for real differences in circumstances. The claimant's higher disability-related costs mean that lower benefit rates may impose a disproportionate burden on the group to which she belongs, potentially perpetuating the historical disadvantage of persons with disabilities and violating s. 15.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of BC, [1989] 1 SCR 143; Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12; Fraser v Canada (Attorney General), 2020 SCC 28
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 15 — Substantive Equality and Perpetuation of Disadvantage

text:
Police officers at the scene of a fatal shooting fail to cooperate with the Special Investigations Unit investigation as required by Ontario law. As a result, the SIU investigation is hampered, no charges are laid, and the family of the deceased cannot determine what happened. The family sues the Chief of Police and the provincial Crown, alleging misfeasance in public office. The Chief of Police argues misfeasance requires an intentional act directed specifically at the plaintiffs; mere non-cooperation is insufficient.

Under the misfeasance in public office tort as described in Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69, what must a plaintiff establish?
opts: Misfeasance in public office requires only negligence by a public official; a plaintiff need not prove intent to harm.

Under Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69, misfeasance in public office requires proof of two elements: (1) deliberate unlawful conduct in the exercise of public functions (either intentional illegal acts or exercise of public power with knowledge that the conduct is unlawful); and (2) the public official must have been aware that their conduct was unlawful and likely to harm the plaintiff. Deliberate non-compliance with a statutory obligation (such as cooperation with an SIU investigation) combined with knowledge that the failure would harm the family suffices; the act need not be specifically directed at the plaintiffs as individuals.
Misfeasance in public office is available only against Crown corporations; individual government employees and police officers are personally immune from this tort.
Misfeasance in public office requires proof that the public official personally profited from their unlawful conduct; absence of personal gain is a complete defence.
expl: In Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69, the Supreme Court confirmed the elements of misfeasance in public office in the context of police officers who failed to comply with their statutory obligation to cooperate with the SIU. The Court held the tort requires: (1) deliberate unlawful conduct in the exercise of public functions — this includes either an intentional illegal act (targeted malice) OR the exercise of public power with knowledge that the conduct is unlawful and will harm the plaintiff (unlawful conduct with advertent harm); and (2) awareness that the unlawful conduct was likely to harm the plaintiff. The tortfeasor need not have specifically targeted the plaintiff as an individual; it is sufficient that the official knew their deliberate failure to perform a statutory duty would likely cause harm to the plaintiff class (here, the family of the deceased). The Chief's narrower formulation is incorrect.
caseRef: Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Misfeasance in Public Office — Odhavji Estate Test

text:
The Tsilhqot'in Nation seeks a declaration of Aboriginal title over a large area of British Columbia land that was used by their ancestors in a semi-nomadic pattern — traversing the territory for hunting, fishing, and seasonal settlement. The Crown argues Aboriginal title can only be established over specific sites of intensive, regular, and exclusive use; vast tracts of land used in a mobile pattern cannot be the subject of Aboriginal title.

Under Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, what is the test for establishing Aboriginal title over territory, and was the Crown's narrow interpretation of the test correct?
opts: Aboriginal title can only be established over specific sites of intensive regular use; the Crown's narrow site-specific approach is correct and semi-nomadic use of vast tracts cannot ground Aboriginal title.

Under Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, Aboriginal title is established over territory (not merely specific sites) where the claimant can demonstrate: (1) sufficient occupation — whether intensive or through semi-nomadic patterns of use — prior to Crown sovereignty; (2) continuity of occupation between pre-sovereignty use and present-day rights; and (3) exclusive occupation, meaning the group treated the land as its own and excluded others. The Court rejected the Crown's site-specific approach: semi-nomadic, territorial use of vast tracts of land can establish title over the entire territory, not just the specific sites of intensive use. Once title is established, the Crown must justify any infringement under the Sparrow framework.
Aboriginal title in Canada is limited to lands specifically identified in treaties; lands not covered by treaties cannot be the subject of an Aboriginal title claim in court.

Tsilhqot'in Nation established that Aboriginal title gives First Nations absolute ownership of land that cannot be infringed by the Crown under any circumstances; the Sparrow justification test does not apply to Aboriginal title lands.
expl: Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, was a landmark decision granting the first judicial declaration of Aboriginal title over a vast territory in Canadian history. The Court held that the test for Aboriginal title requires: (1) Sufficient occupation — the land must have been used, controlled, or occupied by the claimant's ancestors prior to Crown sovereignty; this does not require intensive, permanent settlement at specific sites; semi-nomadic patterns of territorial use (hunting, fishing, traveling through) are sufficient if the group exercised regular and exclusive control over the land; (2) Continuity — a connection between historic occupation and present-day rights must be shown; and (3) Exclusivity — the group must have treated the land as its own and had the intention and capacity to exclude others. Once Aboriginal title is established, the Crown must justify any infringement under a modified Sparrow framework: the objective must be compelling and substantial, the infringement must be consistent with the Crown's fiduciary duty to the First Nation, and the First Nation must be compensated for interference with its title land.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations | Aboriginal Title — Tsilhqot'in Nation Test and Consequences

text:
An Ontario builder receives a written letter from a municipal planning officer confirming that his proposed development complies with all applicable zoning by-laws and that a building permit will be issued as a matter of course. Relying on this letter, the builder demolishes existing structures on the land and spends $2 million in preparatory work. The municipal council subsequently changes the zoning by-law, and the city refuses to issue the building permit. The builder argues the city is estopped from refusing the permit.

Under Canadian administrative law, can estoppel be raised against a public authority to prevent it from exercising a statutory power in the public interest?
opts: Yes. Where a claimant has detrimentally relied on an official's representation, the public authority is fully bound by that representation and cannot exercise its statutory power to the contrary.
Yes. A written representation from a government official always binds the government; the builder is entitled to the permit regardless of the subsequent by-law change.

Generally no. While private law estoppel principles exist, estoppel cannot be used to prevent a public authority from exercising a statutory power in the public interest; the courts have recognized that estoppel does not operate against the Crown or public bodies where its application would defeat the exercise of a statutory duty or power conferred in the public interest — though the claimant may have other remedies in negligence or public law.
The result depends on whether the planning officer was a senior official; representations from junior officials never give rise to estoppel against public authorities.
expl: Canadian administrative law has generally held that estoppel cannot operate against a public authority to prevent the exercise of a statutory power conferred in the public interest. The principle was recognized in Maritime Electric Co v General Dairies Ltd, [1937] AC 610 (PC), and applied in Canadian courts: a public body cannot be estopped from performing its statutory duties and exercising its statutory powers by the unauthorized representations of its officers. The reason is that allowing estoppel to bind a public authority would in effect allow an official to amend or override statutory law through unauthorized representations. The builder's remedy may lie in a claim for damages (in negligence or for misfeasance in public office) but not in compelling the city to issue the permit through estoppel.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Maritime Electric Co v General Dairies Ltd, [1937] AC 610 (PC)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Estoppel Against Public Authorities — Limits in Administrative Law

text:
Toronto police officers fatally shoot an individual. The family of the deceased discovers that several officers failed to comply with the mandatory post-shooting protocol required by Ontario Special Investigations Unit regulations — specifically, the officers refused to provide statements and did not submit to the SIU investigation as required. The estate of the deceased sues the police service and the officers' employer, alleging a duty of care was owed by the employer (the police services board) to ensure officers complied with post-incident investigative protocols.

Under Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, can a police services board owe a duty of care to the estate of a person killed by police officers, arising from the board's failure to ensure officers complied with post-shooting investigative obligations?
opts: No. Police services boards never owe a duty of care to private individuals; their duties run only to the general public and not to specific persons.
No. The duty of care analysis cannot apply to matters arising from police internal investigations; the SIU process is a public law matter immune from tort claims.

Yes. Under Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69, a police services board may owe a duty of care to identifiable persons affected by its failure to ensure officers comply with post-incident investigative obligations; where a board fails to ensure officers comply with mandatory protocols and that failure causes harm to identifiable persons, the Anns/Cooper test may be satisfied and liability may arise.
Yes, but only where the board had actual knowledge of the officers' non-compliance before the estate suffered harm; constructive knowledge is insufficient.
expl: In Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69, the Supreme Court of Canada held that a police services board may owe a duty of care to the estate of a person killed by police officers where the board failed to ensure the officers complied with mandatory post-incident investigative obligations (here, compliance with SIU requirements). The Court applied the Anns/Cooper two-stage test: at stage one, there was a sufficiently close and direct relationship (proximity) between the board and the estate, because the board had supervisory authority over the officers and the estate was an identifiable group directly affected by the post-incident process. The Court found that the policy/operational distinction did not bar the claim, as the board's failure to enforce compliance was an operational failure.
caseRef: Odhavji Estate v Woodhouse, 2003 SCC 69; Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27; Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Police Negligence — Odhavji Estate Principles

text:
The federal government wishes to regulate provincial universities but has no direct head of power to do so (education is exclusively provincial under s. 93). Parliament enacts the National Research Infrastructure Act, which imposes detailed requirements on how universities must manage their research programs, hire faculty, and account for all funding — including provincial funding — framed as conditions for receipt of federal research grants. In practice, virtually all universities must comply or face severe operational hardship.

Under the colourability doctrine, how do courts address federal legislation that appears to exercise one head of power but is actually aimed at legislating in a provincial field?
opts: Courts accept the face value of the legislation; if Parliament labels it as a spending measure, it is a spending measure regardless of its actual purpose or effect.

The colourability doctrine allows courts to look beyond the stated form or label of legislation to its true purpose and legal effect; where the pith and substance of legislation is in reality directed at a matter within provincial jurisdiction, it is unconstitutional even if artfully dressed in federal language.
The colourability doctrine does not apply to federal spending; Parliament may always impose any conditions it wishes on federal grants.
Courts cannot look behind the legislative text; only the literal wording determines constitutionality.
expl: The colourability doctrine, a complement to pith and substance analysis, holds that courts will look beyond the form or label of legislation to its true purpose and actual legal effect. In Attorney General of Canada v Attorney General of Ontario (Labour Conventions), [1937] AC 326, and subsequent cases, the principle was established that legislation cannot be sheltered from constitutional scrutiny by artful drafting. If the true pith and substance of the legislation is the regulation of a matter within exclusive provincial jurisdiction, the law is invalid as colourable legislation, regardless of the federal head of power invoked. In the scenario, if the Act effectively regulates all aspects of university operations and merely uses federal grants as the mechanism, a court may find it is colourably in the provincial field of education.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92, s. 93; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Pith and Substance — Colourability

text:
An accused is charged with a serious fraud offence. From the date the charge is laid to the conclusion of the trial in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice, 42 months elapse. During that period, a three-month delay is attributable to defence counsel's scheduling conflicts and a further two-month delay results from a change of defence counsel that the accused requested. The accused brings a s. 11(b) application for a stay of proceedings.

Under the Jordan framework for s. 11(b) analysis, what is the presumptive ceiling for proceedings in the Superior Court of Justice, and how is defence delay treated?
opts: There is no fixed ceiling; the court assesses all delays case by case and stays proceedings only if the delay is manifestly unreasonable.

Under R v Jordan, the presumptive ceiling for proceedings in the Superior Court of Justice is 30 months; delays clearly caused by the defence are subtracted from the total elapsed time in calculating whether the ceiling has been exceeded.
The presumptive ceiling for Superior Court proceedings is 18 months; any delay beyond 18 months automatically results in a stay of proceedings.
The Jordan framework does not apply to fraud charges; s. 11(b) only governs summary conviction proceedings.
expl: In R v Jordan, 2016 SCC 27, the Supreme Court of Canada established a new framework for s. 11(b). The presumptive ceiling for cases in the Superior Court of Justice (or cases tried after a preliminary inquiry) is 30 months from the date of charge to the completion of trial. Delays clearly caused by the defence — not by the Crown or the court — are subtracted from the total elapsed time when calculating whether the ceiling has been exceeded. Here, the five months of defence-caused delay (three months for defence scheduling, two months for the change of counsel requested by the accused) would be subtracted, leaving 37 months. This exceeds the 30-month ceiling, raising a presumption of unreasonable delay.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(b); R v Jordan, 2016 SCC 27
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 11(b) — Jordan Framework for Unreasonable Delay

text:
Police receive a tip that a person is growing cannabis at his home. Without obtaining a search warrant, they enter the property and look through the windows into the house. They smell cannabis and see equipment through a window. Using this observation, they obtain a warrant and conduct a formal search, finding a large grow operation. The accused argues that the initial warrantless entry onto his property to peer through windows violated s. 8 of the Charter.

Does a person's home attract the highest level of reasonable expectation of privacy under s. 8 of the Charter, and can police approach a private dwelling to observe through windows without engaging s. 8?
opts: No. Once police are on public streets, they may look through any window visible from the street without engaging s. 8.

Yes. Under Canadian constitutional law, a person's home is the location where the reasonable expectation of privacy is highest; police entry onto the curtilage of a home (the area immediately surrounding the dwelling) to conduct observations is a search engaging s. 8, even if no physical entry into the structure occurs.
No. Observation through a window is not a 'search' because the officer does not physically touch or enter the property.
Yes, but only if the windows have blinds or curtains drawn; windows left open to view from the street attract no privacy.
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada has consistently held that a person's home is the location of the highest reasonable expectation of privacy. The concept of 'curtilage' — the area immediately surrounding a dwelling — is also protected. In R v Evans, [1996] 1 SCR 8, the Court held that approaching a private home in a manner inconsistent with an implied licence (such as by going to the back of the house to peer through windows rather than approaching the front door) constitutes a trespass that engages s. 8. Police cannot circumvent the warrant requirement by conducting clandestine observations from the curtilage of a private home.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; R v Evans, [1996] 1 SCR 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 8 — Search of One's Home — Heightened Expectation of Privacy

text:
A party obtains a judicial review of a decision of an Ontario SPPA-governed tribunal. The tribunal prepares and files a certified record of proceedings. The party discovers that the certified record omits two documents: (1) an internal email exchange among tribunal staff that occurred before the hearing, discussing the likely outcome of the case; and (2) an expert report submitted by the opposing party at the hearing that the tribunal states it did not rely on. The party argues both documents should be included in the certified record.

What must be included in the certified tribunal record on judicial review, and must the record include documents the tribunal claims it did not rely on?
opts: The certified record need only include the tribunal's written decision and any statutory provisions cited in the decision; evidence and procedural documents are not part of the record.

The certified tribunal record must include all materials that were before the decision-maker at the time the decision was made, including documents submitted by the parties, evidence received at the hearing, and any other material considered by the tribunal — regardless of whether the tribunal claims it did not rely on a particular document. Internal emails among tribunal staff that relate to the proceeding and were created before the decision may also be includable, particularly if they suggest bias or improper considerations.
The certified record includes all documents submitted by the parties but excludes all internal tribunal communications, as these are subject to absolute privilege.
The certified record only includes documents the tribunal expressly identifies in its written reasons as having been relied upon; other documents in the file are not part of the record.
expl: On judicial review, the certified tribunal record should contain all materials that were before the decision-maker at the time the decision was made. This includes: all documents filed by the parties, evidence introduced at the hearing, the decision and reasons, notices, correspondence, and any other material before the tribunal. A tribunal cannot unilaterally exclude documents by claiming it did not rely on them; the reviewing court is entitled to assess for itself whether the decision is properly grounded in the record, and whether relevant material was improperly ignored. Internal emails among tribunal staff are more complex: they may be subject to deliberative privilege but can be ordered produced where they are relevant to a claim of bias or improper external influence. The party's argument regarding the expert report (which should be in the record regardless of claimed reliance) is well-founded; the argument regarding internal emails raises a deliberative privilege issue requiring more analysis.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22; Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 10
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Certified Tribunal Record — Contents and Completeness

text:
A prisoner successfully completes a prison sentence before their judicial review application challenging a disciplinary segregation order is heard. The disciplinary order has expired and the sentence has been served. The prisoner argues the court should still decide the case because: (1) the issue is capable of repetition yet evading review (segregation orders are short and frequently challenged after completion); (2) there is an adversarial context because the Crown still defends the order; and (3) the case raises a systemic constitutional issue affecting all federally sentenced persons.

Under the Borowski two-stage mootness framework, assuming the case is moot at stage one, which of the following MOST accurately describes the court's analysis at stage two?
opts: At stage two, if the case is capable of repetition yet evading review, the court must hear it; this factor alone is sufficient to require the court to exercise its discretion to decide the matter.

At Borowski stage two, the court exercises discretion by weighing three factors: (1) whether an adversarial context still exists (to sharpen the issues); (2) whether judicial economy favours deciding the issue (systemic importance, avoiding future litigation); and (3) whether deciding the case would be consistent with the court's adjudicative role (avoiding advisory opinions on hypothetical facts). No single factor is automatically determinative; the court must balance all three, and courts have declined to hear moot cases even where all three factors favour hearing.
At Borowski stage two, a systemic constitutional issue is automatically sufficient to require the court to decide the moot case; constitutional questions are never truly moot.
Stage two of Borowski requires the court to determine whether any person in Canada could conceivably face the same issue in the future; if so, the case must be decided.
expl: Under Borowski v Canada (AG), [1989] 1 SCR 342, if a case is found moot at stage one (no live controversy), the court exercises discretion at stage two by weighing three specific factors, none of which is automatically determinative: (1) Whether there remains an adversarial relationship — if the parties are still adversarial, the court has better arguments to work with; (2) Judicial economy — whether deciding the case avoids future litigation on a question of wide systemic importance; and (3) The court's adjudicative role — courts should be cautious not to venture into advisory opinion territory by deciding purely hypothetical issues. The exam trap here is assuming that any one factor (especially systemic importance or repetition-yet-evading-review) compels the court to hear the case. Courts have discretion and have declined even where systemic factors favoured hearing. All three factors must be balanced holistically.
caseRef: Borowski v Canada (Attorney General), [1989] 1 SCR 342
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Mootness — Discretion to Hear Despite Mootness

text:
Police have deliberately destroyed key exculpatory evidence, knowing the defence needed it. The accused has been denied access to the evidence and cannot mount a full answer and defence. The accused brings a Charter application seeking a stay of proceedings on the basis of police misconduct and a violation of the right to a fair trial. The Crown argues a stay is disproportionate because the charges are very serious.

Under what circumstances may a court grant a stay of proceedings as a remedy under s. 24(1) of the Charter?
opts: A stay is available whenever there is any Charter breach; the seriousness of the offence charged is irrelevant.

A stay of proceedings is the most drastic Charter remedy and is reserved for the clearest of cases — where the conduct of the state is so egregious that continuing the prosecution would bring the administration of justice into disrepute, or where no other remedy can cure the prejudice caused to the accused.
A stay is only available under s. 11(b) for unreasonable delay; it cannot be granted under s. 24(1) for police misconduct.
A stay of proceedings requires that the accused be found factually innocent before it can be granted.
expl: A stay of proceedings is the most drastic remedy available under s. 24(1) and is granted only in the clearest of cases. The test established in R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, and R v Babos, 2014 SCC 16, requires showing either: (1) the state conduct was so offensive to societal notions of fair play that proceeding would bring the administration of justice into disrepute (abuse of process), regardless of prejudice to the accused; or (2) the accused suffered actual prejudice — such as loss of the ability to make full answer and defence — that cannot be remedied by any other order. The seriousness of the offence is relevant to the balancing exercise but does not automatically preclude a stay where egregious state conduct is established.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(1); R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411; R v Babos, 2014 SCC 16
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 24(1) — Stay of Proceedings as a Charter Remedy

text:
A non-profit organization representing sex workers in Ontario seeks judicial review of a provincial regulation that criminalizes solicitation in public spaces. The organization has no members who are individually named applicants, and none of the sex workers it represents have personally been charged. The organization argues it has public interest standing to challenge the regulation. The Attorney General argues the organization lacks standing because no member has been directly affected.

Under the Downtown Eastside test for public interest standing, which of the following best states the test a court applies when deciding whether to grant public interest standing to a non-profit organization?
opts: Public interest standing is available only where at least one individual plaintiff can demonstrate direct personal harm from the challenged law; a non-profit alone cannot have standing.

Under Canada (Attorney General) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence, 2012 SCC 45, courts exercise discretion to grant public interest standing by considering three factors: (1) whether there is a serious justiciable issue; (2) whether the applicant has a real stake or genuine interest in the issue; and (3) whether the proposed application is a reasonable and effective means of bringing the issue before the court, having regard to other means available and the desirability of having the issue decided in a concrete factual context.

Public interest standing is available as of right where the organization demonstrates it is a recognized non-profit with a mandate that includes the subject matter of the challenge; no further test is required.
Courts grant public interest standing automatically to any organization seeking to challenge government action on behalf of a marginalized group, provided the organization has 1000 or more members.
expl: In Canada (AG) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence, 2012 SCC 45, the Supreme Court confirmed that public interest standing is a discretionary remedy governed by a three-part test: (1) Is there a serious justiciable issue raised? (2) Does the applicant have a real stake or genuine interest in the issue? (3) Is the proposed proceeding a reasonable and effective means of bringing the matter before the court, considering other means (e.g., individual litigants bringing personal challenges) and whether a concrete factual record is achievable? The test is holistic and flexible; the factors are not a strict checklist but are to be considered together. An organization with a genuine mandate to advocate for sex workers and a serious constitutional challenge to a solicitation law would likely satisfy all three factors, particularly where individual members face personal risks in bringing individual claims.
caseRef: Canada (Attorney General) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence, 2012 SCC 45
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Public Interest Standing — Downtown Eastside Test

text:
Police conduct an unlawful search of a vehicle without reasonable grounds, violating s. 8 of the Charter. They discover a kilogram of cocaine in the trunk. The accused applies to exclude the cocaine under s. 24(2) of the Charter, arguing its admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute. The Crown argues the evidence is reliable and should be admitted despite the breach.

Under s. 24(2) of the Charter and the Grant test, what three inquiries must a court conduct to determine whether to exclude evidence obtained in breach of the Charter?
opts: The court must determine whether the evidence is reliable, relevant, and not prejudicial; if all three are satisfied, the evidence is admitted.

The court must determine: (1) the seriousness of the Charter-infringing state conduct; (2) the impact of the breach on the accused's Charter-protected interests; and (3) society's interest in adjudication on the merits. Evidence must be excluded if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
Under s. 24(2), all evidence obtained through a Charter breach is automatically excluded; no balancing test applies.
Evidence obtained through a Charter breach is excluded only if the breach was deliberate and intentional; accidental or good-faith breaches always allow admission.
expl: In R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32, the Supreme Court of Canada replaced the older Collins/Stillman framework with the three-part Grant test for s. 24(2). The court must assess: (1) the seriousness of the Charter-infringing state conduct — deliberate, flagrant breaches weigh more heavily toward exclusion; (2) the impact of the breach on the accused's Charter-protected interests — more serious intrusions into reasonable expectations of privacy weigh toward exclusion; and (3) society's interest in adjudication on the merits — reliable physical evidence in serious cases weighs toward admission. The court balances all three and excludes the evidence only if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 24(2) — Grant Test for Exclusion of Evidence

text:
A low-income tenant facing eviction applies to the Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB) of Ontario. The tenant asks to be represented at the hearing by a friend who is a licensed paralegal. The LTB's registry clerk tells the tenant that the hearing will proceed without representation because 'the LTB does not allow non-lawyers to appear.' The tenant argues she has a right to representation by her licensed paralegal.

Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act and Ontario law, does a party before an Ontario tribunal have the right to be represented by a person who is a licensed paralegal but not a lawyer?
opts: No. Only lawyers licensed by the Law Society of Ontario may represent parties before Ontario administrative tribunals governed by the SPPA.

Yes. Under s. 10 of the SPPA, a party to a proceeding may be represented by a barrister and solicitor or a paralegal licensed by the Law Society of Ontario; licensed paralegals have the same right of representation as lawyers before SPPA-governed tribunals.
No. While lawyers may appear, paralegals and lay representatives have no statutory right to appear before Ontario administrative tribunals.
Yes, but only where the hearing involves a financial claim exceeding $10,000.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 10, a party to a proceeding before a tribunal governed by the SPPA may be represented at a hearing by a barrister and solicitor or a paralegal licensed by the Law Society of Ontario. Ontario's Law Society Act and SPPA together create a right for licensed paralegals to represent parties before SPPA-governed tribunals. Unlicensed persons do not have a statutory right to represent parties. The clerk's statement that 'no non-lawyers' may appear is incorrect as applied to licensed paralegals, who are authorized representatives under s. 10.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 10; Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Right to Representation — Counsel at Administrative Hearings

text:
A landowner applies to an Ontario municipal committee for a minor variance. The committee refuses without providing any reasons. The landowner wants to challenge the refusal in court. Her lawyer advises that under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, the traditional prerogative remedies of certiorari and mandamus have been absorbed into a simplified application for judicial review.

Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, how does the Act affect the traditional prerogative remedies of certiorari, mandamus, and prohibition?
opts: The JRPA abolishes prerogative remedies entirely; they are no longer available in Ontario.
