Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, the traditional prerogative remedies — certiorari (quashing decisions), mandamus (compelling performance of public duties), and prohibition (preventing excess of jurisdiction) — are preserved but are now sought through a single unified application for judicial review rather than separate prerogative writ proceedings. The court may grant any one or more of these remedies on a judicial review application.
Prerogative remedies are still obtained through separate writ proceedings in Ontario; the JRPA only applies to statutory appeals.
Under the JRPA, certiorari is the only available prerogative remedy; mandamus and prohibition were abolished and replaced with declaratory relief.
expl: The Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, consolidated and simplified judicial review in Ontario. Historically, certiorari (to quash a decision), mandamus (to compel performance of a public duty), prohibition (to prevent excess of jurisdiction), and other prerogative remedies required separate writ proceedings. The JRPA replaced these with a single application for judicial review, while preserving the underlying remedies. Under s. 2 of the JRPA, a court may, on an application for judicial review, grant any relief that could have been obtained on an application for an order in the nature of certiorari, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto, or on an action for a declaration or injunction. The landowner can file a single JRPA application seeking certiorari (to quash the refusal) or mandamus (to compel the committee to provide reasons and reconsider).
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Prerogative Remedies — JRPA Unified Application

text:
Parliament enacts a mandatory minimum sentence of five years for anyone convicted of firearms trafficking. An offender who sold a single firearm to one person — with no violence, no criminal history, and strong mitigating personal circumstances — is convicted and sentenced to the mandatory minimum. Her counsel argues the mandatory minimum is grossly disproportionate even if some traffickers deserve five years. The Crown argues the sentence is not disproportionate for the worst case scenario.

Under the s. 12 'reasonable hypothetical' approach established in R v Nur and R v Lloyd, must a mandatory minimum sentence be grossly disproportionate as applied to the actual accused before the court, or may it be challenged on the basis that it would be grossly disproportionate as applied to a reasonably foreseeable offender?
opts: The s. 12 challenge must be based on the actual accused before the court; a mandatory minimum cannot be challenged based on hypothetical situations.

A mandatory minimum may be challenged as grossly disproportionate as applied to a reasonably foreseeable offender who would be caught by the provision — not just the actual accused. If the mandatory minimum would constitute cruel and unusual punishment for such a reasonably foreseeable offender, the provision is unconstitutional.
A mandatory minimum is unconstitutional under s. 12 only if it is grossly disproportionate for every possible offender to whom it could apply.
Courts may never challenge mandatory minimums under s. 12; sentencing policy is exclusively within Parliament's domain.
expl: In R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15, and R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed the reasonable hypothetical approach to s. 12 challenges to mandatory minimums. The court first considers whether the sentence is grossly disproportionate for the actual offender. If not, it considers whether a reasonable hypothetical offender at the low end of the spectrum of conduct captured by the provision would receive a grossly disproportionate sentence. If so, the mandatory minimum violates s. 12. This approach recognizes that mandatory minimums operate across a range of offenders; the constitutional test is whether any reasonably foreseeable application would be grossly disproportionate, not whether it is disproportionate for every offender.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 12; R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15; R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 12 — Reasonable Hypothetical Approach for Mandatory Minimums

text:
Parliament enacts legislation banning all outdoor advertising by tobacco companies. The government establishes that the ban has a pressing and substantial objective (reducing tobacco use), is rationally connected to that goal, and represents the minimum impairment possible to achieve the objective. The tobacco companies argue the final step of the Oakes test — proportionality of effects — is not satisfied because the total prohibition is devastating to their business.

What does the 'proportionality of effects' component (Stage 2(c)) of the Oakes test require the government to demonstrate?
opts: Stage 2(c) requires the government to prove that the law will succeed in eliminating the targeted harm entirely.

Stage 2(c) requires a final overall balancing: the benefits of the limitation to the public must be proportionate to the severity of the rights violation; a law that achieves a significant public benefit may satisfy this stage even if the limitation on the right is substantial, provided the overall balance is proportionate.
Stage 2(c) is automatically satisfied once minimal impairment is established; there is no separate effects test.
Stage 2(c) requires the government to compensate all parties whose rights were limited by the legislation.
expl: Stage 2(c) of the Oakes test — proportionality of effects — requires the court to conduct a final overall balancing between the benefits of the limitation and the severity of its effects on the right infringed. Even where a law is minimally impairing (Stage 2(b)), the court must ask whether the benefits conferred by the law — measured by the importance of the objective achieved — outweigh the actual limitation imposed on the right. In RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1995] 3 SCR 199, the Supreme Court found that even a total advertising ban may satisfy Stage 2(c) if the benefits to public health outweigh the impact on freedom of expression of tobacco companies, which occupy a lower protected status given the harm of the product. The final balance is a proportionality assessment, not a mathematical calculation.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103; RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1995] 3 SCR 199
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 1 — Proportionality of Effects (Stage 2(c) of Oakes)

text:
Parliament enacts comprehensive securities fraud legislation under s. 91(27) (criminal law). One provision of the Act imposes civil liability on directors of publicly traded companies who made fraudulent misrepresentations, creating a cause of action for investors. A provincial government argues that civil liability and company directors' duties are matters of property and civil rights under s. 92(13), and that this single provision of the federal law is unconstitutional.

Under the ancillary powers doctrine, when may a single provision of otherwise valid federal legislation that encroaches on provincial jurisdiction be upheld as constitutionally valid?
opts: A provision that encroaches on provincial jurisdiction is always unconstitutional and must be severed; the remainder of the Act can then stand on its own.

Under the ancillary powers doctrine, a provision that encroaches on provincial jurisdiction may be upheld if it is sufficiently integrated into a valid federal scheme — that is, if there is a rational functional connection between the provision and the valid part of the scheme, and if the provision is necessary to carry the scheme into operation.
A provision encroaching on provincial jurisdiction is automatically valid if the remainder of the Act is valid; the ancillary doctrine does not require any additional analysis.
The ancillary powers doctrine does not apply to criminal law legislation; any provision of a criminal law Act that touches provincial jurisdiction is automatically severed.
expl: The ancillary powers doctrine allows an otherwise ultra vires provision to be upheld if it is sufficiently integrated into a valid legislative scheme and is necessary for the scheme to operate effectively. In General Motors of Canada Ltd v City National Leasing, [1989] 1 SCR 641, the Supreme Court adopted a proportionality approach: the more serious the intrusion into provincial jurisdiction, the stronger the functional connection to the valid scheme must be. For a minor intrusion, a rational functional connection suffices; for a more significant intrusion, the provision must be truly necessary for the scheme's effectiveness. The civil liability provision in the securities fraud Act may satisfy this test if it is integral to achieving the Act's anti-fraud criminal law purpose.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; General Motors of Canada Ltd v City National Leasing, [1989] 1 SCR 641
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Ancillary Powers — Incidental Encroachment on Other Jurisdiction

text:
Ontario enacts environmental legislation that expressly incorporates federal toxic substance standards by reference: the provincial Act states that any substance designated as toxic under the federal Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999, is automatically subject to the provincial regulatory scheme. A regulated company argues that this referential incorporation is constitutionally invalid because it delegates provincial legislative authority to Parliament.

Under the doctrine of co-operative federalism, is referential incorporation of federal standards into provincial legislation constitutionally permissible?
opts: No. A province cannot incorporate federal standards by reference into provincial legislation; doing so constitutes an impermissible delegation of provincial legislative authority to Parliament.

Yes. The doctrine of co-operative federalism recognizes that federal and provincial legislative regimes may be designed to work together; a province may validly incorporate federal standards by reference into provincial legislation without this constituting an unconstitutional delegation, because the provincial legislature retains the authority to repeal or amend the incorporating provision at any time.
Yes, but only where the federal government consents to the incorporation in writing.
No. Referential incorporation is only permitted from provincial to provincial legislation; cross-jurisdictional incorporation is unconstitutional.
expl: Co-operative federalism is a constitutional doctrine that supports the validity of interlocking federal-provincial regulatory regimes. In OPSEU v Ontario (AG), [1987] 2 SCR 2, and subsequent cases, the Supreme Court confirmed that legislatures may incorporate standards from other jurisdictions by reference. Referential incorporation does not constitute a delegation of legislative power because the incorporating legislature retains authority to amend or repeal the incorporating provision; it is an exercise, not a surrender, of legislative authority. Interlocking regimes where provincial law incorporates federal standards are a recognized and encouraged feature of Canadian co-operative federalism.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; OPSEU v Ontario (AG), [1987] 2 SCR 2; Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999, S.C. 1999, c. 33
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Co-operative Federalism — Referential Incorporation and Interlocking Regimes

text:
Facing a sudden and severe national shortage of essential pharmaceutical drugs caused by a coordinated cyberattack on supply chains, the federal Cabinet invokes emergency legislation to commandeer provincial hospital pharmacies and centralize drug distribution. Several provinces argue that pharmaceutical regulation and hospital administration fall under provincial jurisdiction under ss. 92(7) and 92(13) of the Constitution Act, 1867, and that the federal law is constitutionally invalid.

Under the emergency branch of the POGG power, what conditions must Parliament satisfy to justify legislation that intrudes significantly into provincial jurisdiction?
opts: The emergency branch of POGG permits Parliament to permanently regulate any matter it declares to be a national emergency, regardless of whether the emergency conditions persist.

Under the emergency branch, Parliament may temporarily override provincial jurisdiction in response to a genuine national emergency — war, famine, epidemic, or comparable crisis — where the emergency is real, temporary, and of such pressing magnitude that it justifies federal intrusion; the emergency power lapses when the emergency ends.
The emergency branch of POGG applies only in time of war declared by Parliament; peacetime economic or public health crises cannot engage the emergency branch.

The emergency branch requires prior provincial consent; Parliament cannot override provincial jurisdiction under the emergency branch without the agreement of at least seven provinces representing fifty percent of the population.
expl: Under the Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91 (POGG preamble), and as established in Reference re Anti-Inflation Act, [1976] 2 SCR 373, and Reference re Wartime Leasehold Regulations, [1950] SCR 124, the emergency branch of POGG authorizes Parliament to enact legislation in response to a genuine national emergency. The emergency must be real and of sufficient gravity to warrant temporary federal intrusion into matters otherwise within provincial jurisdiction. Crucially, the emergency power is temporary — legislation justified under the emergency branch must lapse or be repealed when the emergency conditions no longer persist. The branch is not limited to wartime; it extends to any genuine national crisis of sufficient magnitude.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91 (POGG preamble); Reference re Anti-Inflation Act, [1976] 2 SCR 373; Reference re Wartime Leasehold Regulations, [1950] SCR 124

text:
During an arrest, provincial police officers use excessive force causing serious physical injury to a suspect who poses no threat. A court later finds the force used violated the suspect's s. 7 (life, liberty, and security) and potentially s. 12 (cruel and unusual treatment) rights under the Charter. The suspect seeks Charter damages from the provincial Crown under s. 24(1). The Crown argues the officers were acting in good faith.

Under the Ward framework, does the Crown's good faith argument negate a s. 24(1) Charter damages award for excessive force by police officers?
opts: Good faith by individual officers is a complete defence to Charter damages; no award can be made if the officers genuinely believed they were acting lawfully.

Under Ward v Vancouver (City), 2010 SCC 27, good faith is a relevant factor in the Charter damages analysis under the countervailing considerations branch, but it is not a complete defence. Good faith may reduce the quantum of damages but does not automatically eliminate the award, particularly where the breach is serious, the plaintiff suffered significant physical harm, and the deterrence function supports sending a message that excessive force is unacceptable regardless of the officer's subjective belief. The Crown remains vicariously liable for the Charter-infringing acts of its officers.
Good faith is irrelevant to Charter damages; once a Charter breach by the state is proven, damages must be awarded at the maximum rate regardless of any mitigation factors.
The Crown is immune from Charter damages claims arising from police use of force; the Crown Liability and Proceedings Act does not permit Charter damages in such circumstances.
expl: Under the Ward framework, good faith by officers is addressed under the countervailing considerations branch — the third step of the analysis. Good faith may be a relevant factor going to the quantum of damages and to whether an award is necessary for deterrence, but it is not a complete defence. The Ward Court noted that where a breach is serious, causes significant harm, and reflects conduct that requires deterrence (such as excessive force), a damages award may be appropriate even where officers acted in good faith. The Crown cannot use good faith as a blanket shield against all Charter damages; this would undermine the vindicatory and deterrent functions of s. 24(1). The Crown is vicariously liable under the Crown Liability and Proceedings Act and provincial equivalent legislation for the acts of its officers committed in the course of their duties.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, ss. 7, 12, 24(1); Ward v Vancouver (City), 2010 SCC 27; Crown Liability and Proceedings Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-50
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Charter Damages for Police Excessive Force — s. 7 and s. 12

text:
An Ontario professional college's Discipline Committee finds a member guilty of professional misconduct and orders the member struck off the rolls. The enabling statute provides: 'Any party may appeal a decision of the Discipline Committee to the Divisional Court on a question of law.' The member appeals, arguing the Committee misinterpreted a key term in its enabling statute. The College argues reasonableness applies because the Committee is an expert tribunal interpreting its home statute.

Under the Vavilov framework, what standard of review applies to the Committee's interpretation of its enabling statute when the matter reaches the Divisional Court on a statutory appeal?
opts: Reasonableness applies because the Discipline Committee is a specialized expert tribunal interpreting its home statute, and Vavilov preserves deference in such circumstances.

The standard of review is determined by the ordinary appellate standards: correctness for questions of law, and palpable and overriding error for questions of fact and mixed fact and law. Where a legislature has enacted a statutory right of appeal, the Vavilov framework directs courts to apply these appellate standards rather than the reasonableness standard, regardless of the tribunal's expertise.

On a statutory appeal limited to questions of law, courts apply a hybrid standard: reasonableness for interpretations of the tribunal's home statute but correctness for interpretations of external statutes.
Under Vavilov, the existence of a statutory appeal route is irrelevant to the standard of review; reasonableness is always the default for administrative decisions.
expl: Vavilov (2019 SCC 65) established that where a legislature has enacted a statutory right of appeal from a tribunal to a court, the ordinary appellate standards apply — correctness for questions of law, palpable and overriding error for questions of fact and mixed fact/law — rather than reasonableness. This is a categorical exception to the reasonableness default, premised on the constitutional role of courts and the legislature's deliberate choice to establish an appellate pathway. The tribunal's expertise is no longer a free-floating factor that overrides the legislature's choice to provide for court oversight. On a statutory appeal, the Divisional Court applies correctness to the legal question of statutory interpretation, even if the Committee is interpreting its home statute.
caseRef: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65; Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Correctness Review — Statutory Appeal Routes

text:
A wildfire-fighting organization requires all firefighters to pass an aerobic capacity test demonstrating the ability to carry a 45-kilogram pack and run 2.5 kilometres within a specific time. A female applicant fails the test and is denied employment. She argues the standard discriminates on the basis of sex because fewer women can pass it. The employer argues the standard is a bona fide occupational requirement necessary to fight wildfires safely.

Under the Meiorin test, what must an employer demonstrate to establish that a standard constituting prima facie discrimination is a bona fide occupational requirement?
opts: The employer must show the standard was designed in good faith; the discriminatory effect on a protected group does not need to be justified.
The employer must show the standard is rationally connected to the job — that it tests for skills actually needed. No further justification is required.

Under BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the employer must establish on a balance of probabilities: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job; (2) the standard was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that it was necessary to fulfill the legitimate work-related purpose; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish the legitimate work-related purpose — meaning the employer cannot accommodate the affected person without incurring undue hardship.
An employer establishes a BFOR by demonstrating that the majority of employees in the same position can meet the standard; if most people pass, it is a valid requirement.
expl: In BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3, the Supreme Court of Canada established the unified three-part BFOR test. To establish a bona fide occupational requirement, the employer must prove on a balance of probabilities that: (1) the standard was adopted for a purpose rationally connected to the performance of the job — not the discriminatory purpose itself; (2) it was adopted in an honest and good faith belief that the purpose was legitimately necessary; and (3) the standard is reasonably necessary to accomplish the legitimate work-related purpose — that it is impossible to accommodate persons who fail the standard without incurring undue hardship. Critically, the employer must also show it made efforts to craft a standard that accommodates difference, including sex-based physiological differences, to the point of undue hardship.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17(1); BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | BFOR — Bona Fide Occupational Requirement and the Meiorin Test

text:
A respondent before an Ontario professional regulatory tribunal argues that the hearing involves deeply personal medical evidence and requests that the public and media be excluded from the entire hearing. The tribunal chair states that hearings before the tribunal are open to the public as a matter of principle and that no exclusion will be granted.

Under s. 9 of the SPPA, are hearings before Ontario administrative tribunals required to be open to the public, and does the tribunal have any discretion to exclude the public or members of the press?
opts: All hearings before Ontario tribunals governed by the SPPA are private; there is no public access unless the tribunal orders otherwise.

Under s. 9(1) of the SPPA, hearings before tribunals are open to the public; however, under s. 9(1)(a) and (b), the tribunal has discretion to exclude the public or portions of the public where it considers that the desirability of avoiding disclosure of intimate financial or personal matters or other matters outweighs the desirability of open hearings in the public interest.
Hearings before Ontario tribunals are always fully public and there is no exception; the tribunal has no discretion to exclude the public.
The tribunal may exclude the public from hearings only where the enabling statute of the tribunal expressly grants that power; the SPPA alone does not authorize exclusion.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 9(1), hearings before Ontario tribunals are open to the public. However, s. 9(1)(a) provides that a tribunal may hold a closed hearing or exclude members of the public or the press where it considers that the desirability of avoiding disclosure of intimate financial or personal matters, or other matters, outweighs the desirability of having the hearing open to the public. Section 9(1)(b) provides a further ground related to prevention of prejudice to parties. The tribunal chair's absolute statement that no exclusion will be granted fails to exercise the discretion granted by s. 9(1); the chair must genuinely weigh the competing interests before refusing exclusion.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 9(1)(a), s. 9(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Open Hearings — SPPA Section 9 and Exclusion of the Public

text:
A provincial regulatory authority is responsible for inspecting nursing homes for safety compliance under provincial legislation. The authority negligently fails to inspect a nursing home that was known to have recurring safety problems. A nursing home resident suffers serious injuries when a safety hazard — identified in a complaint filed with the authority two months earlier — causes an accident. The resident's family sues the provincial Crown in negligence.

Under the Anns/Cooper framework for Crown negligence liability, what must be established to find a duty of care, and how does the policy/operational distinction affect the analysis?
opts: Government regulatory authorities are completely immune from negligence liability for failure to exercise their inspection powers; the Crown's statutory discretion to inspect is never justiciable.

Under the Anns/Cooper framework (Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79; Anns v Merton LBC [1978] AC 728), establishing a duty of care requires: (1) a prima facie duty of care based on reasonable foreseeability and proximity (Stage 1); and (2) no policy reasons to negate the duty (Stage 2). The policy/operational distinction is critical: where the authority exercised a core policy choice (e.g., how to allocate inspection resources), the Crown may be immune; but where the authority was engaged in operational decisions (implementing inspection procedures for known hazards), negligence liability may arise. A failure to act on a specific complaint about an identified hazard is more operational than policy and is more likely to ground a duty of care.
Crown negligence liability for statutory inspection functions arises automatically where a complaint was filed and not acted upon within 30 days; no further proximity or policy analysis is required.

Under Anns/Cooper, the Crown is only liable if it exercised its statutory inspection power and did so negligently; a complete failure to exercise the power (non-feasance) can never ground negligence liability against the Crown.
expl: The Anns/Cooper framework (Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79) applies to Crown negligence liability for statutory functions. Stage 1 asks whether there is a prima facie duty of care based on: (a) reasonable foreseeability that carelessness would harm persons in the plaintiff's position; and (b) sufficient proximity between the government actor and the plaintiff. Stage 2 asks whether there are policy reasons to negate the duty, including whether imposing liability would conflict with other statutory obligations or impose indeterminate liability. The policy/operational distinction remains important: core policy choices (how to allocate scarce inspection resources, which sectors to prioritize) attract immunity because they involve value judgments beyond judicial supervision. However, operational decisions (how to implement a policy once made, how to respond to a specific identified hazard) are fully justiciable. Here, the authority's failure to act on a specific complaint about an identified hazard is likely operational, bringing it within the zone where a duty of care can exist.
caseRef: Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79; Crown Liability and Proceedings Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-50; Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Negligence Liability for Statutory Duties — Anns/Cooper Test

text:
An Ontario immigration tribunal denies a work permit application. In its written reasons, the tribunal correctly states the legal test but then ignores two pieces of highly relevant evidence — a letter from the employer and a medical report — without any explanation. The tribunal's conclusion appears inconsistent with its own factual findings. On judicial review, the applicant argues the decision is unreasonable under Vavilov.

Under the Vavilov reasonableness framework, which of the following best describes what a reviewing court must find in order to conclude a decision is unreasonable?
opts: The decision is unreasonable only if the tribunal applied the wrong legal test; factual errors alone are insufficient for a finding of unreasonableness.

Under Vavilov, a decision is unreasonable if the reviewing court, after reading the reasons in light of the record, concludes that the decision lacks the hallmarks of reasonableness — justification, transparency, and intelligibility — or where the decision is not justified in relation to the relevant facts and law. Ignoring highly relevant evidence without explanation and reaching a conclusion inconsistent with one's own findings are paradigm cases of unreasonable decisions.
Under Vavilov, reasonableness review requires the court to ask whether the decision is correct; if the court would have decided differently, the decision is unreasonable.
A decision can be found unreasonable only if the tribunal fails to issue written reasons; where reasons are given, courts must defer regardless of their quality.
expl: Under Vavilov (2019 SCC 65), reasonableness review focuses on whether the decision has the hallmarks of justification, transparency, and intelligibility, and whether it is justified in relation to the facts and law constraining the decision-maker. The Court identified several categories of unreasonable decisions, including: (a) decisions that fail to account for the evidence on the record; (b) decisions where the reasoning fails to grapple with key issues or relevant evidence; (c) decisions internally inconsistent (reaching conclusions inconsistent with one's own factual findings); and (d) decisions that ignore or mischaracterize relevant evidence without explanation. Ignoring the employer letter and medical report without explanation, combined with internal inconsistency, squarely falls within Vavilov's description of an unreasonable decision. Reasonableness is not a correctness review in disguise.
caseRef: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Reasonableness Review — What Makes a Decision Unreasonable

text:
A member of an Ontario professional discipline tribunal was, ten years ago, a senior partner at the same law firm where the respondent member is currently a partner. The tribunal member had no direct professional relationship with the respondent. At the outset of the hearing, a party raises a concern about bias. The tribunal member says he harbours no actual bias and states he can decide the case fairly. He declines to recuse himself.

Under the test for reasonable apprehension of bias established in Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, must the tribunal member recuse himself, and does his assertion of subjective impartiality resolve the issue?
opts: The test for bias is subjective; because the tribunal member sincerely believes he is impartial, there is no bias and he need not recuse himself.

Under Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, the test for reasonable apprehension of bias is objective: whether an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically, and having thought it through, would conclude that it is more likely than not that the decision-maker, whether consciously or unconsciously, would not decide fairly. A tribunal member's assertion of subjective impartiality is irrelevant to this objective test. A former partnership at the same firm as the respondent, even 10 years ago, may or may not meet this threshold, depending on the nature and recency of the connection, but the assessment is conducted objectively.
Under the SPPA, a tribunal member must automatically recuse himself from any proceeding where they have ever worked at the same firm as any party; there is no need for a case-by-case analysis.
Bias is only relevant if the tribunal member had a direct financial interest in the outcome of the proceeding; a prior professional connection without financial interest cannot amount to bias.
expl: The leading Canadian authority on the test for reasonable apprehension of bias is Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, where de Grandpré J. articulated the objective test: the question is what an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically, having thought the matter through, would conclude. The test is objective — whether there is an appearance of bias that would undermine public confidence in the administration of justice — not whether the decision-maker actually harbours bias. A tribunal member's sincere assertion of impartiality is therefore entirely irrelevant to the legal analysis; the question is what an outside observer would reasonably conclude. A former partnership at the same firm as the respondent, even 10 years ago, is a fact to be weighed in the objective analysis: courts consider the nature of the connection, the recency, and whether any ongoing relationship exists.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22; Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Reasonable Apprehension of Bias — Test and Application

text:
Parliament enacts regulations under the Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999, designating greenhouse gases as toxic substances and imposing national carbon pricing. Several provinces challenge the legislation, arguing that environmental regulation of greenhouse gases is a matter of provincial property and civil rights (s. 92(13)) and local works and undertakings (s. 92(10)(a)). Parliament relies on s. 91(27) (criminal law) and POGG (national concern).

Which of the following best describes the constitutional framework governing the division of environmental regulatory jurisdiction between Parliament and the provinces?
opts: The environment is an enumerated federal head of power in s. 91; provinces have no jurisdiction over any environmental matter.
The environment is an enumerated provincial head of power in s. 92; Parliament has no jurisdiction over any environmental matter.

The environment is not a discrete head of power assigned exclusively to either level; both Parliament and the provinces may regulate environmental matters under their respective heads of power — Parliament under ss. 91(12) (fisheries), 91(27) (criminal law), and POGG (national concern or emergency); provinces under ss. 92(13) (property and civil rights) and 92(16) (local matters). Overlap is managed through the double aspect doctrine and paramountcy.
All environmental regulation requires the joint approval of both Parliament and a majority of provincial legislatures before it can take effect.
expl: The environment is not a discrete enumerated head of power in the Constitution Act, 1867; it falls under multiple overlapping federal and provincial heads. Parliament may regulate environmental matters under s. 91(12) (sea coast and inland fisheries), s. 91(27) (criminal law — Canadian Environmental Protection Act is a recognized exercise of the criminal law power to protect public health and the environment), and the national concern branch of POGG for matters like greenhouse gas emissions that are indivisible and nationally significant (as confirmed by the Supreme Court in References re Greenhouse Gas Pollution Pricing Act, 2021 SCC 11). Provinces may regulate under ss. 92(13) and 92(16). The double aspect doctrine allows both levels to operate in the same space; paramountcy resolves conflicts.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, ss. 91(12), 91(27), 92(13), 92(16); Canadian Environmental Protection Act, 1999, S.C. 1999, c. 33; References re Greenhouse Gas Pollution Pricing Act, 2021 SCC 11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Environmental Regulation — Federal and Provincial Heads of Power

text:
A regulatory tribunal investigating a professional's conduct obtains documents from the professional's office through an administrative inspection conducted without proper authorization, violating the professional's s. 8 Charter rights. The professional applies to exclude the documents from the regulatory hearing under s. 24(2) of the Charter, arguing the Grant test requires exclusion.

Does s. 24(2) of the Charter and the Grant test apply to the exclusion of evidence in civil or administrative regulatory proceedings?
opts: Yes. Section 24(2) and the Grant test apply equally to all proceedings — criminal, civil, and administrative — wherever a Charter breach occurs.

Section 24(2) by its terms applies only where evidence is sought to be used in proceedings against the accused in whose respect the breach occurred; courts have held that the Grant test is designed for criminal proceedings and that a different — typically broader — framework applies to exclusion of evidence in civil and administrative proceedings, where the focus is on the fairness of the proceeding rather than the Grant three-part balancing.
Section 24(2) never applies in administrative proceedings; all evidence obtained through any means is admissible before regulatory tribunals.
Section 24(2) applies in administrative proceedings only if the tribunal's enabling statute expressly incorporates it.
expl: Section 24(2) of the Charter provides that where evidence is obtained in a manner that infringed or denied any rights or freedoms guaranteed by the Charter, it may be excluded 'if it is established that, having regard to all the circumstances, the admission of it in the proceedings would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.' While s. 24(2) technically applies to any 'proceedings,' the Grant test was developed in the criminal context. Courts have held that the Grant framework is specifically calibrated for criminal proceedings; in civil and administrative contexts, evidence exclusion is governed by broader fairness principles. Tribunals have some discretion to exclude improperly obtained evidence on fairness grounds, but the Grant three-part balancing test does not directly transplant into regulatory proceedings.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 24(2) — Application in Non-Criminal Proceedings

text:
A university committee decides to expel a student for academic misconduct. The student seeks judicial review arguing she was not given notice of the specific allegations against her before the hearing, violating procedural fairness. She asks the court to quash the expulsion decision. The university argues certiorari should not issue because the internal appeal process has not been exhausted.

What is the function of certiorari in administrative law, and is the university's argument about exhaustion of internal appeals a valid objection?
opts: Certiorari quashes a decision but only applies where the decision-maker acted beyond its statutory authority; procedural unfairness does not ground certiorari.

Certiorari is the prerogative remedy that quashes (sets aside) a decision made by a tribunal or administrative body. It is available on grounds including: excess of jurisdiction, breach of procedural fairness/natural justice, and errors of law on the face of the record. The university's argument raises the adequate alternative remedy doctrine: courts generally decline to grant certiorari where an adequate internal appeal exists that has not been exhausted, unless special circumstances apply (e.g., the appeal body cannot provide the full remedy sought).
Certiorari is available as of right once any procedural unfairness is established; courts have no discretion to decline to issue certiorari and the exhaustion of internal appeals is irrelevant.
Certiorari can only be sought after all internal appeal processes have been completed; the student's application is premature and must be dismissed.
expl: Certiorari is the prerogative remedy (sought through a JRPA application in Ontario) that quashes or sets aside a decision of an administrative body. It lies on grounds including excess of jurisdiction, breach of natural justice/procedural fairness, and errors of law on the face of the record. The university's argument is a valid objection based on the adequate alternative remedy doctrine: courts have discretion to decline to grant certiorari (or any other form of judicial review) where an adequate internal appeal mechanism exists and has not been exhausted. However, the doctrine is one of discretion, not an absolute jurisdictional bar; courts may grant certiorari despite an available internal appeal where: the appeal body cannot provide the full remedy (e.g., it cannot address procedural fairness retroactively), where there are delays, or where special circumstances justify intervention. The student can argue these exceptions.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Certiorari — Quashing Administrative Decisions
